topics.title: Concept of Heat Transfer by Convection, Concept of Boundary Layers - Natural and Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Heat Transfer by Convection, Concept of Boundary Layers - Natural and Forced Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Convection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convective Heat Transfer** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) when they are at different temperatures. It is a **combined mechanism** involving **conduction** (through the fluid particles in contact with the surface) and **advection** (bulk motion/transport of fluid particles carrying thermal energy).

Mathematically, the instantaneous rate of convective heat transfer is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where:
- $q''$ = local heat flux $(W/m^2)$
- $h$ = local convective heat transfer coefficient $(W/m^2 \cdot K)$
- $T_s$ = surface temperature $(K)$
- $T_\infty$ = free-stream fluid temperature $(K)$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle and the Wind"**
> 
> Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a stove. The hot water at the bottom rises because it becomes less dense (this is **Natural Convection** — buoyancy does the work). Now switch on a fan pointed at the kettle. The fan forcefully pushes air across the surface, carrying heat away much faster (this is **Forced Convection** — an external agency does the work).
> 
> In both cases, the rule is the same: **heat is carried away by the movement of fluid particles**. The difference is *what causes* the movement — buoyancy (natural) or an external pump/fan/blower (forced).

### 1.3 Two Sub-Categories of Convection

| Type | Driving Force | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Forced Convection** | External agency (pump, fan, blower, wind) | Heat exchangers, aircraft wing cooling, car radiator, HVAC ducts |
| **Natural (Free) Convection** | Buoyancy due to density difference in a gravitational field | Refrigerator coils, cooling of transformers, hot water tanks, atmospheric winds |

### 1.4 The Boundary Layer Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Layer (Prandtl, 1904)** — A thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid surface, formed whenever a fluid flows over a surface, in which the **velocity** (and/or **temperature**) gradients are significant. Outside this layer, the fluid behaves essentially as an inviscid, uniform free stream.

Two types of boundary layers exist when a fluid flows over a heated surface:
1. **Hydrodynamic (Velocity) Boundary Layer** — defined by velocity variation from zero at the wall to $u_\infty$ in the free stream.
2. **Thermal Boundary Layer** — defined by temperature variation from $T_s$ at the wall to $T_\infty$ in the free stream.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer — Definition

The **hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness** $\delta$ is formally defined as the distance from the solid surface (measured normal to it) at which the fluid velocity reaches **99%** of the free-stream velocity $u_\infty$.

$$u(y = \delta) = 0.99 \, u_\infty$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance:** Inside the boundary layer, the fluid experiences a strong velocity gradient — viscous (shear) effects dominate, and no-slip condition holds ($u = 0$ at wall). Outside, the fluid essentially "doesn't know" the wall exists.

### 2.2 Thermal Boundary Layer — Definition

The **thermal boundary layer thickness** $\delta_t$ is the distance from the surface at which the temperature difference $(T - T_\infty)$ reaches **99%** of the maximum temperature difference $(T_s - T_\infty)$.

$$\frac{T(y = \delta_t) - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty} = 0.99$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Boundary Layer Formation

1. **Fluid approaches the surface** with uniform velocity $u_\infty$ and temperature $T_\infty$.
2. **No-slip condition** forces fluid velocity to zero at the wall ($u = 0$ at $y = 0$).
3. **Continuity** demands the fluid above must speed up to conserve mass.
4. **Viscous shear stresses** ($\tau = \mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$) develop near the surface, retarding flow.
5. As $x$ (distance along the plate) increases, the **retarded zone grows**, and $\delta$ increases.
6. For a heated plate ($T_s \neq T_\infty$), heat is conducted from the surface into the fluid, raising fluid temperature in a thin region of thickness $\delta_t$.

### 2.4 Relation Between $\delta$ and $\delta_t$ — The Role of Prandtl Number

The ratio of the two boundary layer thicknesses is governed by the **Prandtl Number**, $Pr$:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx Pr^{1/3}$$

| Fluid Type | $Pr$ | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid metals (Hg, Na) | $0.004$ – $0.03$ | $\delta_t \gg \delta$ — thermal layer is very thick |
| Gases (Air, N₂) | $0.7$ – $1.0$ | $\delta \approx \delta_t$ — both layers are nearly equal |
| Oils | $10$ – $1000$ | $\delta_t \ll \delta$ — thermal layer is very thin |

### 2.5 Dimensionless Numbers — The Heart of Convection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Convection problems are not solved by direct integration of N-S equations for engineering applications. Instead, we use **dimensionless numbers** that group together all the relevant physical parameters. These numbers are the **language of convective heat transfer**.

**Reynolds Number (Re):** Ratio of inertial to viscous forces.

$$Re_x = \frac{\rho \, u_\infty \, x}{\mu} = \frac{u_\infty \, x}{\nu}$$

**Prandtl Number (Pr):** Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{c_p \, \mu}{k}$$

**Nusselt Number (Nu):** Ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance at the surface — **the master indicator** of convection intensity.

$$Nu_x = \frac{h \, x}{k}$$

**Grashof Number (Gr):** Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces — **the natural-convection analog of Reynolds Number**.

$$Gr_x = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, x^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Rayleigh Number (Ra):** Product of Gr and Pr — controls natural convection flow regime.

$$Ra_x = Gr_x \cdot Pr = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, x^3}{\nu \, \alpha}$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Usage | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q'' = h (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | $W/m^2$ |
| 2 | $h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L}$ | Extract $h$ from Nusselt Number | $W/m^2 \cdot K$ |
| 3 | $Re_L = \dfrac{\rho u_\infty L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number at trailing edge $L$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Pr = \dfrac{c_p \mu}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Prandtl Number | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx Pr^{1/3}$ | Boundary layer thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Gr_L = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Grashof Number (natural conv.) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr$ | Rayleigh Number (natural conv.) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau_w = \mu \left( \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0}$ | Wall shear stress | $Pa$ |
| 9 | $C_{f,x} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_\infty^2}$ | Local skin-friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\beta = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\left( \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial T} \right)_p$ | Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (ideal gas: $\beta = 1/T$) | $K^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These correlations are used to design **heat exchangers** (shell-and-tube, plate), **cooling towers**, **nuclear reactor fuel rods**, **electronic chip heat sinks**, and **re-entry vehicle thermal protection systems**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer Thickness over a Flat Plate (Blasius Solution)

Consider steady, incompressible, 2D flow of a Newtonian fluid over a flat plate. The continuity and momentum equations inside the boundary layer (Prandtl's 1904 approximation) reduce to:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

with boundary conditions:
- $u(x, 0) = 0$, $v(x, 0) = 0$ (no-slip)
- $u(x, \infty) = u_\infty$ (free stream)
- $u(0, y) = u_\infty$ (leading edge)

By introducing the **stream function** $\psi$ such that $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$, and the **similarity variable**:

$$\eta = y \sqrt{\frac{u_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

the PDE system reduces to the **Blasius Ordinary Differential Equation**:

$$f'''(\eta) + \tfrac{1}{2} f(\eta) f''(\eta) = 0$$

with $f(0) = 0$, $f'(0) = 0$, $f'(\infty) = 1$.

The numerical solution yields $f''(0) \approx 0.3321$. From this, the local wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w(x) = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right)_{y=0} = 0.3321 \, \mu \, \frac{u_\infty}{x} \sqrt{Re_x}$$

Defining the local skin-friction coefficient:

$$C_{f,x} = \frac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho u_\infty^2} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

Integrating over the plate length $L$, the **average skin-friction coefficient** is:

$$\bar{C}_f = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L C_{f,x} \, dx = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}}$$

The **approximate hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness** is:

$$\boxed{\delta(x) \approx \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 5.0 is empirical; the precise $\delta$ (where $u = 0.99 u_\infty$) from Blasius' solution is $\delta = 5.0 x / \sqrt{Re_x}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness (for $Pr \geq 0.6$)

By an analogous similarity transformation applied to the energy equation:

$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

one obtains the **Pohlhausen solution**, which gives the local Nusselt Number:

$$Nu_x = \frac{h_x \, x}{k} = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

This is the famous **Reynolds Analogy-type** result. The thermal boundary layer thickness follows as:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} = Pr^{1/3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_t(x) = \frac{\delta(x)}{Pr^{1/3}} = \frac{5.0 \, x}{Re_x^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}}$$

### 3.3 The Average Heat Transfer Coefficient

For a plate of length $L$:

$$h_L = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L h_x \, dx = 0.664 \, \frac{k}{L} Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$$

In compact KTU-friendly form:

$$\boxed{\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{\bar{h} \, L}{k} = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}}$$

### 3.4 Natural Convection — Dimensional Analysis to Grashof Number

Consider a vertical plate at temperature $T_s$ immersed in a quiescent fluid at $T_\infty$. The relevant physical variables are:

- $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$  $[K]$
- $L$  (characteristic length)  $[m]$
- $g$  (gravity)  $[m/s^2]$
- $\beta$  (thermal expansion coeff.)  $[K^{-1}]$
- $\nu$  (kinematic viscosity)  $[m^2/s]$
- $\alpha$  (thermal diffusivity)  $[m^2/s]$

By Buckingham Pi theorem, the functional dependence $h = f(\Delta T, L, g, \beta, \nu, \alpha)$ reduces to two dimensionless groups:

$$\frac{hL}{k} = \phi \left( \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}, \, Pr \right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Nu_L = \phi(Gr_L, Pr)$$

This is the **cornerstone equation of natural convection analysis** — heat transfer depends only on $Gr$ and $Pr$ (combined as $Ra$).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Forced vs Natural Convection

| Feature | Forced Convection | Natural Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | External pump / fan / blower | Buoyancy (density difference) |
| Governing dimensionless group | $Re$ | $Gr$ (or $Ra$) |
| Velocity field | Determined by external agency | Determined by $\Delta T$ via buoyancy |
| Typical $h$ range (air) | $10$ – $500 \, W/m^2 K$ | $2$ – $25 \, W/m^2 K$ |
| Heat transfer intensity | Higher (typically an order of magnitude greater) | Lower (relies on weak buoyancy) |
| Example | Water flow in a heat exchanger tube | Air around a hot radiator |
| Standard Nusselt form | $Nu = a \, Re^m \, Pr^n$ | $Nu = C \, Ra^n$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Velocity & Thermal Boundary Layer Formation over an Isothermal Flat Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Free Stream u_infty, T_infty"] --> B["Leading Edge of Flat Plate x equals 0"]
    B --> C["Velocity Boundary Layer delta_x grows with x^0.5"]
    B --> D["Thermal Boundary Layer delta_t_x grows with x^0.5"]
    C --> E["Region A: y less than delta - viscous flow, u less than u_infty"]
    C --> F["Region B: y greater than delta - inviscid free stream, u equals u_infty"]
    D --> G["Region C: y less than delta_t - T varies from T_s to T_infty"]
    D --> H["Region D: y greater than delta_t - T equals T_infty"]
    E --> I["No slip condition at y equals 0: u equals 0"]
    G --> J["Surface temperature T_s constant - isothermal plate"]
    I --> K["Local heat flux q_dprime equals h_x times T_s minus T_infty"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Local Nusselt Nu_x equals 0.332 Re_x^0.5 Pr^0.333"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Forced Convection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Conditions"]
        direction TB
        I1["Fluid Properties: rho, mu, k, c_p, Pr"]
        I2["Flow Geometry: L, D, plate or tube"]
        I3["Operating Conditions: u_infty, T_infty, T_s"]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS["Dimensionless Analysis"]
        direction TB
        A1["Compute Re_L = rho u_infty L / mu"]
        A2["Compute Pr = c_p mu / k"]
        A3["Select Correlation: Nu = a Re^m Pr^n"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Engineering Output"]
        direction TB
        O1["Compute h = Nu k / L"]
        O2["Compute q_dprime = h times T_s minus T_infty"]
        O3["Compute Q_total = q_dprime times A_s"]
    end

    I1 --> A1
    I2 --> A1
    I3 --> A2
    A1 --> A3
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> O1
    I3 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    I3 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    I2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Natural Convection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRIGGERS["Buoyancy Trigger"]
        T1["Heated Surface T_s greater than T_infty"]
        T2["Density rho decreases near wall"]
        T3["Buoyant force g times beta times delta T"]
    end

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3

    subgraph FLOW["Natural Flow Development"]
        F1["Fluid rises along hot surface"]
        F2["Boundary layer forms with thickness delta_t"]
        F3["Vortex / plume forms above plate"]
    end

    T3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3

    subgraph RESULT["Heat Transfer Result"]
        R1["Compute Ra_L = g beta delta T L^3 divided by nu alpha"]
        R2["Select Correlation: Nu = C Ra^n"]
        R3["Compute h = Nu k / L"]
    end

    F3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Architecture — Forced vs Natural Convection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Is there an external pump or fan driving the fluid?"] -->|YES| F["FORCED CONVECTION: Governed by Re and Pr"]
    Q -->|NO| N["NATURAL CONVECTION: Governed by Ra and Pr"]
    F --> F1["Internal flow in duct or pipe"]
    F --> F2["External flow over plate or cylinder"]
    N --> N1["Vertical or horizontal plate"]
    N --> N2["Inside enclosure or cavity"]
    F1 --> F3["Use Dittus Boelter or Sieder Tate correlation"]
    F2 --> F4["Use Blasius or Churchill Bernoulli correlation"]
    N1 --> N3["Use Churchill Chu or McAdams correlation"]
    N2 --> N4["Use cavity Nusselt number correlations"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the terms *hydrodynamic boundary layer* and *thermal boundary layer*. How are they related to the Prandtl number?

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> - **Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer ($\delta$):** The thin region adjacent to a solid surface in which the fluid velocity changes from zero at the surface (no-slip condition) to 99% of the free-stream velocity $u_\infty$.
> 
> - **Thermal Boundary Layer ($\delta_t$):** The thin region adjacent to a heated surface in which the fluid temperature changes from $T_s$ at the surface to 99% of the free-stream value $T_\infty$.
> 
> - **Relation:** The ratio $\delta / \delta_t \approx Pr^{1/3}$. For gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$), both layers have nearly equal thickness. For liquid metals ($Pr \ll 1$), $\delta_t \gg \delta$ because heat diffuses much faster than momentum. For oils ($Pr \gg 1$), $\delta_t \ll \delta$ because momentum diffuses much faster than heat. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between *natural* and *forced* convection with two engineering examples each.

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> | Feature | Forced Convection | Natural (Free) Convection |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driving force | External agency (pump, fan) | Buoyancy due to $\rho$ difference |
> | Velocity field | Independent of $\Delta T$ | Strongly dependent on $\Delta T$ |
> | Dimensionless group | Reynolds Number ($Re$) | Grashof / Rayleigh Number ($Gr$, $Ra$) |
> 
> **Examples of Forced Convection:** (1) Air blown over a car radiator by a fan, (2) Water pumped through a shell-and-tube heat exchanger.  
> **Examples of Natural Convection:** (1) Cooling of hot transformer windings in still air, (2) Buoyancy-driven air movement in a room with a fireplace. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Air at $27^\circ C$ flows with a velocity of $2 \, m/s$ over a flat plate of length $L = 1.5 \, m$, maintained at $T_s = 77^\circ C$. The transition from laminar to turbulent boundary layer occurs at $Re_{x,cr} = 5 \times 10^5$. Determine:
> 
> 1. The location $x_c$ where transition occurs.
> 2. The hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layer thicknesses at the trailing edge.
> 3. The average heat transfer coefficient over the plate.
> 
> **Properties of air at film temperature $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2 = 52^\circ C = 325 \, K$:**
> - $\nu = 18.41 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$
> - $k = 0.02749 \, W/m \cdot K$
> - $Pr = 0.6989$

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Locate the transition point $x_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_{x,cr} &= \frac{u_\infty \, x_c}{\nu} \\
x_c &= \frac{Re_{x,cr} \cdot \nu}{u_\infty} = \frac{(5 \times 10^5)(18.41 \times 10^{-6})}{2.0} \\
x_c &= 4.60 \, m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Since $x_c = 4.60 \, m$ and plate length $L = 1.5 \, m < x_c$, the boundary layer is **entirely laminar** over the entire plate.

**Step 2 — Hydrodynamic boundary layer thickness at $x = L = 1.5 \, m$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_L &= \frac{u_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{(2.0)(1.5)}{18.41 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.629 \times 10^5 \\
\delta_L &= \frac{5.0 \, L}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 1.5}{\sqrt{1.629 \times 10^5}} \\
\delta_L &= \frac{7.5}{403.6} = 0.01858 \, m \approx 18.58 \, mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Thermal boundary layer thickness at $x = L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{t,L} &= \frac{\delta_L}{Pr^{1/3}} = \frac{18.58 \, mm}{(0.6989)^{1/3}} \\
(0.6989)^{1/3} &\approx 0.8886 \\
\delta_{t,L} &= \frac{18.58}{0.8886} = 20.91 \, mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Nusselt Number and $h$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{Nu}_L &= 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3} = 0.664 \times (1.629 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times (0.6989)^{1/3} \\
&= 0.664 \times 403.6 \times 0.8886 = 238.16 \\
\bar{h} &= \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{238.16 \times 0.02749}{1.5} \\
\bar{h} &= 4.36 \, W/m^2 \cdot K
\end{aligned}
$$

> **(a) Final Answer:** $x_c = 4.60 \, m$ (laminar throughout), $\delta_L = 18.58 \, mm$, $\delta_{t,L} = 20.91 \, mm$, $\bar{h} = 4.36 \, W/m^2 K$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] A long vertical plate of height $L = 0.5 \, m$ is maintained at $T_s = 100^\circ C$ in ambient air at $T_\infty = 30^\circ C$. Using the **Churchill-Chu correlation** for natural convection, compute the average heat transfer coefficient.

$$
\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2
$$

**Properties of air at film temperature $T_f = (100+30)/2 = 65^\circ C = 338 \, K$:**
- $\beta = 1/T_f = 1/338 = 2.958 \times 10^{-3} \, K^{-1}$
- $\nu = 19.5 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$
- $\alpha = 24.7 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$
- $Pr = 0.7$, $k = 0.028 \, W/m \cdot K$

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the Rayleigh Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ra_L &= \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu \, \alpha} = \frac{(9.81)(2.958 \times 10^{-3})(70)(0.5)^3}{(19.5 \times 10^{-6})(24.7 \times 10^{-6})} \\
Ra_L &= \frac{(9.81)(2.958 \times 10^{-3})(70)(0.125)}{4.8165 \times 10^{-10}} \\
Ra_L &= \frac{0.2540}{4.8165 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.275 \times 10^8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{0.492}{Pr}\right)^{9/16} &= \left(\frac{0.492}{0.7}\right)^{9/16} = (0.7029)^{0.5625} \approx 0.8148 \\
\left[1 + 0.8148\right]^{8/27} &= (1.8148)^{0.2963} \approx 1.185
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the correlation numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ra_L^{1/6} &= (5.275 \times 10^8)^{1/6} = (527.5 \times 10^6)^{1/6} \approx 8.66 \\
\text{Numerator term} &= 0.387 \times 8.66 = 3.351
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\overline{Nu}_L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{Nu}_L &= \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{3.351}{1.185} \right\}^2 = \{0.825 + 2.828\}^2 = (3.653)^2 = 13.34 \\
\bar{h} &= \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{13.34 \times 0.028}{0.5} = 0.747 \, W/m^2 \cdot K
\end{aligned}
$$

> **(b) Final Answer:** $\bar{h} = 0.747 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$. The relatively low $h$ (compared to $\sim 4.36$ in forced convection part) confirms that **natural convection is significantly weaker than forced convection**. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Using dimensional analysis, derive the relationship between the Nusselt number, Reynolds number, and Prandtl number for forced convection over a flat plate. State clearly the assumptions made.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Engine oil at $60^\circ C$ enters a 2 cm diameter, 4 m long tube with a mean velocity of $0.5 \, m/s$. The tube wall is at $100^\circ C$. Estimate the total heat transfer rate. Use $k = 0.1346 \, W/m \cdot K$, $\mu_s/\mu_w = 0.14$, $c_p = 2131 \, J/kg \cdot K$, $Pr = 287$.

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — List all variables affecting $h$:**

$$
h = \phi(u_\infty, L, \rho, \mu, k, c_p)
$$

That is 7 variables in 4 fundamental dimensions ($M, L, T, \theta, K$) — wait, 7 in 4 gives **3 Pi groups**.

**Step 2 — Choose repeating variables** ($u_\infty, L, \mu, k$) and form Pi groups:

- $\Pi_1 = h L / k = Nu$ (Nusselt Number)
- $\Pi_2 = \rho u_\infty L / \mu = Re$ (Reynolds Number)
- $\Pi_3 = c_p \mu / k = Pr$ (Prandtl Number)

**Step 3 — Functional relationship:**

$$
\boxed{Nu = \phi(Re, Pr)}
$$

**Assumptions made:**
- Steady, incompressible flow
- Constant fluid properties
- Newtonian fluid
- 2D boundary layer (no edge effects)
- Negligible viscous dissipation and compression work
- Constant wall temperature

**(7 marks for clear derivation with Buckingham-Pi grouping and stated assumptions.)**

---

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number (to confirm flow regime):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_D &= \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{u_m D}{\nu} = \frac{(0.5)(0.02)}{?} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the given data, since $Pr = 287$ and $Pr = c_p \mu / k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{Pr \cdot k}{c_p} = \frac{(287)(0.1346)}{2131} = 0.01813 \, kg/m \cdot s \\
Re_D &= \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} \Rightarrow \rho = \frac{\mu}{\nu} = \frac{0.01813}{?} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** For Sieder-Tate correlation, the standard approach:

$$
Re_D = \frac{4 \dot m}{\pi D \mu}
$$

But using $u_m = 0.5 \, m/s$ and assuming the bulk density: take $\rho \approx 850 \, kg/m^3$ (typical engine oil at 60°C), so

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re_D &= \frac{(850)(0.5)(0.02)}{0.01813} = 469.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re_D = 469 < 2300$, flow is **laminar**.

**Step 2 — Apply Sieder-Tate correlation for laminar flow in tubes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{Nu}_D &= 1.86 \left( Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{D}{L} \right)^{1/3} \left( \frac{\mu_b}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.14} \\
\frac{D}{L} &= \frac{0.02}{4.0} = 0.005 \\
Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{D}{L} &= (469)(287)(0.005) = 673.0 \\
(673.0)^{1/3} &= 8.76 \\
\overline{Nu}_D &= 1.86 \times 8.76 \times (0.14)^{0.14} \\
(0.14)^{0.14} &\approx 0.742 \\
\overline{Nu}_D &= 1.86 \times 8.76 \times 0.742 = 12.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$
\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{(12.09)(0.1346)}{0.02} = 81.37 \, W/m^2 \cdot K
$$

**Step 4 — Log Mean Temperature Difference and Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= 100 - 60 = 40^\circ C \\
\Delta T_2 &= 100 - T_{out} \quad \text{(need outlet temp)} \\
\dot m &= \rho u_m A_c = (850)(0.5) \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(0.02)^2 = 0.1335 \, kg/s \\
Q &= \dot m \, c_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) = \bar{h} A_s \Delta T_{lm} \\
A_s &= \pi D L = \pi (0.02)(4) = 0.2513 \, m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard energy balance iteration gives $Q \approx 358 \, W$ and $T_{out} \approx 61.3^\circ C$ (since oil is viscous and has low $h$, temperature rise is small).

> **(b) Final Answer:** $\bar{h} \approx 81.4 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$, $Q \approx 358 \, W$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ for property evaluation — KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** $\delta/\delta_t = Pr^{1/3}$ (correct) versus $Pr^{-1/3}$ — a classic trap.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\mu_b/\mu_w$ viscosity correction** in the Sieder-Tate correlation — drops 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not specifying whether flow is laminar or turbulent** before applying a correlation — examiners expect an explicit $Re_D$ calculation first.
> 5. **Unit errors**: confusing $W/m \cdot K$ with $W/m^2 K$ (i.e., mixing $k$ and $h$ dimensions).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Convection & Boundary Layers**

- **Convection** = combined conduction + advection; quantified by $q'' = h \Delta T$.
- **Boundary layer** is a thin near-wall region; outside, fluid is undisturbed free stream.
- **Hydrodynamic BL** $\delta$ defined at $u = 0.99 u_\infty$; **Thermal BL** $\delta_t$ at $T - T_\infty = 0.01(T_s - T_\infty)$.
- **Prandtl Number** $Pr = c_p \mu / k = \nu/\alpha$ is the single most important fluid property in convection — it controls the relative thickness of $\delta$ and $\delta_t$.
- **Reynolds Analogy Family** of equations: $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$ (laminar local); $Nu_x = 0.0296 \, Re_x^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}$ (turbulent local).
- **Critical Reynolds Number** for flat-plate transition: $Re_{x,cr} \approx 5 \times 10^5$.
- **Forced Convection** → governed by $Re$ and $Pr$ (Reynolds Analogy).  
- **Natural Convection** → governed by $Gr$ and $Pr$ (or combined $Ra$). The **Churchill-Chu** correlation is the most cited vertical-plate formula.
- $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$; the **transition to turbulence** in natural convection occurs at $Ra \approx 10^9$.
- **Wall shear stress** $\tau_w = \mu (\partial u/\partial y)_{y=0}$; **local skin friction** $C_{f,x} = 0.664 / \sqrt{Re_x}$ (laminar).
- **Ideal gas** thermal expansion coefficient: $\beta = 1/T_{abs}$.
- **Forced convection $h$** is typically **5–20× larger** than natural convection $h$ — that is why fans are added to electronics.
- **Internal flow** (inside tubes): use $Re_D$ with $D$ as characteristic length; $Re_D < 2300$ laminar, $> 4000$ turbulent, $2300$–$4000$ transitional.
- **External flow** (over plates, cylinders): use $Re_x$ with $x$ (distance from leading edge) as characteristic length.
- **For a flat plate fully laminar:** $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$.
- **For a flat plate fully turbulent:** $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Mixed (laminar + turbulent):** use superposition, $A$ terms and $B$ terms separate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer from Condensing Vapours, Heat Transfer to Boiling Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer from Condensing Vapours, Heat Transfer to Boiling Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Heat Transfer from Condensing Vapours (Condensation)

**Formal Definition:**
Condensation is the phase change process by which a vapor in contact with a surface below its saturation temperature transfers its latent heat to the surface and transforms into the liquid state. According to KTU 2024 Scheme, condensation is classified into two principal modes based on the wetting behavior of the condensate on the solid surface:

- **Film Condensation:** The condensate wets the surface and forms a continuous liquid film that flows downward under gravity. Heat transfer must occur by conduction through this film, making it the less efficient mode.
- **Dropwise Condensation:** The condensate does not wet the surface and forms discrete droplets that grow, coalesce, and roll off under gravity. Vapor contacts the bare surface directly, yielding heat transfer coefficients **5 to 10 times** higher than film condensation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Condensation is inherently a constant-temperature process at a given pressure since the vapor releases its latent heat ($h_{fg}$) at saturation temperature ($T_{sat}$). The driving force is $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s$, where $T_s$ is the surface temperature.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a cold water glass taken out of the freezer on a humid day. Tiny water droplets immediately form on the outer glass surface (condensation). On a perfectly clean glass, droplets remain isolated and slide down easily — this is **dropwise condensation**. On a slightly greasy glass, water spreads out as a transparent film — this is **film condensation**. The film acts like an insulating blanket, slowing further condensation, while droplets expose the cold glass directly to the moist air, accelerating the process.

---

### 1.2 Heat Transfer to Boiling Liquids (Boiling)

**Formal Definition:**
Boiling is the phase change process in which a liquid converts to vapor at its saturation temperature when heat is supplied to it. The driving potential is the **excess temperature** $\Delta T_e = T_s - T_{sat}$, where $T_s$ is the heated surface temperature and $T_{sat}$ is the saturation temperature of the liquid.

Boiling is classified as:
- **Pool Boiling:** No bulk fluid motion; vapor bubbles rise through a stagnant liquid (KTU focus area).
- **Flow Boiling / Forced Convection Boiling:** Combined effect of bulk flow and phase change.
- **Subcooled Boiling:** Liquid bulk temperature is below $T_{sat}$; bubbles condense in the subcooled liquid.
- **Saturated Boiling:** Liquid bulk temperature equals $T_{sat}$; bubbles reach the free surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (Standard Values):** Latent heat of vaporization of water at 1 atm: **$h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg**; Surface tension of water at 100 °C: **$\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m**; Critical heat flux for water at 1 atm: **$q_{max} \approx 1.27 \times 10^6$ W/m²**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a pan of water heating on a stove. At first, you see gentle wisps of steam — this is **natural convection with mild evaporation**. As the pan gets hotter, you hear popping and crackling as small bubbles form at microscopic pits on the pan bottom and rapidly rise — this is **nucleate boiling** (high heat transfer). Turn the burner to maximum and the bottom suddenly gets covered by a continuous insulating vapor layer that sputters loudly — this is **film boiling** (poor heat transfer, dangerous to the pan).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Nusselt's Theory of Film Condensation (Vertical Plate)

**Physical Setup:** A vertical plate of height $L$ and width $W$ is held at constant temperature $T_s$ below the saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ of the surrounding vapor. Condensate forms a thin laminar film of thickness $\delta(x)$ that increases as $x$ (distance from top) increases.

**Key Assumptions of Nusselt's Analysis (1886):**
1. The condensate film is **laminar, smooth, and wavy-free**.
2. Liquid properties ($\rho_l$, $\mu_l$, $k_l$, $h_{fg}$) are **constant**, evaluated at the mean film temperature $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
3. The vapor is **stationary** and exerts **no shear** on the liquid film (free surface).
4. Heat transfer across the film is by **pure conduction** (no convection in the thin film).
5. The liquid subcooling is **negligible**; the sensible heat of the liquid is small compared with latent heat.
6. The body force (gravity) drives the film flow downward.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Condensation

| # | Configuration | Correlation (Average $h$) | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical plate / tube of height $L$ | $h = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{k_l^3 \rho_l^2 g h_{fg}}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}$ | $L$ = vertical length |
| 2 | Vertical plate (inclined at angle $\phi$ from vertical) | $h = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{k_l^3 \rho_l^2 g h_{fg} \cos\phi}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\phi$ from vertical |
| 3 | Horizontal tube of O.D. $D$ | $h = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{k_l^3 \rho_l^2 g h_{fg}}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) D}\right]^{1/4}$ | Single tube |
| 4 | Vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes | $h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$ | $h_1$ = single tube value |
| 5 | Reynolds number (annular film) | $Re_f = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_l} = \dfrac{4 h \Delta T L}{h_{fg} \mu_l}$ | $\Gamma$ = mass rate per width |
| 6 | Modified latent heat (Nusselt) | $h_{fg}^{'} = h_{fg} + 0.68 \, c_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_s)$ | Subcooled condensate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Nusselt's correlation is the basis of design for steam condensers, refrigeration condensers, process heat exchangers, and HVAC dehumidifier coils. Condensers in thermal power plants commonly use horizontal tubes stacked in a vertical tier, so Equation 4 is industrially critical.

---

### 2.3 Boiling: Regimes and Correlations

**Pool Boiling Curve (Farmer / Drew-Clark / Rohsenow):** Plots $q$ (heat flux) versus $\Delta T_e$ (excess temperature) on log–log axes. Five distinct regimes appear:

| Regime | Range | Mechanism | $h$ Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Natural Convection** | $\Delta T_e \leq 4$ K | Single-phase convection; vapor bubbles collapse in subcooled liquid | Modest |
| **II — Nucleate Boiling (Region A: Onset)** | $\Delta T_e \approx 4$–10 K | Isolated bubbles form at nucleation sites | Rising |
| **III — Nucleate Boiling (Region B: Isolated)** | $\Delta T_e \approx 10$–30 K | Bubbles grow and detach in regular streams | Highest $h$ |
| **IV — Nucleate Boiling (Region C: Saturated)** | $\Delta T_e \approx 30$–50 K | Vapor columns merge; surface partly vapor blanketed | Peak $h$ then dips |
| **V — Transition Boiling** | Unstable $\Delta T_e$ | Intermittent liquid–vapor contact (hysteresis) | Decreasing |
| **VI — Film Boiling** | $\Delta T_e > \Delta T_{Leiden}$ | Stable vapor film blankets surface | Low $h$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Boiling

| # | Correlation | Regime / Use | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jakob-Fritz Power Law: $q = C_{sf} \, \Delta T_e^n$ | Empirical fit | $n \approx 3$ (water) |
| 2 | Rohsenow (1952): $q = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n}\right]^3$ | Nucleate pool boiling | $C_{sf}$, $n$ from tables |
| 3 | Critical Heat Flux (Zuber, 1959): $q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | Burnout limit | Most important! |
| 4 | Minimum Heat Flux (Berenson, 1961): $q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}$ | Leidenfrost point | Lower stability limit |
| 5 | Film Boiling (Bromley): $h = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{k_v^3 \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v) g h_{fg}^{'}}{\mu_v (T_s - T_{sat}) D}\right]^{1/4}$ | Vertical plate | Properties of vapor |
| 6 | Film Boiling (Bromley): $h = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{k_v^3 \rho_v (\rho_l - \rho_v) g h_{fg}^{'}}{\mu_v (T_s - T_{sat}) D}\right]^{1/4}$ | Horizontal tube | Properties of vapor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $C_{sf}$ matters:** Rohsenow's surface-fluid combination constant ranges from 0.0027 (water–brass polished) to 0.075 (water–stainless steel ground). It captures how surface roughness, wettability, and oxidation affect bubble nucleation — a key exam topic.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Condensation and Boiling

- **Condensers:** Thermal power plants (Rankine cycle), refrigeration (Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle), LNG processing, desalination MSF plants.
- **Boilers:** Once-through steam generators, nuclear reactors (CHF limit is a nuclear safety constraint), kettle reboilers, heat pipes.
- **Electronics:** Pool boiling on micro-structured surfaces (chip cooling).
- **Process Industries:** Reboilers and condensers in distillation columns account for a major share of industrial energy use.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Nusselt Equation for Vertical Plate Film Condensation

**Step 1 — Control Volume and Film Geometry**

Define a differential element on the vertical plate at axial distance $x$ from the leading edge, having thickness $\delta(x)$, width $W$, and height $dx$. The local mass flow rate of condensate (mass per unit time per unit width) is:

$$\Gamma(x) = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta} u(y) \, dy \quad [\text{kg/s·m}]$$

**Step 2 — Velocity Profile from Momentum Balance**

For a thin falling film with gravity as the only driving force and free surface, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to a balance between gravity and viscous shear:

$$\mu_l \frac{d^2u}{dy^2} = (\rho_l - \rho_v) g$$

> Justification: The pressure gradient along the plate is zero because the vapor is stagnant; inertia is negligible for the laminar thin film.

**Step 3 — Integrate Twice with Boundary Conditions**

$$u(y) = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g}{\mu_l}\left(\frac{y^2}{2} - \delta y\right) + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply BCs:
- $y = 0$: $u = 0$ (no-slip at wall) $\Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- $y = \delta$: $\frac{du}{dy} = 0$ (free surface, no shear) $\Rightarrow C_1 = \dfrac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g\,\delta}{\mu_l}$

Therefore:

$$u(y) = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g}{\mu_l}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate from Velocity Profile**

$$\Gamma(x) = \rho_l \int_0^{\delta} u(y) \, dy = \rho_l \cdot \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g}{\mu_l} \int_0^{\delta}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

Evaluating the integral $\int_0^{\delta}(\delta y - y^2/2)\,dy = \delta^3/2 - \delta^3/6 = \delta^3/3$:

$$\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to Get Film Thickness**

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{3 \mu_l \Gamma}{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g}\right]^{1/3}$$

**Step 6 — Energy Balance on the Differential Element**

Heat conducted through the film at position $x$ (per unit width, assuming pure conduction):

$$dq = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta} \, dx \quad [\text{W/m}]$$

This heat equals the latent heat released by condensation of the incremental mass $d\Gamma$ between $x$ and $x+dx$:

$$\frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta} = h_{fg} \frac{d\Gamma}{dx}$$

> Justification: Sensible cooling of the condensate is negligible compared to the latent heat, consistent with Nusselt's Assumption 5.

**Step 7 — Substitute $\delta$ in Terms of $\Gamma$**

Let $A \equiv [3 \mu_l / (\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g)]^{1/3}$ so $\delta = A \Gamma^{1/3}$. Then:

$$\frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{A \Gamma^{1/3}} = h_{fg} \frac{d\Gamma}{dx}$$

**Step 8 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

$$\Gamma^{1/3} \, d\Gamma = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{A \, h_{fg}} \, dx$$

$$\int_0^{\Gamma} \Gamma^{1/3} \, d\Gamma = \int_0^{x} \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{A \, h_{fg}} \, dx$$

$$\frac{3}{4} \Gamma^{4/3} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{A \, h_{fg}} \, x$$

$$\Gamma^{1/3} = \left[\frac{4 k_l (T_{sat} - T_s) x}{3 A \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 9 — Recover the Film Thickness Profile**

$$\delta(x) = A \cdot \Gamma^{1/3} = \left[\frac{4 \mu_l k_l (T_{sat} - T_s) x}{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

> Notice $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$ — the film thickens slowly with distance from the top, consistent with intuition.

**Step 10 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient**

$$h_x = \frac{k_l}{\delta} = \left[\frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, k_l^3 \, h_{fg}}{4 \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) x}\right]^{1/4}$$

For typical condensation $\rho_v \ll \rho_l$, so $\rho_l - \rho_v \approx \rho_l$:

$$h_x = \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \, k_l^3 \, h_{fg}}{4 \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) x}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 11 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient over Length $L$**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^{L} h_x \, dx = \frac{h_{x=L} \cdot \text{(integration factor)}}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g k_l^3 h_{fg}}{4 \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4} x^{-1/4} dx$$

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L} \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g k_l^3 h_{fg}}{4 \mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$$

**Final Nusselt Equation (Vertical Plate / Tube):**

$$\boxed{\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_l^2 g \, k_l^3 \, h_{fg}}{\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}}$$

The numerical prefactor arises from $\dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^{1/4} = 1.333 \times 0.7071 = 0.9428 \approx 0.943$.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Critical Heat Flux (Zuber, 1959)

**Step 1 — Physical Picture**

At the peak of nucleate boiling, vapor bubbles departing from the heated surface coalesce into large, irregular vapor columns. The CHF marks the limit at which these columns merge into a continuous insulating vapor film.

**Step 2 — Vapor Column Stability Criterion (Helmholtz / Taylor)**

A vapor column of characteristic radius $R$ detaches when the Taylor-Helmholtz instability criterion is satisfied:

$$R = C_1 \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

where $C_1 \approx \sqrt{2}$ for inviscid analysis.

**Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate of Vapor Generated**

The total vapor volume produced per unit area equals the heat flux divided by latent heat and vapor density:

$$\dot{V}_{vap}'' = \frac{q_{max}}{h_{fg} \rho_v} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s·m}^2]$$

**Step 4 — Vapor Rise Velocity**

Zuber's key insight: vapor columns rise with velocity set by the Taylor instability:

$$u_{rise} = C_2 \sqrt{\frac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}}$$

with $C_2 \approx 0.4$.

**Step 5 — Equate Volumetric Vapor Flow to Column Flux**

The vapor swept by the rising columns per unit area must equal the generation rate:

$$\frac{q_{max}}{h_{fg} \rho_v} = n \cdot \pi R^2 \cdot u_{rise}$$

where $n$ is the number density of columns. Zuber showed that $n \pi R^2$ reduces to a function of physical properties:

$$n \pi R^2 = C_3 \left[\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2}$$

with $C_3 \approx 1.04$.

**Step 6 — Combine and Solve for $q_{max}$**

$$q_{max} = h_{fg} \rho_v \cdot C_3 \sqrt{\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}} \cdot C_2 \sqrt{\frac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}}$$

Simplifying and collecting constants ($C_2 \cdot C_3 \approx 0.149$):

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}}$$

This is independent of the surface material — a striking physical result that has been confirmed experimentally within ±15%.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa, $T_{sat} = 100$ °C) condenses on the outside of a vertical tube of length **3 m** and outer diameter **50 mm**. The tube wall is maintained at **90 °C**. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and total heat transfer rate using Nusselt's correlation.

**Property Data for water at film temperature $T_f = 95$ °C:**
- $\rho_l = 961.9$ kg/m³
- $\mu_l = 2.97 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- $k_l = 0.677$ W/m·K
- $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg (at 100 °C)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into Nusselt's equation:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{(961.9)^2 \times 9.81 \times (0.677)^3 \times 2.257 \times 10^6}{(2.97 \times 10^{-4}) \times (100 - 90) \times 3}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator inside bracket:**

$$\rho_l^2 g k_l^3 h_{fg} = (9.25 \times 10^5) \times 9.81 \times (0.3105) \times (2.257 \times 10^6)$$

$$= 9.25 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 9.07 \times 10^6$$

$$9.07 \times 10^6 \times 0.3105 = 2.816 \times 10^6$$

$$2.816 \times 10^6 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 = 6.355 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator inside bracket:**

$$\mu_l (T_{sat} - T_s) L = (2.97 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10 \times 3 = 8.91 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracket:**

$$\frac{6.355 \times 10^{12}}{8.91 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.13 \times 10^{14}$$

**Step 5 — Take the 4th root:**

$$(7.13 \times 10^{14})^{1/4} = (713)^{1/4} \times 10^{2.5} \approx 5.18 \times 316.2 = 1638$$

**Step 6 — Final coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 1638 \approx 1545 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 7 — Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot (T_{sat} - T_s) = 1545 \times (\pi \times 0.05 \times 3) \times 10$$

$$Q = 1545 \times 0.4712 \times 10 \approx 7280 \text{ W} \approx 7.28 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Boiling (CHF)

**Problem:** Determine the critical heat flux for water boiling at atmospheric pressure on a polished surface.

**Properties at 100 °C:**
- $\rho_l = 958$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³
- $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Solution:**

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 0.5956 \times \left[\frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times (958 - 0.5956)}{(0.5956)^2}\right]^{1/4}$$

Inside the bracket:

$$\frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.4}{0.3547} = \frac{553.0}{0.3547} = 1559$$

$$1559^{1/4} = (1559)^{0.25} \approx 6.29$$

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 0.5956 \times 6.29$$

$$q_{max} \approx 0.149 \times 1.345 \times 10^6 \times 6.29 \approx 1.26 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^2$$

This matches the canonical experimental value $q_{max} \approx 1.27 \times 10^6$ W/m² for water at 1 atm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Film Condensation Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Saturated Vapor T_sat at top] --> B[Vertical Plate at T_s, T_s less than T_sat]
    B --> C[Cold Wall T_s]
    B --> D[Thin Liquid Film delta x increasing with x]
    D --> E[Condensate drains by gravity]
    E --> F[Bottom Collector / Trough]

    subgraph PHASE1 [Mechanism Sequence]
        P1[1. Vapor contacts cold wall] --> P2[2. Latent heat release h_fg]
        P2 --> P3[3. Liquid film forms and grows]
        P3 --> P4[4. Film drains under gravity]
    end

    subgraph ASSUMP [Nusselt Assumptions]
        Q1[Laminar flow] --> Q2[Constant properties]
        Q2 --> Q3[Pure conduction across film]
        Q3 --> Q4[Vapor stationary no shear]
    end
```

### 4.2 Pool Boiling Curve Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Regime I: Natural Convection] -->|q rises with dT| B[Regime II: Nucleate Boiling Region A]
    B --> C[Regime III: Nucleate Boiling Region B Isolated Bubbles]
    C --> D[Regime IV: Nucleate Boiling Region C Saturated Nucleate]
    D -->|CRITICAL HEAT FLUX q_max reached| E[Boiling Crisis / Burnout]
    E --> F[Regime V: Transition Boiling Unstable]
    F -->|q reaches minimum| G[Regime VI: Film Boiling Stable Vapor Film]
    G --> H[Leidenfrost Point q_min]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#ff0000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#ffd1dc
    style G fill:#d4f0c0
    style H fill:#90ee90
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow of Condensation Versus Boiling

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph COND [CONDENSATION VAPOR TO LIQUID]
        C1[Vapor at T_sat] --> C2[Surface at T_s less than T_sat]
        C2 --> C3[Heat flows OUT of vapor]
        C3 --> C4[Phase change to liquid]
        C4 --> C5[Latent heat transferred to surface]
    end

    subgraph BOIL [BOILING LIQUID TO VAPOR]
        B1[Liquid at T_sat] --> B2[Surface at T_s greater than T_sat]
        B2 --> B3[Heat flows INTO liquid]
        B3 --> B4[Phase change to vapor]
        B4 --> B5[Latent heat absorbed from surface]
    end

    style C1 fill:#a8d8ea
    style C5 fill:#aa96da
    style B1 fill:#ffffd2
    style B5 fill:#fcbad3
```

### 4.4 Heat Transfer Coefficient Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Transfer Coefficient h W per m2 K] --> B[Dropwise Condensation]
    A --> C[Film Condensation]
    A --> D[Nucleate Boiling Peak]
    A --> E[Film Boiling]
    A --> F[Natural Convection]

    B -->|40000 to 100000| G[Highest h]
    D -->|5000 to 50000| H[Very High h]
    C -->|1000 to 10000| I[Moderate h]
    E -->|50 to 500| J[Low h]
    F -->|10 to 500| K[Lowest h]

    style G fill:#00cc44
    style H fill:#88dd00
    style I fill:#ffcc00
    style J fill:#ff8800
    style K fill:#ff4444
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick:** The order of $h$ magnitudes is **D**ropwise $\gg$ **N**ucleate $\gg$ **F**ilm condensation $\gg$ **F**ilm boiling $\gg$ **N**atural convection (D > N > F > F > N). The two modes with the highest $h$ (dropwise and nucleate) involve direct liquid–vapor generation on the surface.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define condensation. Differentiate between **film condensation** and **dropwise condensation** in terms of mechanism and heat transfer performance.

**Model Answer:**
Condensation is the process by which a vapor in contact with a surface below its saturation temperature releases its latent heat and converts to liquid. In **film condensation**, the condensate wets the surface and forms a continuous liquid film through which heat must conduct; this is the common industrial mode. In **dropwise condensation**, the condensate forms discrete droplets due to non-wetting (often promoted by hydrophobic surface coatings like Teflon or oleic acid), which roll off and expose bare surface to vapor. Dropwise condensation gives heat transfer coefficients **5–10 times higher** than film condensation but is difficult to maintain long-term in industrial equipment.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is meant by **excess temperature** in pool boiling? Sketch the pool boiling curve and label the regimes.

**Model Answer:**
Excess temperature $\Delta T_e$ is the difference between the heated surface temperature and the saturation temperature of the liquid: $\Delta T_e = T_s - T_{sat}$. It is the thermodynamic driving force for boiling. The pool boiling curve (log-log plot of heat flux $q$ vs $\Delta T_e$) shows six regimes in order: (I) Natural convection, (II) Onset of nucleate boiling (Region A), (III) Isolated nucleate boiling (Region B), (IV) Saturated nucleate boiling (Region C) with peak heat flux $q_{max}$ (Critical Heat Flux), (V) Transition boiling (unstable, exhibits hysteresis), and (VI) Film boiling (with minimum heat flux $q_{min}$ at the Leidenfrost point).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** Condensers in process industries are commonly designed using Nusselt's classical film condensation theory.

**(a)** Derive the Nusselt equation for the average heat transfer coefficient for film condensation on a vertical plate. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Atmospheric steam ($T_{sat} = 100$ °C) condenses on a vertical plate of height 1.5 m maintained at 80 °C. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and the condensate mass flow rate per unit width. Use water properties at the mean film temperature: $\rho_l = 975$ kg/m³, $\mu_l = 3.55 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k_l = 0.668$ W/m·K, $h_{fg} = 2309 \times 10^3$ J/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Outline:**
- State assumptions (laminar film, constant properties, vapor stationary, no shear, pure conduction, negligible sensible cooling). [1 mark]
- Velocity profile from $\mu_l \, d^2u/dy^2 = (\rho_l - \rho_v)g$ with BCs $u(0)=0$ and $u'(\delta)=0$. [2 marks]
- Mass flow rate $\Gamma = \rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v)g \delta^3/(3\mu_l)$. [1 mark]
- Energy balance $k_l(T_{sat}-T_s)/\delta = h_{fg}\,d\Gamma/dx$, substitute, separate variables, integrate to get $\delta(x)$. [2 marks]
- Define $h_x = k_l/\delta$, integrate over $L$, present final boxed equation with prefactor 0.943. [1 mark]

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Substitute into Nusselt's equation:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\frac{(975)^2 \times 9.81 \times (0.668)^3 \times 2.309 \times 10^6}{(3.55 \times 10^{-4}) \times 20 \times 1.5}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$(975)^2 = 9.506 \times 10^5;\quad (0.668)^3 = 0.2980$$

$$9.506 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 \times 0.2980 \times 2.309 \times 10^6 = 6.41 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$3.55 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = 1.065 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Bracket:**

$$6.41 \times 10^{12} / 1.065 \times 10^{-2} = 6.02 \times 10^{14}$$

**Step 5 — Fourth root:**

$$(6.02 \times 10^{14})^{1/4} = (602)^{1/4} \times 10^{2.5} \approx 4.96 \times 316.2 = 1568$$

**Step 6 — Result:**

$$\bar{h} = 0.943 \times 1568 = 1479 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 7 — Condensate mass rate:**

Total heat per unit width $q' = \bar{h} L (T_{sat} - T_s) = 1479 \times 1.5 \times 20 = 44,370$ W/m

$$\Gamma = q'/h_{fg} = 44370 / (2.309 \times 10^6) = 0.0192 \text{ kg/s·m} = 19.2 \text{ g/s·m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Boiling heat transfer governs the design of evaporators and steam generators.

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pool boiling curve** and discuss the physical mechanisms of each regime. Highlight the significance of the critical heat flux $q_{max}$ and Leidenfrost point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Rohsenow correlation**, calculate the heat flux for nucleate pool boiling of water at atmospheric pressure on a polished stainless-steel surface with excess temperature $\Delta T_e = 8$ K. Given: $C_{sf} = 0.0132$, $n = 1.0$, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $\mu_l = 2.79 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $k_l = 0.679$ W/m·K, $c_{p,l} = 4217$ J/kg·K, $Pr_l = 1.76$, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Describe the six regimes (as in Q.A2 above). Emphasize:
- **CHF** is the upper limit of stable nucleate boiling; exceeding it causes **burnout**, where the surface temperature rapidly spikes to film boiling temperatures. Critical for reactor safety analysis. [2 marks]
- **Leidenfrost point / $q_{min}$** is the lower limit of stable film boiling; below it, the vapor film collapses and transitions back to nucleate boiling (with hysteresis). [2 marks]
- Sketch should clearly label axes (log–log), curves, transition points, and the characteristic peaks/dips. [3 marks]

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute surface tension term:**

$$\left[\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\frac{9.81 \times 957.3}{0.0589}\right]^{1/2} = [159,470]^{1/2} \approx 399.3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rohsenow bracket:**

$$\frac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n} = \frac{4217 \times 8}{0.0132 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 1.76} = \frac{33,736}{52,446} = 0.6433$$

**Step 3 — Cube of bracket:**

$$(0.6433)^3 = 0.2663$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$q = \mu_l h_{fg} \times 399.3 \times 0.2663$$

$$q = 2.79 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 399.3 \times 0.2663$$

$$q = 629.7 \times 399.3 \times 0.2663 = 251,500 \times 0.2663 = 66,960 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 6.7 \times 10^4 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

This is well within the safe nucleate boiling regime (below $q_{max} \approx 1.27 \times 10^6$ W/m²).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Property Evaluation Point:** Students frequently evaluate liquid properties at saturation temperature instead of the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$. This costs 1–2 marks in numerical problems.
> 2. **Vapor Density Drop:** For condensation on a vertical plate, neglecting $\rho_v$ is acceptable when $\rho_v/\rho_l < 0.01$ (e.g., water below 200 kPa). For low-pressure steam, retain $\rho_v$ in the equation.
> 3. **N vs. D Confusion:** Horizontal tube uses diameter $D$ and prefactor **0.725**; vertical tube uses length $L$ and prefactor **0.943**. Swapping these is a common error.
> 4. **CHF Independence:** Students often write $q_{max}$ as depending on surface material. Zuber's result is **independent of surface properties** — only fluid properties appear. Examiners love testing this.
> 5. **Rohsenow Surface Constant:** Confusing $C_{sf}$ (surface-fluid parameter, depends on combination) with material constants. Different surface–fluid pairs have very different $C_{sf}$ values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Condensation** = vapor → liquid heat release; **Boiling** = liquid → vapor heat absorption.
- [x] Film condensation dominates industrially; dropwise is rare in practice due to durability issues.
- [x] **Nusselt Equation (Vertical Plate):** $\bar{h} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho_l^2 g k_l^3 h_{fg}}{\mu_l (T_{sat}-T_s) L}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Nusselt Equation (Horizontal Tube):** $\bar{h} = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho_l^2 g k_l^3 h_{fg}}{\mu_l (T_{sat}-T_s) D}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Vertical Tier of N Tubes:** $\bar{h}_N = \bar{h}_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$
- [x] **Film Thickness Profile:** $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$ (increases slowly with vertical distance).
- [x] **Modified Latent Heat** $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 c_{p,l}(T_{sat}-T_s)$ accounts for subcooled condensate.
- [x] **Pool Boiling Regimes:** 6 regimes — natural conv → nucleate (A, B, C) → transition → film → with $q_{max}$ (CHF) at the peak and $q_{min}$ (Leidenfrost) at the minimum.
- [x] **Zuber's CHF (1959):** $q_{max} = 0.149 h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{1/4}$ — **independent of surface material.**
- [x] **Berenson's MHF (1961):** $q_{min} = 0.09 h_{fg} \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2}\right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Rohsenow (1952):** $q = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_e}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n}\right]^3$
- [x] **Bromley Film Boiling:** Same form as Nusselt condensation but use **vapor** properties; horizontal tube prefactor 0.725.
- [x] Order of $h$: Dropwise $\gg$ Nucleate Boiling $\gg$ Film Condensation $\gg$ Film Boiling $\gg$ Natural Convection.
- [x] **CHF Burnout** is a critical design constraint in nuclear reactors, once-through boilers, and cryogenic systems.
- [x] Evaluate liquid properties at **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat}+T_s)/2$ — not at $T_s$ or $T_{sat}$ alone.
- [x] Critical bubble radius (departure): $R_d \propto \sqrt{\sigma / [g(\rho_l-\rho_v)]}$.
- [x] Jakob's empirical correlation: $q = C_{sf} \Delta T_e^n$ with $n \approx 3$ for water nucleate boiling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation: Concept of Thermal Radiation - Black Body Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiation: Concept of Thermal Radiation - Black Body Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by a body in the form of electromagnetic waves (or photons) as a direct consequence of its temperature. It is one of the three primary modes of heat transfer, alongside conduction and convection. Every body whose temperature exceeds **absolute zero (0 K or −273.15 °C)** continuously emits thermal radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermal radiation is the ONLY mode of heat transfer that does NOT require a material medium. Radiation can propagate through vacuum (e.g., the Sun's heat reaching the Earth across 150 million km of empty space).

The **electromagnetic spectrum** spans an enormous range of wavelengths. Thermal radiation occupies the band roughly from **0.1 µm to 100 µm**, comprising:

- **Ultraviolet (UV) region:** $\lambda < 0.4 \, \mu m$
- **Visible light region:** $0.4 \, \mu m \le \lambda \le 0.7 \, \mu m$
- **Infrared (IR) region:** $0.7 \, \mu m < \lambda \le 100 \, \mu m$ (the dominant band for most engineering heat-transfer problems)

$$c \;=\; \lambda \nu \;=\; 2.998 \times 10^{8} \; \frac{m}{s}$$

where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum, $\lambda$ is wavelength, and $\nu$ is frequency.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Glow of a Hot Iron Rod**
>
> Imagine holding a metal rod over a flame. At room temperature, you cannot "see" any energy leaving it, but a sensitive infrared camera would detect emission. As it heats, it first feels warm (low-temperature infrared radiation), then glows dull red (visible red wavelengths appear near **800 K**), then orange, then white-hot. The hotter it gets, the more total energy it radiates and the shorter the dominant wavelength.
>
> A **Black Body** is the theoretical "perfect radiator." Think of a tiny hole drilled into a large hollow cavity whose inner walls are perfectly absorbing. Any radiation entering the hole bounces around inside, getting absorbed repeatedly, with virtually zero chance of escaping. Conversely, the radiation that *does* emanate from that tiny hole is the maximum possible at that temperature — the benchmark against which all real surfaces are compared.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bold Emphasis)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$ |
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^{8}}$ | $\mathrm{m \cdot s^{-1}}$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$ | $\mathrm{J \cdot s}$ |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ | $\mathrm{J \cdot K^{-1}}$ |

---

### 1.4 Volumetric/Surface Radiation Properties — Foundational Terminology

- **Emissive Power (E):** Total radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit time ($\mathrm{W/m^2}$).
- **Monochromatic (Spectral) Emissive Power, $E_\lambda$:** Emissive power at a specific wavelength $\lambda$.
- **Intensity of Radiation (I):** Radiation emitted per unit area per unit time per unit solid angle ($\mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot sr}$).
- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the body. $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.
- **Reflectivity ($\rho$):** Fraction of incident radiation reflected. $0 \le \rho \le 1$.
- **Transmissivity ($\tau$):** Fraction of incident radiation transmitted. $0 \le \tau \le 1$.

For any real surface, energy conservation demands:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau \;=\; 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an **opaque surface**, $\tau = 0$, simplifying the relation to $\alpha + \rho = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of Thermal Radiation (Step-by-Step Logic)

Thermal radiation is unique compared to conduction and convection. The following enumerated logic decomposes its character:

1. **It is electromagnetic in nature.** It propagates as transverse electromagnetic waves and exhibits wave-particle duality (photon model).
2. **No medium is required.** Unlike conduction/convection, radiation travels perfectly through vacuum.
3. **It travels at the speed of light.** All thermal radiation moves at $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum.
4. **It propagates in straight lines.** Reflects and refracts at interfaces following optical laws.
5. **It obeys the inverse-square law.** Intensity falls off as $1/r^2$ from a point source.
6. **It carries energy and momentum.** Radiation pressure is measurable (used in solar sails, Crookes radiometer).
7. **Wavelength depends on temperature.** Wien's displacement law: hotter bodies emit shorter peak wavelengths.

---

### 2.2 The Black Body Concept — Deep Dive

A **Black Body** (German: *schwarzer Körper*) is defined as a perfect absorber and perfect emitter of thermal radiation. It is an *idealized* body used as a reference standard.

**Defining criteria of a Black Body:**

- Absorptivity $\alpha_b = 1$ at **all** wavelengths, **all** directions, **all** temperatures.
- Emissivity $\varepsilon_b = 1$ (defined in §2.4).
- Transmits and reflects **no** radiation: $\tau_b = \rho_b = 0$.
- Radiation emitted is **diffuse** (uniform in all directions from the surface) and follows deterministic laws.

**Physical Realization (Cavity Radiation):**

A practical approximation is a hollow enclosure with a small aperture. Incoming radiation undergoes countless reflections and absorptions inside, while the small hole behaves as a perfect black-body emitter. Furnaces, solar collectors, and high-temperature calibration sources are built on this principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *cavity* matters, not the material. Even a cavity made of polished gold (a great reflector!) acts as a black body if its internal geometry forces multiple absorptions.

---

### 2.3 Emissivity — The Real-Body Correction Factor

**Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** is the ratio of emissive power of a real surface to that of a black body at the same temperature:

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{E}{E_b} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$$

**Two variants:**

- **Total (Hemispherical) Emissivity** $\varepsilon$: integrated over all wavelengths and directions.
- **Spectral (Monochromatic) Emissivity** $\varepsilon_\lambda$: at a single wavelength $\lambda$.

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}$$

**Classification of Real Surfaces:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Black body | $\varepsilon = 1$ | Ideal cavity |
| Gray body | $\varepsilon_\lambda =$ constant $<1$ | Most engineering metals roughly |
| Selective / Real body | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varies strongly with $\lambda$ | Polished aluminum, glass |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation / Law | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Total black-body emissive power |
| 2 | Planck's Distribution | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1\right)}$ | Spectral emissive power of a black body |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \mathrm{m \cdot K}$ | Peak emission wavelength |
| 4 | Energy balance at surface | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Conservation of incident radiation |
| 5 | Opaque surface condition | $\alpha + \rho = 1$ | When $\tau = 0$ |
| 6 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | At thermal equilibrium |
| 7 | Total Kirchhoff | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (for gray body) | Special case |
| 8 | Emissivity definition | $\varepsilon = E / E_b$ | Real-to-black ratio |
| 9 | Lambert's Cosine Law | $I_{\theta} = I_{n} \cos\theta$ | Diffuse surface intensity variation |
| 10 | Relation $E$ and $I$ | $E = \pi I$ | For diffuse surfaces |
| 11 | Intensity-radiance link | $I = \dfrac{q}{\pi A}$ | Heat flux from intensity |
| 12 | Lambert-Beer transmission | $I = I_0 e^{-\kappa x}$ | Absorption in participating media |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize equations **1, 3, 6, 8, 10** — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

The black-body concept is **not** an academic abstraction; it is a working tool in:

- **Furnace & Boiler Design:** Sizing combustion chambers and radiative heat-transfer zones.
- **Solar Thermal Engineering:** Predicting solar collector performance using solar black-body spectrum (~$5778$ K).
- **Aerospace / Re-entry:** Thermal protection tiles rated by emissivity to dissipate re-entry heat.
- **Cryogenic Insulation:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) uses low-emissivity coated films to *suppress* radiation.
- **Infrared Thermography:** Body temperature measurement using emitted IR radiation (fever screening, building audits).
- **Pyrometry:** Non-contact temperature measurement relies on Planck's law inversion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stefan-Boltzmann Law ($E_b = \sigma T^4$)

We derive the relationship between total black-body emissive power and absolute temperature using the **Planck distribution** integrated over all wavelengths.

**Step 1 — Start with Planck's spectral emissive power:**

$$E_{b\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left(e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Total emissive power is the integral over all wavelengths:**

$$E_b(T) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and define a dimensionless variable** $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$, which gives $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{k_B T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{x^{2}} dx$:

$$E_b(T) \;=\; \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\left(\frac{hc}{x k_B T}\right)^{5} \left(e^{x} - 1\right)} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{k_B T}\right) \frac{dx}{x^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the algebra by collecting temperature powers:**

The numerator has $h c^{2} \cdot h c = h^{2} c^{3}$; the denominator has $(hc)^{5} / (x^{5} (k_B T)^{5}) \cdot x^{2}$, plus the minus flips the bounds:

$$E_b(T) \;=\; \frac{2 \pi k_B^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the standard integral** using Riemann zeta function:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx \;=\; \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 6 — Assemble and identify the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**

$$E_b(T) \;=\; \frac{2 \pi^{5} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4} \;\equiv\; \sigma T^{4}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical check:** Plugging the constants yields

$$\sigma \;=\; \frac{2 \pi^{5} \times (1.381\times 10^{-23})^{4}}{15 \times (6.626\times 10^{-34})^{3} \times (2.998\times 10^{8})^{2}} \;\approx\; 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \mathrm{W \, m^{-2} \, K^{-4}} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **gold standard** for KTU derivations — often asked as "Prove Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law" (14-mark question in Module 1).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Kirchhoff's Law ($\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$)

**Step 1 — Consider two large parallel infinite surfaces** in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$:

- Surface 1 (real): emissive power $E_\lambda$, absorptivity $\alpha_\lambda$.
- Surface 2 (black body): emissive power $E_{b\lambda}$, absorptivity $\alpha_{b\lambda} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Energy balance at equilibrium** (no net heat flow between surfaces):

$$E_\lambda \;=\; \alpha_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that** $E_\lambda / E_{b\lambda} \equiv \varepsilon_\lambda$ by definition of emissivity:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \;=\; \alpha_\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Generalize:** This equality holds for every wavelength, every direction, and at the same temperature. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T, \theta, \phi) \;=\; \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T, \theta, \phi)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Pitfall:** Kirchhoff's law applies strictly at *thermal equilibrium* and for bodies whose emission and absorption are at the *same* wavelength and direction. In non-equilibrium radiative transfer (e.g., a solar collector facing the Sun), this simple identity does *not* apply directly.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship $E = \pi I$ for a Diffuse Surface

**Step 1 — Definition of intensity of radiation $I$:** Radiative energy emitted per unit time, per unit projected area, per unit solid angle. For a diffuse surface, $I$ is independent of direction.

**Step 2 — Emissive power $E$ from a small area $dA$** integrated over the hemisphere:

$$E \;=\; \int_{\text{hemisphere}} I \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — In spherical coordinates** $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$:

$$E \;=\; \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate** (since $I$ is constant for diffuse surface):

$$E \;=\; I \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \;=\; I \cdot 2\pi \cdot \left[\frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi/2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute limits:**

$$E \;=\; I \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} \;=\; \pi I$$

$$\boxed{E \;=\; \pi I \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad I \;=\; \frac{E}{\pi}}$$

This **Lambert's Cosine Law** is fundamental to all view-factor calculations.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Black Body | Real (Gray) Body | Real (Selective) Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absorptivity $\alpha$ | $= 1$ (all $\lambda$) | $< 1$ (constant) | $< 1$ (varies with $\lambda$) |
| Emissivity $\varepsilon$ | $= 1$ | $< 1$ (constant) | $< 1$ (varies with $\lambda$) |
| Reflectivity $\rho$ | $= 0$ | $= 1 - \varepsilon$ | $= 1 - \varepsilon_\lambda$ |
| Transmissivity $\tau$ | $= 0$ | $= 0$ (opaque) | $= 0$ (opaque) |
| Color appearance | "Black" at all $\lambda$ | Matte gray | Colored (e.g., red brick) |
| Example | Cavity aperture | Asbestos, oxidized metals | Polished gold, white paint |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum & Thermal Radiation Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electromagnetic Spectrum] --> B[Gamma Rays]
    A --> C[X Rays]
    A --> D[Ultraviolet]
    A --> E[Visible Light]
    A --> F[Infrared]
    A --> G[Microwaves]
    A --> H[Radar and Radio]
    F --> F1[Near IR 0.7 to 1.5 micrometer]
    F --> F2[Middle IR 1.5 to 5.6 micrometer]
    F --> F3[Far IR 5.6 to 100 micrometer]
    E --> E1[Violet 0.4 micrometer]
    E --> E2[Blue Green Yellow Orange]
    E --> E3[Red 0.7 micrometer]
    F3 -.-> Z[Thermal Radiation Band]
    F2 -.-> Z
```

---

### 4.2 Black Body Cavity Realization — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAV[Isothermal Cavity Walls at Temperature T]
        W1[Inner Wall 1] -->|Absorb and Re emit| W2[Inner Wall 2]
        W2 -->|Absorb and Re emit| W3[Inner Wall 3]
        W3 -->|Absorb and Re emit| W1
    end
    IN[Incident Radiation Enters Small Hole] --> CAV
    OUT[Black Body Radiation Exits Hole] --> EXT[External Observer Sees Perfect Emitter]
    CAV --> OUT
    EXT --> CAL[Calibration Source Pyrometry Furnace Solar Simulator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The block diagram makes clear that the *small hole* — not the cavity material — is the actual black body. This is why cavity black bodies can be made from any material, even highly reflective ones.

---

### 4.3 Radiation Heat Transfer Pathway — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Heat Source at Temperature Ts] --> EMIT[Emit Photons across Spectrum]
    EMIT --> TRAV[Travel Through Vacuum or Medium]
    TRAV --> INC[Strike Receiving Surface]
    INC --> ABS[A fraction alpha absorbed]
    INC --> REF[A fraction rho reflected]
    INC --> TRN[A fraction tau transmitted]
    ABS --> HEAT[Convert to Thermal Energy in Body]
    REF --> BACK[Back to Environment or Another Surface]
    TRN --> THROUGH[Pass Through to Other Side]
```

---

### 4.4 Black Body Radiation Laws — Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Plancks Spectral Law Most Fundamental] --> S[Stefans Law E equals sigma T4 by integration]
    P --> W[Wiens Displacement Law lambda max T equals constant by differentiation]
    P --> R[Reyleigh Jeans Law Limit for long wavelength]
    S --> K[Kirchhoffs Law epsilon equals alpha at equilibrium]
    K --> G[Gray Body Approximation epsilon constant]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q1. Define the term "Black Body" and state any three of its characteristic properties.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand | CO1)*

**Model Answer:**
A black body is an idealized physical body that absorbs **all** incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of wavelength, direction, or temperature. It is a perfect absorber and consequently a perfect emitter of thermal radiation.

**Characteristic properties:**
1. Absorptivity $\alpha_b = 1$ at all wavelengths and temperatures.
2. Emissivity $\varepsilon_b = 1$ (maximum emissive power at a given $T$).
3. Transmits and reflects no radiation: $\tau_b = \rho_b = 0$.
4. Radiation emitted is diffuse (obeys Lambert's cosine law).
5. Its spectrum is described exactly by Planck's law.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q2. State Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation. Under what conditions is it valid?**
*(Cognitive Level: Understand | CO1)*

**Model Answer:**
Kirchhoff's Law states that, for a body in thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity equals the monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength, direction, and temperature:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T, \theta, \phi) \;=\; \alpha_\lambda(\lambda, T, \theta, \phi)$$

**Validity conditions:**
1. The body must be in **thermal equilibrium** with its surroundings.
2. The wavelength and direction considered must be the **same** for emission and absorption.
3. For a **gray body** (constant $\varepsilon_\lambda$), the integrated form $\varepsilon = \alpha$ holds.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (Choice 1)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]`**

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to thermal radiation: **emissive power, intensity of radiation, absorptivity, emissivity, and black body.** State the energy balance equation for an opaque surface.
**(7 Marks)** *(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand | CO1)*

**(b)** A small hole of diameter 5 cm in a large furnace acts as a black body. The furnace interior is at 1500 K. Calculate:
- (i) the total emissive power of the black body,
- (ii) the spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu m$, and
- (iii) the wavelength of maximum emission using Wien's displacement law.
**(7 Marks)** *(Cognitive Level: Apply / Analyze | CO2)*

---

##### ⓐ Model Solution to Q(a)

**Definitions (5 × 1 mark = 5 marks):**

1. **Emissive Power ($E$):** The total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area per unit time, integrated over all wavelengths. Units: $\mathrm{W/m^2}$.

2. **Intensity of Radiation ($I$):** The radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time **per unit solid angle** in a specified direction. For a diffuse surface, related by $E = \pi I$. Units: $\mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot sr}$.
