\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dA} = \frac{dT_h}{dA} - \frac{dT_c}{dA}
= -\frac{U\,\Delta T}{C_h} + \frac{U\,\Delta T}{C_c}
= -U\,\Delta T\!\left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Separate and integrate

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\!\left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right) dA
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right)
$$

For counter flow, take $C_h = C_{\max}$, $C_c = C_{\min}$ (this is the configuration that **maximises** effectiveness):

$$
\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{1}{C_{\max}} - \frac{1}{C_{\min}} = \frac{C_{\min} - C_{\max}}{C_{\min}\,C_{\max}} = -\frac{1 - C^*}{C_{\min}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = \text{NTU}\,(1 - C^*)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} = \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]
$$

### Step 4 — Express $\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1$ in terms of $\varepsilon$

For counter flow, the hot stream is at the **right** of the diagram (inlet) and the cold stream enters at the **right** with $T_{c,\text{in}}$ and exits at the **left** with $T_{c,\text{out}}$. We get:

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}, \qquad
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}
$$

Following analogous algebra to the parallel case but accounting for the swapped outlet locations:

$$
\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon\,C^*}
$$

### Step 5 — Equate and solve

$$
\frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon\,C^*} = \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon\,C^*}\right) = \text{NTU}(1 - C^*)
$$

Inverting:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NTU}_{\text{counter}} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon\,C^*}{1 - \varepsilon}\right]}{1 - C^*}\;}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ gives the equivalent form:

$$
\text{NTU}_{\text{counter}} = \frac{-\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon\,C^*}\right]}{1 - C^*}
$$

For $C^* = 1$, the indeterminate form yields (via L'Hôpital's rule):

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{counter}}(C^*=1) = \frac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{\;\text{NTU} = \frac{\varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}\quad(\text{when }C^*=1)\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Counter-Flow Exchanger)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A counter-flow heat exchanger has $UA = 12\,000$ W/K. Hot oil ($C_h = 6000$ W/K) enters at $200\,°C$; cold water ($C_c = 3000$ W/K) enters at $30\,°C$. Find the heat-transfer rate and the outlet temperatures.

**Step 1 — Identify $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$:**

$$
C_{\min} = 3000 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_{\max} = 6000 \text{ W/K}, \quad C^* = \frac{3000}{6000} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NTU:**

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \frac{12\,000}{3000} = 4.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effectiveness using the counter-flow formula:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]}{1 - C^* \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]}
$$

The argument of the exponential is $-4.0 \times 0.5 = -2.0$, so $e^{-2} = 0.1353$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.1353}{1 - 0.5 \times 0.1353} = \frac{0.8647}{1 - 0.0677} = \frac{0.8647}{0.9323} = 0.9275
$$

**Step 4 — Heat duty:**

$$
Q = \varepsilon\,C_{\min}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 0.9275 \times 3000 \times (200 - 30) = 0.9275 \times 3000 \times 170
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 473\,025 \text{ W} \approx 473 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 5 — Outlet temperatures:**

$$
T_{h,\text{out}} = T_{h,\text{in}} - \frac{Q}{C_h} = 200 - \frac{473\,025}{6000} = 200 - 78.84 = 121.2\,°C
$$

$$
T_{c,\text{out}} = T_{c,\text{in}} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{473\,025}{3000} = 30 + 157.7 = 187.7\,°C
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check (verify $\Delta T$ ratio):**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 200 - 187.7 = 12.3\,°C
$$
$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 121.2 - 30 = 91.2\,°C
$$
$$
\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} = \frac{91.2}{12.3} = 7.41 \approx e^{2.00} = 7.39 \quad \checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The $\varepsilon$–NTU Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Heat Exchanger Problem] --> B[Identify Stream Inlet Temperatures Th in and Tc in]
    B --> C[Compute Heat Capacity Rates Ch and Cc from m dot cp]
    C --> D{Determine Cmin and Cmax}
    D --> E[Compute Heat Capacity Ratio C star equals Cmin over Cmax]
    E --> F[Compute NTU equals UA over Cmin]
    F --> G{Identify Flow Configuration}
    G --> G1[Parallel Flow]
    G --> G2[Counter Flow]
    G --> G3[1-2 Shell and Tube]
    G --> G4[Cross Flow Variant]
    G1 --> H[Apply epsilon NTU Formula to get epsilon]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Compute Q actual equals epsilon times Cmin times delta T inlet]
    I --> J[Compute Outlet Temperatures Th out and Tc out]
    J --> K[Verify Energy Balance Qh equals Qc]
    K --> L[END Report Q, Th out, Tc out, epsilon]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — NTU as a Heat Exchanger "Sizing Index"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        U[Overall U]
        A[Area A]
        Cmin[Min Heat Capacity Rate Cmin]
        Cmax[Max Heat Capacity Rate Cmax]
    end
    subgraph DERIVED["DERIVED DIMENSIONLESS GROUPS"]
        NTU[NTU equals UA over Cmin]
        Cstar[C star equals Cmin over Cmax]
    end
    subgraph CORRELATION["CONFIGURATION SPECIFIC CORRELATION"]
        F[epsilon equals f of NTU, C star, configuration]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["ENGINEERING OUTPUT"]
        EPS[Effectiveness epsilon]
        Q[Heat Duty Q]
        T[Outlet Temperatures]
    end
    U --> NTU
    A --> NTU
    Cmin --> NTU
    Cmin --> Cstar
    Cmax --> Cstar
    NTU --> F
    Cstar --> F
    F --> EPS
    EPS --> Q
    Q --> T
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — LMTD Method vs $\varepsilon$–NTU Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Choose Heat Exchanger Analysis Method] --> P1{Known Quantities}
    P1 -- Known Area A, Unknown Outlet T --> M1[Use epsilon NTU Method]
    P1 -- Known Outlet T, Unknown Area A --> M2[Use LMTD Method]
    M1 --> M1a[Compute Cmin, Cmax, C star]
    M1a --> M1b[Compute NTU equals UA over Cmin]
    M1b --> M1c[Look up or compute epsilon from correlation]
    M1c --> M1d[Q equals epsilon Cmin delta T inlet]
    M2 --> M2a[Compute LMTD from inlet outlet T]
    M2a --> M2b[Compute Q equals U A LMTD F]
    M2b --> M2c[Solve for Unknown A]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how both methods converge on the same heat duty $Q$ and outlet temperatures, but each is computationally optimised for a different class of problem. The $\varepsilon$–NTU method is the **rate-first, design-second** approach and is the one KTU 2024 Module 2 emphasises.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the Number of Transfer Units (NTU) of a heat exchanger. Briefly explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is a dimensionless parameter that measures the **thermal size** of a heat exchanger, defined as:

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}
$$

where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is the heat transfer area, and $C_{\min}$ is the minimum heat capacity rate of the two fluid streams.

**Physical Significance:**
- NTU represents the **ratio of the conductance (UA)** to the **limiting thermal capacity rate (Cmin)** of the flowing streams.
- It is an index of the heat exchanger's ability to **transfer thermal energy** relative to the smaller stream's "carrying capacity."
- A **high NTU** means the exchanger is "long and well-conducting" — it can drive the temperature difference between the streams close to zero at the outlet, giving high effectiveness.
- A **low NTU** means the exchanger is "small and resistive" — only a small fraction of the available thermal energy can be exchanged.
- In essence, NTU quantifies the **number of elementary heat-transfer stages** in series, each contributing one "transfer unit" of temperature change to the limiting stream. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the effectiveness of a heat exchanger. How is it related to NTU?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effectiveness $\varepsilon$** of a heat exchanger is the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate $Q$ to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate** $Q_{\max}$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{Q}{C_{\min}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}
$$

It lies in the range $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$.

**Relation to NTU:**
The effectiveness is a function of NTU, the heat capacity ratio $C^*$, and the flow configuration. The general form is:

$$
\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C^*, \text{configuration})
$$

For example, for a **counter-flow** exchanger:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]}{1 - C^* \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*)\big]}
$$

As NTU → ∞, the limiting effectiveness $\varepsilon \to 1$ for the counter-flow configuration, while for the parallel-flow configuration it asymptotically approaches $1/(1+C^*)$. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the $\varepsilon$–NTU relationship for a parallel-flow heat exchanger.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the differential heat balance in a slice $dA$ of the exchanger:

$$
dQ = U(T_h - T_c)\,dA = -C_h\,dT_h = C_c\,dT_c
$$

Hence:

$$
\frac{dT_h}{dA} = -\frac{U(T_h - T_c)}{C_h}, \qquad
\frac{dT_c}{dA} = \frac{U(T_h - T_c)}{C_c}
$$

For parallel flow, the hot fluid has the higher capacity rate. Set $C_h = C_{\max}$ and $C_c = C_{\min}$. Define $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ and $C^* = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$:

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dA} = -\frac{U\,\Delta T}{C_{\min}}\,(1 + C^*)
$$

Integrating from $A = 0$ (inlet, $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$) to $A$ (outlet, $\Delta T_2$):

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -\text{NTU}\,(1 + C^*)
$$

Now express $\Delta T_2$ using the effectiveness definition. With $\varepsilon = (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})/(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$:

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = \Delta T_1\big[1 - \varepsilon(1 + C^*)\big]
$$

Equating and rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1 + C^*)\big]}{1 + C^*}\;}
$$

This is the required $\varepsilon$–NTU relationship. *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) In a parallel-flow heat exchanger used to cool engine oil, hot oil enters at $T_{h,\text{in}} = 150\,°C$ with $C_h = 4000$ W/K, and cooling water enters at $T_{c,\text{in}} = 30\,°C$ with $C_c = 2000$ W/K. If $UA = 6000$ W/K, determine the heat-transfer rate and the outlet temperatures.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Cmin and Cmax:**

$$
C_{\min} = 2000 \text{ W/K (cooling water)}, \quad C_{\max} = 4000 \text{ W/K (oil)}
$$
$$
C^* = \frac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} = \frac{2000}{4000} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NTU:**

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \frac{6000}{2000} = 3.0
$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness from parallel-flow formula:**

Argument: $-\text{NTU}(1+C^*) = -3.0 \times 1.5 = -4.5$, and $e^{-4.5} = 0.01111$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.01111}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{0.98889}{1.5} = 0.6593
$$

**Step 4 — Heat-transfer rate:**

$$
Q = \varepsilon\,C_{\min}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 0.6593 \times 2000 \times (150 - 30)
$$
$$
Q = 0.6593 \times 2000 \times 120 = 158\,232 \text{ W} \approx 158.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Outlet temperatures:**

$$
T_{h,\text{out}} = 150 - \frac{158\,232}{4000} = 150 - 39.56 = 110.4\,°C
$$
$$
T_{c,\text{out}} = 30 + \frac{158\,232}{2000} = 30 + 79.12 = 109.1\,°C
$$

**Step 6 — Verify energy balance:** $Q = C_c(T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 2000 \times (109.1 - 30) = 158\,200$ W. Match within rounding. ✓

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 158.2 \text{ kW}, \quad T_{h,\text{out}} \approx 110.4\,°C, \quad T_{c,\text{out}} \approx 109.1\,°C}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare the LMTD method and the $\varepsilon$–NTU method of heat-exchanger analysis. Under what circumstances is the $\varepsilon$–NTU method preferred?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LMTD Method | $\varepsilon$–NTU Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting point** | Log-mean temperature difference | Effectiveness (dimensionless) |
| **Primary unknown** | Area $A$ (design problem) or $Q$ (rating) | Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ |
| **Best suited for** | **Design problem** — area unknown, inlet/outlet temperatures known | **Rating problem** — area known, outlet temperatures unknown |
| **Correction factor** | Requires $F$-factor for non-ideal configurations | Built into the $\varepsilon$–NTU correlation per configuration |
| **Mathematical form** | $Q = U A \,F\,\text{LMTD}$ | $Q = \varepsilon\,C_{\min}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ |
| **Iteration needed** | Yes, if both $A$ and outlet $T$ unknown | No, straightforward plug-and-chug |
| **Data requirement** | All four inlet/outlet temperatures | Only inlet temperatures + $UA$ + capacity rates |

**Circumstances when $\varepsilon$–NTU is preferred:**

1. **Rating problem:** When the heat-exchanger area is known and outlet temperatures must be computed (typical of HVAC, refrigeration, and operating-plant situations).
2. **Comparison of different configurations:** Effectiveness charts (or formulae) for parallel, counter, cross-flow, and shell-and-tube geometries allow rapid graphical comparison.
3. **Iterative design:** When the design must be repeated for many sets of operating conditions, the $\varepsilon$–NTU method avoids recalculation of LMTD each time.
4. **Compact and plate heat exchangers:** These have complex flow paths where LMTD is poorly defined; $\varepsilon$–NTU is the universal rating method.
5. **Variable-area problems** (e.g., design of an economiser): NTU is a direct measure of size; doubling area approximately doubles NTU, so the design effect is transparent.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> - Students often confuse which stream is $C_{\min}$ and which is $C_{\max}$. Always label the streams explicitly: **whichever stream has the smaller $\dot{m}\,c_p$ is $C_{\min}$**.
> - Forgetting the configuration-specific formula: writing the counter-flow formula for a parallel-flow problem (or vice versa) costs **all 7 marks** of the derivation part.
> - Misapplying the $C^*=1$ special case to the general formula yields an indeterminate form 0/0. Always handle $C^*=1$ separately or use the simplified form.
> - Inversion errors: students mix up $\ln[(1-\varepsilon C^*)/(1-\varepsilon)]$ with $\ln[(1-\varepsilon)/(1-\varepsilon C^*)]$. The sign of the logarithm matters!
> - Unit mismatches: temperatures in °C and ΔT calculations are fine because ΔT is the same in K and °C, but the final $Q$ must be in **watts**, not kW unless the question asks.

---

**(b) A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to condense a chemical vapour. The condensing stream has $C_h = 5000$ W/K and enters at $180\,°C$. The cooling water has $C_c = 4000$ W/K and enters at $25\,°C$. If $UA = 16\,000$ W/K, calculate (i) the effectiveness, (ii) the heat-transfer rate, and (iii) the outlet temperatures.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Cmin and Cmax:**

$$
C_{\min} = 4000 \text{ W/K (water)}, \quad C_{\max} = 5000 \text{ W/K (vapour)}
$$
$$
C^* = \frac{4000}{5000} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NTU:**

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \frac{16\,000}{4000} = 4.0
$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness (counter flow, $C^* = 0.8 \neq 1$):**

Compute the exponent: $-\text{NTU}(1 - C^*) = -4.0 \times 0.2 = -0.8$, so $e^{-0.8} = 0.4493$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.4493}{1 - 0.8 \times 0.4493} = \frac{0.5507}{1 - 0.3595} = \frac{0.5507}{0.6405} = 0.8597
$$

**Step 4 — Heat-transfer rate:**

$$
Q = \varepsilon\,C_{\min}\,(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 0.8597 \times 4000 \times (180 - 25)
$$
$$
Q = 0.8597 \times 4000 \times 155 = 533\,014 \text{ W} \approx 533.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Outlet temperatures:**

$$
T_{h,\text{out}} = 180 - \frac{533\,014}{5000} = 180 - 106.6 = 73.4\,°C
$$
$$
T_{c,\text{out}} = 25 + \frac{533\,014}{4000} = 25 + 133.3 = 158.3\,°C
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** $\Delta T_1 = 180 - 158.3 = 21.7\,°C$, $\Delta T_2 = 73.4 - 25 = 48.4\,°C$. The ratio $48.4/21.7 = 2.23$ should equal $e^{0.8} = 2.225$ ✓

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.860, \quad Q \approx 533.0 \text{ kW}, \quad T_{h,\text{out}} \approx 73.4\,°C, \quad T_{c,\text{out}} \approx 158.3\,°C}
$$

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for NTU (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** $\text{NTU} = UA / C_{\min}$ — dimensionless thermal "size" of an exchanger.
- **Heat Capacity Ratio:** $C^* = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$, with $0 < C^* \leq 1$.
- **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = Q / Q_{\max} = Q / [C_{\min}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})]$.
- **Universal relation:** $\varepsilon = f(\text{NTU}, C^*, \text{flow configuration})$.
- **Counter-flow** is the most effective configuration; it can in principle reach $\varepsilon = 1$ (infinite NTU).
- **Parallel-flow** is bounded by $\varepsilon \leq 1/(1 + C^*)$ no matter how large NTU becomes — a critical difference from counter flow.
- **Always identify $C_{\min}$ correctly:** the stream with the **smaller** $\dot{m}\,c_p$.
- **Master the three canonical formulas:** parallel flow, counter flow (general), counter flow ($C^*=1$ special case).
- **Inverted form** (finding NTU from a known $\varepsilon$): very useful for design problems where target outlet temperatures are specified.
- **LMTD vs $\varepsilon$–NTU:** LMTD for *design* (area unknown); $\varepsilon$–NTU for *rating* (area known, outlet $T$ unknown).
- **Cross-flow and shell-and-tube** configurations are usually read from charts, though closed-form approximations exist.
- **Mass transfer analogue:** the same NTU framework applies to gas absorption, drying, extraction, and humidification — with $K\,a\,V/L$ replacing $UA/C_{\min}$.
- **Limiting behaviour:** as $\text{NTU} \to \infty$, $\varepsilon \to 1$ for counter flow; $\varepsilon \to 1/(1+C^*)$ for parallel flow.
- **Energy-balance check** must always be performed: $Q = C_h(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) = C_c(T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$.
- **Use the correct log form** in the inverted formula: $\ln[(1-\varepsilon C^*)/(1-\varepsilon)]$ is the counter-flow NTU expression.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 KTU Marks-Loss Triggers for NTU Problems:**
> 1. Swapping the hot and cold streams when assigning $C_h$ vs $C_c$ — this flips $C_{\min}/C_{\max}$ and ruins the effectiveness.
> 2. Using the parallel-flow formula in a counter-flow problem (or vice versa) — full-marks loss on the derivation.
> 3. Forgetting the $C^*=1$ special case and getting a 0/0 indeterminate form — examiners specifically look for the correct handling of this case.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporators: Types of Evaporation - Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporator, Natural and Forced Circulation Evaporator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporators: Types of Evaporation - Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporator, Natural and Forced Circulation Evaporator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Evaporation (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> Evaporation is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (usually water) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute (such as salt, sugar, or caustic solution) by supplying heat through a heating surface, thereby concentrating the solution. The equipment in which this operation is carried out is called an **Evaporator**.

In the context of chemical engineering, an evaporator is essentially a **heat exchanger** combined with a **vapor-liquid separator (vapor body)**, where the heat supplied is used to vaporize the solvent rather than to raise the temperature of the bulk liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2, the focus is restricted to **classification of evaporators** based on (a) **Number of Effects** (Single vs. Multiple Effect) and (b) **Mode of Circulation** (Natural vs. Forced Circulation). Detailed design calculations using boiling point elevation and Duhring's rule are covered in the numerical problems section.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Tea-Kettle" Analogy:**
> Imagine boiling water in a tea-kettle on a stove. The flame heats the kettle's bottom (the heating surface), water turns to steam (vapor) and escapes, while any salt, sugar, or tea residue stays behind and becomes more concentrated. **A single-effect evaporator is essentially an industrial-scale, closed tea-kettle** with vapor capture. A **multiple-effect evaporator** is like using the steam leaving one kettle to heat a second kettle — a brilliant "heat recycling" trick that dramatically reduces fuel consumption.

### 1.3 Industrial Significance

Evaporation is one of the largest energy-consuming operations in process industries. Typical applications include:

- **Sugar industry:** Concentrating cane juice from ~15% to ~70% solids.
- **Dairy industry:** Concentrating milk to produce condensed milk.
- **Caustic soda production:** Concentrating NaOH solution.
- **Desalination:** Producing fresh water from seawater.
- **Pulp & paper:** Concentrating black liquor.

### 1.4 Primary Classification of Evaporators

Evaporators are classified along two independent axes in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **By Number of Effects** | Single Effect | Multiple Effect |
| **By Mode of Circulation** | Natural Circulation | Forced Circulation |

> [!NOTE]
> These two classifications are **orthogonal** (independent). For example, a multiple-effect evaporator may employ either natural or forced circulation. Both axes directly affect the **Capacity (kg/hr of water vaporized)** and the **Steam Economy (kg water evaporated / kg steam supplied)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Single Effect Evaporator — Operating Principle

A **single-effect evaporator** consists of three essential components:

1. **Heating Element / Calandria:** A bundle of tubes (steam side and solution side) through which saturated steam condenses on one side and the feed solution boils on the other.
2. **Vapor Body (Separator):** An empty chamber where the vapor-liquid separation occurs; vapor rises to the top, while concentrated liquid (called *thick liquor* or *concentrated liquor*) falls to the bottom.
3. **Condenser (optional):** If the vapor is not recovered, it is passed through a condenser to create a vacuum and improve the boiling-point depression.

**Mass Balance (overall and solute):**
Let $F$ = feed flow rate (kg/hr), $V$ = water vaporized (kg/hr), $L$ = concentrated liquor (kg/hr), and the subscripts $w$ and $s$ denote water and solute respectively.

$$
F = L + V
$$

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

Combining the two:

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

**Energy Balance (assuming feed enters at boiling point):**
The latent heat supplied by the steam is essentially equal to the latent heat required to vaporize water (since no sensible heating of feed is required and concentrated liquor leaves at nearly the boiling point):

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda
$$

$$
\frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_S}{\lambda}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = mass flow rate of steam (kg/hr)
- $\lambda_S$ = latent heat of steam (kJ/kg)
- $\lambda$ = latent heat of vapor at operating pressure (kJ/kg)
- $V/S$ = **Steam Economy** (a dimensionless performance index)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:**
> For a single-effect evaporator with feed at boiling point, the theoretical maximum steam economy is **1.0** (approximately, since $\lambda_S \approx \lambda$ for low-pressure operation). This limitation is the primary motivation for using multiple-effect evaporators.

### 2.2 Multiple Effect Evaporator — Operating Principle

A **multiple-effect evaporator** uses the **vapor generated in one effect (called the previous effect)** as the **heating medium for the next effect**. The pressure (and therefore the boiling point) is progressively reduced from the first effect to the last effect, allowing spontaneous vapor flow and successive evaporation without additional steam input (except in the first effect).

#### 2.2.1 Feed Configurations (KTU-High-Yield Topic)

There are **three standard feed arrangements** in industrial multiple-effect evaporators:

**a) Forward Feed (Parallel Feed):**
- Feed and steam flow in the **same direction** (Feed: Effect 1 → 2 → 3; Steam: Effect 1 → 2 → 3).
- Pressure is highest in Effect 1 and lowest in the last effect.
- **Advantage:** Simple operation, no pumps required between effects (pressure gradient drives the flow).
- **Disadvantage:** Cold feed enters the hottest effect — the most viscous (already concentrated) liquor is in the **coldest** effect, where heat transfer coefficients are lowest.

**b) Backward Feed:**
- Feed flows **opposite** to the steam (Feed: Effect 3 → 2 → 1; Steam: Effect 1 → 2 → 3).
- The most concentrated liquor is in the **hottest effect** (Effect 1), where heat transfer coefficients are higher → best for viscous liquids.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires pumps between effects because feed must flow from low pressure to high pressure.

**c) Parallel Feed:**
- Feed is split and supplied **equally** to all effects simultaneously.
- Used when the feed is already at saturation temperature or when continuous crystallized product is required.

#### 2.2.2 Capacity and Economy of Multiple Effect

**Capacity** of an evaporator = total kg of water evaporated per unit time.

$$
\text{Capacity (multiple)} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \dots + V_n
$$

**Economy** = total water evaporated / total steam supplied.

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n}{S}
$$

For an ideal $n$-effect evaporator (equal heat transfer area, equal $\lambda$, no losses):

$$
\text{Economy (theoretical max)} = n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:**
> In actual operation, due to boiling point elevation (BPE), sensible heating requirements, and heat losses, the practical economy is approximately **0.7n to 0.85n** rather than the theoretical $n$.

### 2.3 Natural Circulation Evaporators

In **natural (thermosiphon) circulation**, the movement of the liquid through the tubes is driven by the **density difference** between the cold liquid entering the tube and the hot liquid-vapor mixture leaving it. No external pump is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Driving Force Equation:**
> $$\Delta P_{\text{driving}} = (\rho_L - \rho_{LV}) \cdot g \cdot H$$
> Where $H$ is the tube height, $\rho_L$ is the density of liquid entering, and $\rho_{LV}$ is the density of the two-phase mixture leaving.

The natural circulation velocity is typically **0.3 to 1.5 m/s**, which is sufficient for non-viscous, non-scaling liquids.

#### Common Types of Natural Circulation Evaporators (KTU 2024 Syllabus):

| Type | Tube Length | Key Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-Tube (Calandria / Standard Vertical-Tube)** | 1 to 2 m | Short tubes with central downtake | Sugar, salt, general-purpose |
| **Long-Tube (Kestner / Climbing Film)** | 6 to 10 m | Thin film rises rapidly inside tube | Heat-sensitive materials (juice, milk) |
| **Basket-Type (Suspended Basket)** | 1 m | Heating element suspended like a basket | Viscous liquids, easy cleaning |

### 2.4 Forced Circulation Evaporator

In a **forced circulation evaporator**, a **mechanical pump** is used to circulate the liquid at high velocity (typically **2 to 6 m/s**) through the tubes. The circulation is independent of the density-difference driving force.

**Schematic Components:**
- **Steam chest (calandria)**
- **Circulation pump** (axial flow or centrifugal)
- **Vapor-liquid separator** (often external, to avoid entrainment)
- **Return line** for unvaporized liquid

**Why is Forced Circulation Needed?**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Confusing Point:**
> Forced circulation is **NOT** about increasing the evaporation rate per se. Its primary purpose is to:
> 1. **Prevent scaling and fouling** on tube walls by maintaining high velocity.
> 2. **Handle viscous and crystallizing solutions** where natural circulation velocity would be too low.
> 3. **Allow operation with very low temperature differences** (ΔT) — useful when the heating medium has limited temperature availability.

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher capital cost (pump, piping).
- Higher operating cost (pump power).
- Risk of mechanical failure of the pump.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exams (Module 2)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = L + V$ | Overall mass balance | kg/hr |
| 2 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Solute mass balance | kg solute/hr |
| 3 | $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$ | Water evaporated | kg/hr |
| 4 | $S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda$ | Energy balance (feed at B.P.) | kJ/hr |
| 5 | $V/S = \lambda_S / \lambda$ | Steam economy (single effect) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Economy}_n \approx 0.8n$ | Practical economy of $n$ effects | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$ | Heat transfer area | m² |
| 8 | $\text{Capacity} = \sum V_i$ | Total evaporation rate | kg/hr |
| 9 | $\Delta P = (\rho_L - \rho_{LV}) g H$ | Natural circulation driving force | Pa |
| 10 | $\text{BPE} = T_b^{solution} - T_b^{water}$ | Boiling point elevation | K or °C |

> [!NOTE]
> When a formula contains an absolute value or conditional (e.g., $|T_1 - T_2|$), use `\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert` or `\mid T_1 - T_2 \mid` inside the markdown table to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam economy** is the single most important economic parameter. Switching from single-effect to triple-effect reduces steam consumption by ~60–65%, directly cutting fuel costs.
- **Forced circulation** is mandatory in **sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄)** evaporation (severe scaling) and in **black liquor concentration** in the paper industry (highly viscous, crystallizing).
- **Backward feed** is the industry standard for **caustic soda** and **glycerol** concentration due to high viscosity at high concentration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material and Energy Balance of Single Effect Evaporator

**Problem Setup:**
A feed solution containing 10% (by mass) NaOH enters a single-effect evaporator at its boiling point. It is concentrated to 40% (by mass). The evaporator uses saturated steam at 3 bar (gauge). Saturated pressure inside the evaporator body is 0.2 bar (absolute). Determine:
(a) The kg of water evaporated per 1000 kg of feed.
(b) The kg of steam required, assuming no heat losses.
(c) The steam economy.

**Given Data:**
- $F = 1000$ kg/hr
- $x_F = 0.10$ (mass fraction)
- $x_L = 0.40$ (mass fraction)
- $S$ at 3 bar gauge (≈ 4 bar abs): $\lambda_S = 2133$ kJ/kg (from steam tables)
- Vapor at 0.2 bar abs: $\lambda = 2358$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Solute Mass Balance (solute is non-volatile)**

The solute (NaOH) entering must equal the solute leaving:

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

Substituting values:

$$
1000 \times 0.10 = L \times 0.40
$$

Solving for $L$:

$$
L = \frac{100}{0.40} = 250 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2: Overall Mass Balance**

$$
F = L + V
$$

$$
V = F - L = 1000 - 250 = 750 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

This directly answers part (a). **Answer (a): 750 kg of water evaporated per 1000 kg of feed.**

**Step 3: Energy Balance**

Since the feed enters at the boiling point and the concentrated liquor leaves at approximately the same boiling point (no sensible heating or cooling), the heat supplied by the condensing steam is utilized solely in vaporizing water:

$$
Q = S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda
$$

Substituting known values:

$$
S \times 2133 = 750 \times 2358
$$

Solving for $S$:

$$
S = \frac{750 \times 2358}{2133}
$$

Calculating the numerator:

$$
750 \times 2358 = 1{,}768{,}500 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Dividing by $\lambda_S$:

$$
S = \frac{1{,}768{,}500}{2133} \approx 829.1 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Answer (b): Approximately 829.1 kg of steam is required per hour.**

**Step 4: Steam Economy**

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{750}{829.1} \approx 0.905
$$

**Answer (c): The steam economy is approximately 0.905 (i.e., about 0.905 kg of water is evaporated per kg of steam).**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacity and Economy of a Triple-Effect Evaporator

**Problem Setup:**
A triple-effect forward-feed evaporator concentrates a salt solution from 5% to 25%. The feed rate is 10,000 kg/hr. The steam economy is known to be 2.4 (practical). Determine:
(a) Total capacity of the evaporator system.
(b) Total steam consumption.
(c) Steam economy per effect (if equal evaporation is assumed).

**Given:**
- $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr
- $x_F = 0.05$, $x_L = 0.25$
- $n = 3$
- Economy $E = 2.4$

**Step 1: Total Water Evaporated (Capacity)**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{0.25}\right)
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 10{,}000 \times 0.80
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 8{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Answer (a): Total capacity = 8,000 kg/hr of water evaporated.**

**Step 2: Total Steam Required**

$$
E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S}
$$

$$
S = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{E} = \frac{8{,}000}{2.4} \approx 3{,}333.3 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Answer (b): Total steam consumption ≈ 3,333 kg/hr.**

**Step 3: Evaporation per Effect (assuming equal heat transfer area and equal $\Delta T$)**

If the system is balanced, the heat transferred is approximately equal in each effect. Assuming $\lambda$ is similar across effects:

$$
V_1 \approx V_2 \approx V_3 = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{3} = \frac{8{,}000}{3} \approx 2{,}666.7 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Transfer Area Calculation (Boiling Point Elevation Included)

**Problem Setup:**
Determine the heating surface area of a single-effect evaporator handling 5000 kg/hr of feed (5% solids) to concentrate to 30% solids. Feed enters at 30°C. Steam is at 110°C (latent heat = 2230 kJ/kg). The solution boils at 105°C. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 1500$ W/(m²·K). Specific heat of feed $C_p = 3.9$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 1: Calculate $V$ and $L$**

$$
V = 5000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.05}{0.30}\right) = 5000 \times 0.8333 = 4{,}166.7 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

$$
L = 5000 - 4166.7 = 833.3 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2: Sensible Heat Required (feed at 30°C, must be heated to 105°C)**

$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_f = 5000 \times 3.9 \times (105 - 30)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = 5000 \times 3.9 \times 75 = 1{,}462{,}500 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 3: Latent Heat Required for Vaporization**

$$
Q_{\text{latent}} = V \cdot \lambda = 4166.7 \times \lambda_{\text{vapor at } 105°C}
$$

Assume $\lambda = 2250$ kJ/kg (approximate):

$$
Q_{\text{latent}} = 4166.7 \times 2250 = 9{,}375{,}075 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 4: Total Heat Duty**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} = 1{,}462{,}500 + 9{,}375{,}075 = 10{,}837{,}575 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Converting to watts (1 kJ/hr = 0.2778 W):

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 10{,}837{,}575 \times 0.2778 \approx 3{,}010{,}654 \text{ W} \approx 3.01 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5: Steam Required**

$$
S = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\lambda_S} = \frac{10{,}837{,}575}{2230} \approx 4{,}860 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 6: Area Calculation**

$\Delta T$ (driving force) = Steam temperature − Boiling temperature of solution:

$$
\Delta T = 110 - 105 = 5 \text{ °C} = 5 \text{ K}
$$

Using $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{3{,}010{,}654}{1500 \times 5}
$$

$$
A = \frac{3{,}010{,}654}{7{,}500} \approx 401.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Answer: The required heat transfer area is approximately 401.4 m².**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Single Effect | Multiple Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Steam supply | Required in every effect | Required only in 1st effect |
| Steam economy | ≈ 0.8 – 0.9 | ≈ 0.7n – 0.85n |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Operating cost | High (more steam) | Low (steam recycled) |
| Operating pressure | Single pressure | Stepped pressure |
| Floor space | Small | Large |
| Vacuum pump capacity | Small | Large (only last effect) |

| Parameter | Natural Circulation | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Density difference | Mechanical pump |
| Liquid velocity | 0.3 – 1.5 m/s | 2 – 6 m/s |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Power consumption | None (auxiliary) | Significant (pump) |
| Suitable for | Non-viscous, non-scaling liquids | Viscous, scaling, crystallizing liquids |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Moderate | High |
| Tube length limitation | Practically no limit (long-tube) | Limited by pump head |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Single Effect Evaporator Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank F xF] --> B[Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Steam Heated Calandria]
    S[Steam Supply S lambdaS] --> C
    C --> D[Vapor Body Separator]
    D --> E[Concentrated Liquor L xL]
    D --> F[Water Vapor V]
    F --> G[Condenser Vacuum System]
    G --> H[Non condensable vent]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The feed from tank `A` is pumped by `B` into the steam-heated calandria `C`, where the saturated steam `S` supplies the latent heat of vaporization. The two-phase mixture enters the separator `D`, where concentrated liquor `E` is withdrawn from the bottom and vapor `F` rises to the condenser `G`, with non-condensable gases vented at `H`.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Multiple Effect Evaporator (Forward Feed, 3 Effects)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed F xF] --> E1[Effect 1 Highest Pressure]
    S[Steam S lambdaS] --> E1
    E1 --> V1[V1 to Effect 2 as heating medium]
    E1 --> L1[Liquid L1 to Effect 2]
    V1 --> E2[Effect 2 Intermediate Pressure]
    L1 --> E2
    E2 --> V2[V2 to Effect 3]
    E2 --> L2[Liquid L2 to Effect 3]
    V2 --> E3[Effect 3 Lowest Pressure]
    L2 --> E3
    E3 --> P[Final Concentrate L]
    E3 --> C[Condenser Vacuum System]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Backward Feed Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed F] --> E3[Effect 3 Lowest Pressure]
    S[Steam] --> E1[Effect 1 Highest Pressure]
    E1 --> V1[V1 to Effect 2]
    E1 --> L1[Liquid to Effect 2 via pump]
    L1p[Pump 1] --> E2
    V1 --> E2[Effect 2]
    E2 --> V2[V2 to Effect 3]
    E2 --> L2[Liquid to Effect 3 via pump]
    L2p[Pump 2] --> E3
    V2 --> E3
    E3 --> L[Final Concentrate]
    E3 --> C[Condenser]
```

**Note:** Pumps (L1p, L2p) are mandatory in backward feed because the liquid must flow from a lower-pressure effect to a higher-pressure effect.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Forced Circulation Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Tank] --> P[Axial Flow Circulation Pump]
    P --> C[Steam Heated Calandria]
    S[Steam Supply] --> C
    C --> S1[External Vapor Liquid Separator]
    S1 --> V[Vapor to Condenser or Next Effect]
    S1 --> R[Recirculation Line back to pump]
    L[Concentrate Withdrawal] --> S1
```

**Key feature:** The recirculation loop from separator `S1` back to the pump `P` allows the unvaporized liquid to be circulated multiple times through the calandria, maintaining high velocity and preventing tube fouling.

### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: Natural vs. Forced Circulation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NAT[NATURAL CIRCULATION THERMOSIPHON]
        N1[Liquid in downtake] --> N2[Tube inlet low density]
        N2 --> N3[Boiling inside tube]
        N3 --> N4[Liquid vapor mixture rises]
        N4 --> N5[Separator]
        N5 --> N6[Density difference drives flow]
        N6 --> N1
    end

    subgraph FOR[FORCED CIRCULATION]
        F1[Sump or separator] --> Fp[Mechanical Pump]
        Fp --> F2[High velocity into calandria]
        F2 --> F3[External separator]
        F3 --> F4[Recirculation to pump]
        F4 --> Fp
        F3 --> FW[Concentrate Withdrawal]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term "Steam Economy" of an evaporator. What is the theoretical maximum steam economy of a triple-effect evaporator?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Steam Economy is defined as the ratio of the mass of water evaporated to the mass of steam consumed in an evaporator:

$$
\text{Steam Economy} = \frac{\text{kg of water vaporized}}{\text{kg of steam supplied}}
$$

For an ideal $n$-effect evaporator with no heat losses, no boiling point elevation, and feed at boiling point, the theoretical maximum steam economy equals $n$. Thus, for a **triple-effect** evaporator, the theoretical maximum steam economy is **3.0**. In practice, due to boiling point elevation and heat losses, it ranges between **2.1 and 2.55**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between natural circulation and forced circulation evaporators. Give any two industrial applications where forced circulation is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Natural Circulation | Forced Circulation |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Density difference between liquid and vapor-liquid mixture | Mechanical pump |
| Liquid velocity | 0.3 – 1.5 m/s | 2 – 6 m/s |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Moderate | High |
| Capital cost | Low | High |

**Two industrial applications of forced circulation evaporators:**
1. **Concentration of black liquor** in the paper industry (highly viscous and prone to scaling).
2. **Evaporation of sodium sulfate (Glauber's salt) solutions** (severe scaling and crystallization on tube walls).
3. (Optional) Caustic soda concentration in the chlor-alkali industry.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **single-effect evaporator**. Derive the material and energy balance equations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single-effect evaporator concentrates 5000 kg/hr of a 10% NaOH solution to 50% solids. The feed enters at 30°C. The steam is supplied at 1.5 bar (gauge) and the evaporator operates at atmospheric pressure. Determine:
- (i) Capacity of the evaporator
- (ii) Steam consumption (kg/hr)
- (iii) Steam economy

Take $C_p$ of feed = 3.8 kJ/(kg·K); boiling point of solution at 1 atm ≈ 105°C; $\lambda_S$ at 2.5 bar abs ≈ 2180 kJ/kg; $\lambda$ at 1 atm ≈ 2257 kJ/kg. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to Part (a) — Working Principle and Derivations:**

A single-effect evaporator consists of (1) a heating element (calandria) where saturated steam condenses on one side of the tubes and the feed solution boils on the other; (2) a vapor body where the liquid and vapor are separated; and (3) optionally, a condenser and vacuum system.

**Material Balance:**
Let $F$ = feed rate (kg/hr), $V$ = water evaporated (kg/hr), $L$ = concentrated liquor (kg/hr).

Overall balance:

$$
F = L + V \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

Solute (non-volatile) balance:

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Solving (1) and (2):

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

$$
L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} \quad \text{...(4)}
$$

**Energy Balance:**
Heat supplied by steam = Sensible heat to raise feed to boiling point + Latent heat to vaporize water + Sensible heat to raise liquor to boiling point + Heat losses

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda + L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot (T_L - T_b) + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

For the simplified case (feed at boiling point, no losses, liquor leaves at boiling point):

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda \quad \text{...(5)}
$$

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $F = 5000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.10$, $x_L = 0.50$, $T_F = 30°C$, $T_b = 105°C$, $C_p = 3.8$ kJ/(kg·K), $\lambda_S = 2180$ kJ/kg, $\lambda = 2257$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Capacity:**
$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right)
$$
$$
V = 5000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 5000 \times 0.80 = 4000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**(ii) Steam Consumption:**

Sensible heat required:

$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_b - T_F) = 5000 \times 3.8 \times (105 - 30)
$$
$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = 5000 \times 3.8 \times 75 = 1{,}425{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Latent heat required:

$$
Q_{\text{latent}} = V \cdot \lambda = 4000 \times 2257 = 9{,}028{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Total heat duty:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 1{,}425{,}000 + 9{,}028{,}000 = 10{,}453{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Steam consumption:

$$
S = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\lambda_S} = \frac{10{,}453{,}000}{2180} \approx 4794.5 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**(iii) Steam Economy:**

$$
E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{4000}{4794.5} \approx 0.834
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Do **NOT** forget the sensible heat term $(F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T)$ when the feed enters below boiling point — this is a very common mistake. Many students write $S = V \cdot \lambda / \lambda_S$ directly, which is valid **only when feed is at boiling point**.
> 2. Always verify whether steam pressure is **absolute or gauge**. The question says "1.5 bar gauge" → total = 1.5 + 1.0 = 2.5 bar absolute.
> 3. **Significant figures:** Keep at least 3–4 decimal places in intermediate steps to avoid propagation error.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Compare forward feed and backward feed arrangements in a multiple-effect evaporator. State their advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A triple-effect forward-feed evaporator concentrates a sugar solution from 12% to 60% solids. The feed rate is 20,000 kg/hr at 30°C. Saturated steam at 2.5 bar abs is used ($\lambda_S = 2180$ kJ/kg). The boiling temperatures in the three effects are 95°C, 85°C, and 65°C respectively. Assume equal heat transfer area, equal latent heat ($\lambda = 2300$ kJ/kg in all effects), feed $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), and overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 1200$ W/(m²·K). Determine:
- (i) Total capacity and steam economy (assume equal evaporation per effect).
- (ii) Steam consumption per hour.
- (iii) Required heat transfer area per effect. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to Part (a):**

| Feature | Forward Feed | Backward Feed |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Feed & steam in same direction | Feed opposite to steam |
| Pressure profile | Highest in Effect 1 → lowest in Effect n | Same profile |
| Feed temperature | Cold feed enters hottest effect | Cold feed enters coldest effect |
| Most viscous liquor | In coldest effect (lowest U) | In hottest effect (highest U) |
| Pumps required between effects | No (pressure drives flow) | Yes (pumps required) |
| Suitable feed | Cold dilute, non-viscous solutions | Hot viscous or crystallizing solutions |
| Typical applications | Sugar, salt, milk | Caustic soda, glycerine, black liquor |

**Advantages of Forward Feed:** Simple; no pumps needed; low maintenance; lower capital cost.
**Advantages of Backward Feed:** Higher overall heat transfer coefficient due to lower viscosity in hot effects; better capacity utilization for viscous liquids.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $F = 20{,}000$ kg/hr, $x_F = 0.12$, $x_L = 0.60$, $T_F = 30°C$, $T_S = T_{\text{steam saturation at 2.5 bar}} \approx 127.4°C$, $\lambda_S = 2180$ kJ/kg, $\lambda = 2300$ kJ/kg, $C_p = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), $U = 1200$ W/(m²·K).

**(i) Total Capacity:**

$$
V_{\text{total}} = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 20{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.12}{0.60}\right)
$$

$$
V_{\text{total}} = 20{,}000 \times (1 - 0.20) = 20{,}000 \times 0.80 = 16{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Evaporation per effect (equal assumption):**

$$
V_1 = V_2 = V_3 = \frac{16{,}000}{3} \approx 5{,}333.3 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Economy:** For a triple-effect with practical efficiency of ~0.8:

$$
E \approx 0.8 \times 3 = 2.4
$$

**(ii) Steam Consumption:**

Heat supplied in Effect 1 must vaporize $V_1$ water + sensible heat of incoming feed:

$$
Q_1 = F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_1 - T_F) + V_1 \cdot \lambda
$$

$$
Q_1 = 20{,}000 \times 3.5 \times (95 - 30) + 5{,}333.3 \times 2300
$$

$$
Q_1 = 20{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 65 + 12{,}266{,}590
$$

$$
Q_1 = 4{,}550{,}000 + 12{,}266{,}590 = 16{,}816{,}590 \text{ kJ/hr}
$$

Steam consumption:

$$
S = \frac{Q_1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{16{,}816{,}590}{2180} \approx 7{,}714 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Steam economy (calculated):**

$$
E_{\text{actual}} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \frac{16{,}000}{7{,}714} \approx 2.074
$$

**(iii) Heat Transfer Area per Effect:**

Using $Q_1 = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_1$:

$\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{steam}} - T_{b1} = 127.4 - 95 = 32.4°C$

Converting $Q_1$ to W (1 kJ/hr = 0.2778 W):

$$
Q_1 \text{ in W} = 16{,}816{,}590 \times 0.2778 \approx 4{,}672{,}649 \text{ W}
$$

Area of Effect 1:

$$
A_1 = \frac{Q_1}{U \cdot \Delta T_1} = \frac{4{,}672{,}649}{1200 \times 32.4} = \frac{4{,}672{,}649}{38{,}880} \approx 120.2 \text{ m}^2
$$

By the equal-area assumption: $A_1 = A_2 = A_3 \approx 120.2$ m².

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Equal heat transfer area** assumption is a KTU-standard simplification. In rigorous design, areas are iteratively balanced using the $\Delta T_i \propto (V_i \cdot \lambda_i) / U_i$ rule.
> 2. **Boiling temperatures** given are for the solution, **not** for pure water. The ΔT must be calculated using solution boiling point and steam saturation temperature.
> 3. **Don't confuse Economy with Capacity.** Capacity is kg/hr of water removed; Economy is a dimensionless ratio.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Evaporators**

- [ ] **Evaporation** = vaporization of a volatile solvent from a non-volatile solute to concentrate the solution.
- [ ] **Single-effect evaporator** uses steam in only one vessel; max steam economy ≈ 0.8 – 0.9.
- [ ] **Multiple-effect evaporator** reuses vapor from one effect as the heating medium for the next; max theoretical economy = $n$.
- [ ] **Three feed configurations:** Forward, Backward, Parallel. Forward = simple; Backward = best for viscous.
- [ ] **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** reduces the effective ΔT and is the primary reason practical economy < theoretical.
- [ ] **Natural circulation** is driven by density difference (thermosiphon effect); velocity = 0.3 – 1.5 m/s.
- [ ] **Forced circulation** uses a mechanical pump; velocity = 2 – 6 m/s; used for viscous, scaling, or crystallizing liquids.
- [ ] **Common types of natural circulation evaporators:** Short-tube (calandria), Long-tube (Kestner/climbing film), Basket-type.
- [ ] **Key mass balance equation:** $V = F(1 - x_F/x_L)$.
- [ ] **Key energy balance (feed at B.P.):** $S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda$.
- [ ] **Heat transfer area formula:** $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$.
- [ ] **Solute (NaOH, sugar, salt) is non-volatile** — it never appears in the vapor phase; this is the foundation of all evaporator material balances.
- [ ] **Capacity** = total kg of water evaporated per hour across all effects.
- [ ] **Economy** = total water evaporated / total live steam consumed.
- [ ] **Forced circulation** is NOT for increasing evaporation rate per se — it is for **preventing fouling** and **handling difficult fluids**.
- [ ] **Long-tube vertical evaporator (Kestner)** uses climbing-film principle — ideal for **heat-sensitive** materials like fruit juices and milk.
- [ ] **Practical steam economy of $n$ effects** ≈ $0.7n$ to $0.85n$ (NOT $n$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Mass Transfer - Fick's Law of Molecular Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principles of Mass Transfer - Fick's Law of Molecular Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mass Transfer** is defined as the net movement (transport) of a chemical species from a region of higher chemical potential (usually higher concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential, under the influence of a concentration gradient, within a single phase or across phase boundaries. In a homogeneous single phase, this transport is termed **molecular diffusion** (or simply **diffusion**).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, mass transfer by molecular diffusion is described by **Fick's Law**, an empirical law analogous to Fourier's Law of heat conduction and Newton's Law of viscosity. The general statement is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fick's First Law of Molecular Diffusion (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> *"The molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion relative to a fixed coordinate system in a stationary medium is proportional to the gradient of its molar concentration and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration."*

Mathematically (1-D, z-direction):

$$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Where:
- $J_{A,z}$ = molar flux of species A in the z-direction $\left[ \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}} \right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = binary diffusion (or mass diffusivity) coefficient of A in B $\left[ \dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}} \right]$
- $C_A$ = molar concentration of A $\left[ \dfrac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^3} \right]$
- $z$ = direction of diffusion $\left[ \text{m} \right]$
- The negative sign signifies transport **down** the concentration gradient.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you spray **perfume** in one corner of a closed, still room. Initially, the air near the bottle is highly concentrated with perfume molecules. Within a few minutes, the fragrance spreads uniformly across the entire room. Why?

- Individual perfume molecules are in constant, **random thermal motion** (Brownian motion).
- Because there are far more perfume molecules in the corner than the opposite wall, statistically, **more molecules move *away* from the concentrated region than *toward* it**.
- This net statistical drift of molecules from high to low concentration is called **molecular diffusion**.

The rate of this spread is governed by the **diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$**, analogous to thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$) in heat conduction.

| Phenomena | Driving Potential | Transport Property | Governing Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heat Conduction | Temperature gradient | Thermal conductivity ($k$) | Fourier's Law |
| Momentum Transfer | Velocity gradient | Viscosity ($\mu$) | Newton's Law |
| Mass Diffusion | Concentration gradient | Diffusivity ($D_{AB}$) | **Fick's Law** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All three transport phenomena (heat, momentum, mass) are mathematically **analogous** (Reynolds analogy). This is a heavily tested concept in KTU ESE papers.

### 1.3 Modes of Mass Transfer

Mass transfer occurs by **two principal modes**:

1. **Molecular Diffusion** – Caused by random molecular motion; dominant in **stagnant fluids** or within laminar boundary layers. Governed by Fick's Law.
2. **Convective (Eddy) Mass Transfer** – Caused by bulk fluid motion (turbulent eddies); dominant in turbulent flows. Analogous to forced convection in heat transfer.

In this module, we focus exclusively on **molecular diffusion** in binary systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Molecular Diffusion

For binary systems (A + B), molecular diffusion problems are categorised as follows:

- **Type 1: Equimolar Counter-diffusion** ($N_A = -N_B$)
  - Both species diffuse in **opposite directions** at equal molar rates.
  - Classic example: counter-diffusion in Stefan diffusion tube when pure A and pure B meet at an interface (e.g., evaporation of A into stagnant B at equal and opposite rates, or distillation of a binary mixture at constant total moles).

- **Type 2: Diffusion of A through Stagnant B** ($N_B = 0$)
  - Species A diffuses through a **stationary** medium of B.
  - Classic example: evaporation of water (A) into stagnant air (B), absorption of a soluble gas (A) into a liquid containing non-diffusing gas (B).

### 2.2 Molar Flux Reference Frame (Crucial Concept)

Two distinct flux definitions exist in mass transfer:

1. **Molar flux with respect to a fixed coordinate (stationary frame):** $N_A$ [kmol/m²·s]
2. **Molar flux with respect to the average molar velocity of the mixture:** $J_A$ [kmol/m²·s]

The relationship (for binary A–B) is:

$$N_A = J_A + \frac{C_A}{C} (N_A + N_B)$$

Where $C = C_A + C_B$ is the total molar concentration. For **incompressible liquids and gases at constant $T, P$**, total $C$ is approximately constant.

### 2.3 Fick's First Law — Molar Basis

$$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Using $C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{RT}$ (ideal gases) and $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$:

$$J_{A,z} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \, \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$

### 2.4 Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-State Diffusion)

For non-steady (time-dependent) diffusion with constant $D_{AB}$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\;}$$

This is the **one-dimensional** form; for 3-D Cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right)$$

### 2.5 Stefan Diffusion (Diffusion through Stagnant Film)

For a gas A diffusing through stagnant gas B, the **steady-state** molar flux is given by the **Stefan equation**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

Where the **log-mean partial pressure of B** is:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise This!**

| S.No. | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fick's First Law (general) | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | $J_A$ in kmol/(m²·s) |
| 2 | Fick's First Law (gas form) | $J_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT} \dfrac{dp_A}{dz}$ | Partial pressure form |
| 3 | Molar flux relationship | $N_A = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)$ | $x_A = C_A / C$ |
| 4 | Equimolar Counter-diffusion | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}$ | Steady-state, $N_A = -N_B$ |
| 5 | Stefan Diffusion (A through stagnant B) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \cdot \dfrac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$ | $\delta = z_2 - z_1$ |
| 6 | Log-mean partial pressure of B | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | Used in Stefan eqn. |
| 7 | Fick's Second Law (1-D, constant $D_{AB}$) | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Unsteady state |
| 8 | Equimolar form (gas) | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{R T (z_2 - z_1)}$ | Pressure form |
| 9 | Total molar concentration (gas) | $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Ideal gas law |
| 10 | Diffusivity dimension | $[D_{AB}] = \text{m}^2 / \text{s}$ | Always L²/T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> - Design of **gas absorption** columns (CO₂ in air, NH₃ in water).
> - **Distillation** trays (equimolar counter-diffusion).
> - **Drying** of solids (moisture transfer by diffusion).
> - **Membrane separation** processes.
> - **Pulmonary** gas exchange (O₂–CO₂ diffusion in lungs).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law

We perform an **unsteady-state mass balance** on a thin element of fluid of thickness $\Delta z$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$ (see schematic in §4).

**Step 1 — Identify all flows into and out of the element.**

- Molar flow of A *entering* the element at $z$: $N_A \big\vert_z \, A_c$
- Molar flow of A *leaving* the element at $z + \Delta z$: $N_A \big\vert_{z+\Delta z} \, A_c$

**Step 2 — Apply the General Mass Balance.**

$$\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

No chemical reaction is assumed (diffusion only):

$$N_A \big\vert_z \, A_c - N_A \big\vert_{z+\Delta z} \, A_c + 0 = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \, A_c \, \Delta z$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\frac{N_A \big\vert_z - N_A \big\vert_{z+\Delta z}}{\Delta z} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $\Delta z \to 0$.**

By the definition of derivative:

$$-\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Fick's First Law** $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$:

$$-\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right) = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}$$

**Step 6 — Assuming constant $D_{AB}$** (a valid assumption for dilute systems and short time scales):

$$\boxed{\;D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Flux Equation

**Assumption:** Steady-state, 1-D, constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas, total pressure and temperature constant.

**Step 1 — Equimolar condition** gives $N_A = -N_B$ (constant magnitudes, opposite directions).

**Step 2 — Start with the general flux equation:**

$$N_A = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Since $N_A = -N_B$, the term $(N_A + N_B) = 0$, therefore $N_A = J_A$.

**Step 3 — Apply Fick's First Law:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — For dilute systems and constant $C$ (ideal gas), $D_{AB}$ is constant.** Rearranging:

$$N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \, dC_A$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $z = z_1$ (where $C_A = C_{A1}$) to $z = z_2$ (where $C_A = C_{A2}$):**

$$N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A$$

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1}) = D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N_A$:**

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1}\;}$$

This is the **Stefan's law of equimolar counter-diffusion**. Using $C_A = p_A / RT$ and $C = P / RT$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{R T (z_2 - z_1)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Stefan Equation: Diffusion of A through Stagnant B

**Assumption:** Steady-state, 1-D, constant $D_{AB}$ and $T$, $P$ constant. B is **stagnant** ($N_B = 0$). No bulk flow in opposite direction.

**Step 1 — General flux equation with $N_B = 0$:**

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} (N_A + 0)$$

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $N_A$:**

$$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

$$N_A \left(\frac{C - C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Since $C_B = C - C_A$:

$$N_A \left(\frac{C_B}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to partial pressures** using $C_A = p_A / RT$ and $C = P / RT$ (ideal gas):

$$N_A \left(\frac{p_B}{P}\right) = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.** Using $p_B = P - p_A$, so $dp_B = -dp_A$. Hence $dp_A = -dp_B$.

$$N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{p_B}$$

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{B1}}^{p_{B2}} \frac{dp_B}{p_B}$$

(Flipping limits and sign.)

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the log-mean partial pressure** of B:

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$

Therefore $\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1}) = (p_{B2} - p_{B1}) / p_{BM}$.

**Step 7 — Final form of the Stefan equation:**

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The presence of $p_{BM}$ in the denominator **reduces** the flux relative to equimolar counter-diffusion. This is because as A diffuses, it must "drag" the stagnant B backward due to the no-net-bulk-flow condition, effectively retarding its own progress. This effect becomes more pronounced when B is dilute (low $p_{BM}$).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Molar Diffusion Element — Mass Balance Geometry

The following Mermaid block diagrams a functional control volume used in the derivation of Fick's Second Law (§3.1), showing the molar flux in/out and the concentration accumulation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Inlet at plane z, flux NA at z times Area] --> node2[Control Volume: thickness delta z, area A]
    node2 --> node3[Outlet at plane z plus delta z, flux NA at z plus delta z times Area]
    node2 -.-> node4[Accumulation: partial CA over partial t times A times delta z]
    node1 -->|minus| node5[Net mass balance equation]
    node3 -->|minus| node5
    node4 -->|equals| node5
    node5 -->|Apply Ficks First Law| node6[Final: DAB times d2CA over dz2 equals dCA over dt]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Binary Molecular Diffusion

The following Mermaid block maps the taxonomy of binary molecular diffusion problems, the governing flux equation for each, and the KTU-relevant physical examples.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Binary Molecular Diffusion of A and B]
    rootA --> typ1[Type 1: Equimolar Counter Diffusion]
    rootA --> typ2[Type 2: Diffusion of A through Stagnant B]
    rootA --> typ3[Type 3: Knudsen Diffusion: very small pores]

    typ1 --> eq1[Condition: NA equals minus NB]
    typ1 --> eq2[Governing Equation: NA equals DAB times CA1 minus CA2 divided by delta z]
    typ1 --> ex1[KTU Example: Distillation of A and B at constant total moles]

    typ2 --> st1[Condition: NB equals zero, B is stagnant]
    typ2 --> st2[Governing Equation: Stefan Equation with pBM in denominator]
    typ2 --> ex2[KTU Example: Water evaporating into stagnant air, NH3 absorbed by water]

    typ3 --> kn1[Applicable when pore diameter less than mean free path of gas molecules]
    kn3[KTU Example: Catalyst pores, membrane separation]

    eq1 --- eq2 --- ex1
    st1 --- st2 --- ex2
```

### 4.3 Steady-State Concentration Profile (Linear Gradient)

The following block diagram shows how Fick's First Law produces a **linear** steady-state concentration profile in a stagnant film (z₁ → z₂), as is assumed in all standard KTU 2-mark and 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Plane z1: Bulk concentration of A is CA1, higher] --> p2[Stagnant Gas Film, thickness delta z equals z2 minus z1]
    p2 --> p3[Plane z2: Bulk concentration of A is CA2, lower]
    p1 -.->|Linear profile: CA decreases| p3
    p2 -.->|Slope equals minus dCA over dz equals NA divided by DAB| p1
    p2 -.->|Steady state assumption: dNA over dz equals zero| p3
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State **Fick's First Law of Molecular Diffusion** and write its mathematical form in (i) molar concentration form, and (ii) partial pressure form. State the units of the diffusion coefficient.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fick's First Law:** *"The molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion in a stationary medium is proportional to the concentration gradient of that species, in the direction of decreasing concentration."*

(i) Molar concentration form:

$$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

(ii) Partial pressure form (for ideal gases, since $C_A = p_A / RT$):

$$J_{A,z} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$

**Units of $D_{AB}$:** $\text{m}^2 / \text{s}$ (always, since it represents a *diffusivity*, i.e., area per unit time).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is **equimolar counter-diffusion**? Give **two industrial examples** where this assumption is valid.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Equimolar counter-diffusion** is a special case of binary molecular diffusion in which the two species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** ($N_A = -N_B$). As a result, there is no net bulk molar flow, and the total molar concentration $C$ is constant throughout the system.

The governing equation is:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{z_2 - z_1} = \frac{D_{AB}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{RT(z_2 - z_1)}$$

**Two industrial examples:**
1. **Distillation of a binary ideal liquid mixture** — for every mole of A vaporising, one mole of B condenses, maintaining equal counter-diffusion.
2. **Counter-diffusion in the Stefan diffusion tube** when a pure gas A meets a pure gas B at an interface (e.g., H₂–N₂ system, O₂–N₂ system).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to use the **log-mean** $p_{BM}$ in the Stefan equation (using arithmetic mean loses 3–4 marks).
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing $J_A$ (relative to mean velocity) with $N_A$ (relative to fixed frame) — this is a **favourite 2-mark KTU trap**.
> - **Mistake 3:** Not stating **assumptions** (constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas, 1-D, steady-state) before applying the formula. Examiners deduct 1 mark per missing assumption.
> - **Mistake 4:** Using **wrong units for $R$** — $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} / (\text{mol}\cdot\text{K})$ when $P$ is in Pa, or $R = 0.0821 \, \text{L·atm} / (\text{mol}\cdot\text{K})$ when $P$ is in atm. Mixing units loses 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the **Stefan equation** for the steady-state molar flux of a gas A diffusing through a stagnant gas B, starting from Fick's First Law. Define each term and clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A tube of length 0.20 m contains a mixture of **H₂ (A) and N₂ (B)** in equimolar counter-diffusion at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and a total pressure of $1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$. The partial pressure of H₂ at one end is $2000 \, \text{Pa}$ and at the other end is $200 \, \text{Pa}$. The binary diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the **molar flux of H₂** in mol/(m²·s). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of Stefan Equation (7 marks):**

Refer to **§3.3** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key points the examiner expects:

1. State the general flux equation: $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + \dfrac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$
2. Apply stagnant B condition $N_B = 0$.
3. Simplify to $N_A \dfrac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$.
4. Convert to partial pressure form.
5. Integrate with limits $z_1 \to z_2$ and $p_{A1} \to p_{A2}$.
6. Introduce $p_{BM}$ to obtain the final Stefan form:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

7. State **assumptions**: steady-state, 1-D, ideal gas, constant $T$ and $P$, constant $D_{AB}$, no chemical reaction.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = z_2 - z_1 = 0.20 \, \text{m}$
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298 \, \text{K}$
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_{A1} = 2000 \, \text{Pa}$, $p_{A2} = 200 \, \text{Pa}$
- $D_{AB} = 7.67 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- Equimolar counter-diffusion ($N_A = -N_B$)

**Step 1 — Use the equimolar counter-diffusion equation (since $N_A = -N_B$):**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{R T (z_2 - z_1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$N_A = \frac{(7.67 \times 10^{-5}) \times (2000 - 200)}{8.314 \times 298 \times 0.20}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$7.67 \times 10^{-5} \times 1800 = 1.3806 \times 10^{-1} \; \text{Pa·m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$8.314 \times 298 \times 0.20 = 495.514 \; \text{Pa·m}^2/(\text{mol·K})\cdot\text{K} \cdot \text{m} \cdot /(\text{s·m}^2)$$

More cleanly: $8.314 \times 298 = 2477.572$; multiplied by $0.20$ gives $495.514 \, \text{Pa·m} \cdot \text{s/mol·m}^2 = 495.514 \, \text{kg/(m·s³)} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s/mol} / \text{m}^2$... we will compute numerically:

$$N_A = \frac{0.13806}{495.514} = 2.786 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mol} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 2.79 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mol} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Define **molar flux with respect to a fixed coordinate** ($N_A$) and **molar flux with respect to the average molar velocity** ($J_A$). Derive the relationship between them for a binary system. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vertical tube, $0.020 \, \text{m}$ long, contains **water vapour (A)** diffusing through **stagnant air (B)** at a total pressure of $1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$ and a temperature of $25\,^\circ\text{C}$. The partial pressure of water vapour at the bottom is $3169 \, \text{Pa}$ and at the top is $0 \, \text{Pa}$. The diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the **molar flux of water vapour** in kmol/(m²·s) using the Stefan equation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Flux Definitions & Relationship (7 marks):**

- $N_A$ — **Molar flux of A relative to a fixed coordinate** (e.g., the wall of a pipe). It includes both the diffusive flux and the **convective (bulk) flux** due to molar-averaged motion of the mixture. Units: kmol/(m²·s).

- $J_A$ — **Molar flux of A relative to the molar-average velocity** of the mixture. It represents **purely diffusive transport** with no bulk-motion contribution. Units: kmol/(m²·s).

**Derivation of the relationship:**

The molar-average velocity of the mixture is:

$$v_M = \frac{C_A v_A + C_B v_B}{C_A + C_B} = \frac{C_A v_A + C_B v_B}{C}$$

The total flux of A relative to a fixed plane is the sum of (i) the diffusive flux relative to $v_M$ and (ii) the convective flux due to bulk motion:

$$N_A = C_A v_A = C_A (v_M + v_A - v_M) = C_A v_M + C_A (v_A - v_M)$$

The first term $C_A v_M$ is the **bulk convective flux**, and the second term is the diffusive flux $J_A$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,N_A = J_A + C_A v_M = J_A + \frac{C_A}{C} (C v_M) = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)\,}$$

**Key observations:**
- If $N_A + N_B = 0$ (equimolar counter-diffusion), then $N_A = J_A$.
- If $N_B = 0$ (stagnant B), then $N_A = J_A / (1 - x_A) = J_A C / C_B$.

**(b) Stefan Diffusion Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\delta = z_2 - z_1 = 0.020 \, \text{m}$
- $T = 298 \, \text{K}$, $P = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$
- $p_{A1} = 3169 \, \text{Pa}$ (bottom), $p_{A2} = 0 \, \text{Pa}$ (top)
- $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $N_B = 0$ (stagnant air)

**Step 1 — Use the Stefan equation:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$:**

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 101300 - 3169 = 98131 \, \text{Pa}$$
$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 101300 - 0 = 101300 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the log-mean partial pressure of B:**

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{101300 - 98131}{\ln(101300/98131)}$$

$$\ln(101300/98131) = \ln(1.03231) = 0.03181$$

$$p_{BM} = \frac{3169}{0.03181} = 99622 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerical value:**

$$N_A = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1.013 \times 10^5)}{8.314 \times 298 \times 0.020} \times \frac{3169 - 0}{99622}$$

Numerator: $2.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 2.6338$

Denominator: $8.314 \times 298 \times 0.020 = 49.55$

$$N_A = \frac{2.6338}{49.55} \times \frac{3169}{99622}$$

$$N_A = 0.05315 \times 0.03182 = 1.691 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 1.69 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}) = 1.69 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{kmol} / (\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fick's Law of Molecular Diffusion**

- **Fick's First Law:** $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ — defines the *flux* as proportional to the *concentration gradient*.
- **Fick's Second Law:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ — applies to **unsteady-state** diffusion (constant $D_{AB}$).
- **Equimolar counter-diffusion** ($N_A = -N_B$): No bulk flow, $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{\delta}$.
- **Stagnant B** ($N_B = 0$): **Stefan equation** with $p_{BM}$ in the denominator — bulk flow *retards* A's diffusion.
- **$J_A$ vs $N_A$**: $J_A$ is *relative to mean molar velocity*; $N_A$ is *relative to a fixed plane*; they differ by the bulk convective term $x_A(N_A + N_B)$.
- **Units to memorise:** $D_{AB}$ in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$; $N_A$ in $\text{kmol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$; $R = 8.314 \, \text{J}/(\text{mol}\cdot\text{K})$.
- **Log-mean $p_{BM}$** is the most-frequently-tested detail in KTU ESE — always use it; never use arithmetic mean.
- **Steady-state** → only Fick's First Law is needed; **unsteady-state** → Fick's Second Law must be used with boundary conditions.
- **Industrial relevance:** Distillation (equimolar), absorption & drying (Stefan), gas–liquid contactors, membrane separation, catalyst pore diffusion.
- **Common KTU traps:** Confusing $J_A$ and $N_A$; forgetting assumptions (steady-state, constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas); mixing SI and CGS units; not computing $p_{BM}$ in Stefan problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> Always start every mass-transfer derivation or numerical problem by writing the **governing equation**, then listing the **assumptions** (1-D, steady/unsteady, constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas, stagnant condition). Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for **clear statement of assumptions** before solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion in Solids and Liquids, Concept of Mass Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diffusion in Solids and Liquids, Concept of Mass Transfer Coefficients ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Molecular Diffusion? **Molecular diffusion** is the transport of mass (individual molecules) through a medium by random molecular motion, caused by a **concentration gradient**. It is the fundamental mechanism by which mass transfer occurs in quiescent (non-flowing) or laminar systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** "Diffusion in Solids" refers to the migration of atoms/molecules through a solid matrix (lattice or amorphous) due to thermal agitation and concentration gradients.
