"Diffusion in Liquids" refers to the relative motion of species in a liquid phase in the absence of bulk convective flow, governed by molecular collisions and concentration gradients. ### 1.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (The Governing Equation) The mathematical backbone of diffusion is **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, which states that the molar flux of a diffusing species is proportional to its concentration gradient: $$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ Where: - $J_A^*$ = Molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s) - $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusion coefficient or mass diffusivity of A in B (m²/s) - $\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ = Concentration gradient of A in the z-direction (kmol/m⁴) - The **negative sign** indicates that mass flows from high to low concentration. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant Note:** The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ typically ranges from **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s for gases**, **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s for liquids**, and **$10^{-10}$ to $10^{-14}$ m²/s for solids**. The dramatic decrease in magnitude explains why diffusion in solids is extremely slow. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Ink Drop in Water:** Imagine dropping a single drop of **red ink** into a still glass of clear water. Initially, the ink forms a concentrated red blob. Over time, even without stirring, the red color spreads throughout the glass until the water becomes uniformly pink. This happens because random thermal motion of water molecules constantly collides with ink molecules, gradually dispersing them from high to low concentration regions. This spontaneous spreading is **diffusion**. The "driving force" is the concentration gradient, and the "resistance" is determined by the medium (water = low resistance; ice = high resistance; solid metal = very high resistance). ### 1.4 Classification of Diffusion Systems | Type | Description | Example | |------|-------------|---------| | **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion** | Two species A and B diffuse in opposite directions at equal molar rates ($N_A = -N_B$) | Distillation column (ideal) | | **Diffusion of A through Stagnant B** | Species A diffuses, while B is non-diffusing ($N_B = 0$). This is **Stefan Diffusion** | Liquid evaporation into stagnant air | | **Steady-State Diffusion** | Concentration profile does not change with time ($\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = 0$) | Drying of solid slab (long time) | | **Unsteady-State Diffusion** | Concentration varies with both position and time | Moisture absorption in food | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Diffusion in Solids — The Underlying Physics Diffusion in solids occurs via three primary mechanisms: 1. **Vacancy Diffusion:** Atoms move into adjacent vacant lattice sites. This is the dominant mechanism in crystalline solids (metals, ionic crystals). 2. **Interstitial Diffusion:** Small atoms (like H, C, N, O) move through the interstitial spaces between host atoms. This is much faster than vacancy diffusion. 3. **Knudsen Diffusion:** In porous solids with very small pores, molecules collide more with pore walls than with each other. Mean free path becomes comparable to pore diameter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** For steady-state diffusion through a solid slab of thickness $z_2 - z_1$, with surface concentrations $C_{A1}$ and $C_{A2}$, the flux is constant and given by Fick's law in integrated form. Always remember: **in solids, we usually work with partial pressures or mole fractions** rather than concentrations. ### 2.2 Steady-State Diffusion Through a Solid Slab (Planar Geometry) For a solid slab of thickness $L$ with constant surface concentrations, integrating Fick's First Law yields: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$ Where: - $N_A$ = Molar flux of A through the slab (kmol/m²·s) - $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity in the solid (m²/s) - $C_{A1}, C_{A2}$ = Surface concentrations of A (kmol/m³) - $L$ = Thickness of the slab (m) ### 2.3 Unsteady-State Diffusion in Solids (Fick's Second Law) When diffusion in solids is time-dependent, we use **Fick's Second Law**: $$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$ For semi-infinite medium with constant surface concentration $C_{A,s}$, the solution is: $$\frac{C_A(z,t) - C_{A,0}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,0}} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB}\,t}}\right)$$ Where **erf** is the Gaussian error function, $C_{A,0}$ is the initial bulk concentration. ### 2.4 Diffusion in Liquids — Key Theories Two main theories describe liquid-phase diffusion: - **Stokes-Einstein Equation** (for large spherical molecules in dilute solution): $$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}$$ - **Wilke-Chang Equation** (most common empirical correlation for liquids): $$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} (\phi M_B)^{1/2} T}{\mu_B V_{A}^{0.6}}$$ Where $\phi$ is the association factor, $M_B$ is molecular weight of solvent B, $\mu_B$ is viscosity, $V_A$ is molar volume of solute A at normal boiling point. ### 2.5 Mass Transfer Coefficients — The Concept In practical engineering, the detailed concentration gradient inside a phase is often unknown. We therefore define a **mass transfer coefficient** $k_c$ that relates the flux to a *measurable* driving force (concentration difference): $$N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$ Where: - $k_c$ = Convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $C_{A,s}$ = Concentration of A at the interface - $C_{A,\infty}$ = Concentration of A in the bulk > [!NOTE] > **KTU Critical Concept:** The mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ has units of **velocity (m/s)**. This is conceptually similar to a heat transfer coefficient. It lumps together all the complex molecular diffusion near the interface into a single engineering parameter. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Application | |---|--------------------|---------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Fick's First Law | $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ | kmol/m²·s | All diffusion | | 2 | Steady-state flux in solid | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\Delta C_A}{L}$ | kmol/m²·s | Solid slab | | 3 | Fick's Second Law | $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | kmol/m³·s | Unsteady solids | | 4 | Semi-infinite solid | $\frac{C_A - C_{A,0}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,0}} = \text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Surface absorption | | 5 | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} C_{T}}{z_2 - z_1}\ln\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}$ | kmol/m²·s | Distillation | | 6 | A through stagnant B (Stefan) | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2-z_1)}\ln\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}$ | kmol/m²·s | Evaporation | | 7 | Wilke-Chang | $D_{AB} = \frac{7.4\times10^{-8}(\phi M_B)^{0.5}T}{\mu_B V_A^{0.6}}$ | cm²/s | Liquids | | 8 | Stokes-Einstein | $D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu_B r_A}$ | m²/s | Large molecules | | 9 | Mass transfer flux | $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | kmol/m²·s | Convective | | 10 | Two-film overall | $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$ | dimensionless | Two-phase systems | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion) This is the most important derivation in KTU Module 3. Consider a liquid A evaporating into stagnant gas B (e.g., water evaporating into air). Species A diffuses upward, but air (B) is essentially stationary. **Starting Point — Fick's Law for A relative to stationary coordinates:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ Since B is stagnant, $N_B = 0$ (this is the key assumption). Also, for an ideal gas, $C_A = \frac{p_A}{RT}$ and $C_T = \frac{P}{RT}$. Substituting: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{d}{dz}\left(\frac{p_A}{RT}\right) + \frac{p_A}{P} N_A$$ Rearranging to isolate the differential term: $$N_A - \frac{p_A}{P} N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$ $$N_A \left(\frac{P - p_A}{P}\right) = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$ $$N_A \, dp_A \cdot \frac{1}{P - p_A} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \cdot P \, \frac{dp_A}{dz}$$ Let us integrate across the diffusion path from $z_1$ to $z_2$: $$N_A \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A}$$ $$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \left[\ln(P - p_A)\right]_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}}$$ $$N_A (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \ln\left(\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \ln\left(\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)}$$ The corresponding logarithmic-mean form: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T (z_2 - z_1)} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{P_{BM}}$$ where $P_{BM}$ is the **logarithmic mean of $(P - p_A)$**: $$P_{BM} = \frac{(P - p_{A2}) - (P - p_{A1})}{\ln\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}} = \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{\ln\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion For the case $N_A = -N_B$ (e.g., ideal binary distillation), starting from the general flux equation: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ With $N_A = -N_B$, the second term vanishes, giving pure Fickian diffusion: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ In terms of gas-phase mole fractions $y_A = p_A / P$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT(z_2 - z_1)} \ln\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$$ Equivalently, with $C_{BM}$ as the log-mean of $(C_T - C_A)$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} C_T (y_{A1} - y_{A2})}{(z_2 - z_1) (1 - y_A)_{LM}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Overall Mass Transfer Coefficient (Two-Film Theory) Consider absorption of A from gas phase into liquid phase. Define individual phase coefficients: - Gas phase: $N_A = k_y(y_{AG} - y_{Ai})$ - Liquid phase: $N_A = k_x(x_{Ai} - x_{AL})$ At the interface, equilibrium is established via the relation $y_{Ai} = m\, x_{Ai}$. The overall flux can be written as: $$N_A = K_y(y_{AG} - y_{A}^*)$$ where $y_{A}^*$ is the bulk gas concentration in equilibrium with bulk liquid. Eliminating $y_{Ai}$ and $x_{Ai}$: $$N_A = k_y(y_{AG} - y_{Ai}) = k_x(x_{Ai} - x_{AL})$$ From the equilibrium relation: $y_{Ai} = m x_{Ai}$, so $x_{Ai} = y_{Ai}/m$. $$N_A = k_x\left(\frac{y_{Ai}}{m} - x_{AL}\right)$$ Multiply gas-phase equation by $1/k_y$ and liquid-phase equation by $m/k_x$: $$\frac{N_A}{k_y} = y_{AG} - y_{Ai}$$ $$\frac{m N_A}{k_x} = y_{Ai} - m x_{AL} = y_{Ai} - y_{A}^*$$ Adding both equations: $$\frac{N_A}{k_y} + \frac{m N_A}{k_x} = y_{AG} - y_{A}^*$$ $$N_A \left(\frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}\right) = y_{AG} - y_{A}^*$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}}$$ Similarly, expressed in liquid-phase basis: $$\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m\,k_y}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** The overall resistance $\frac{1}{K_y}$ equals the sum of gas-phase resistance $\frac{1}{k_y}$ plus the liquid-phase resistance $\frac{m}{k_x}$. When $k_x \gg m k_y$ (gas-phase controlling), $K_y \approx k_y$. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A slab of solid 5 mm thick contains moisture that diffuses out into a dry air stream. The surface concentration at $z = 0$ is $C_{A1} = 0.02$ kmol/m³ and at $z = L$ is $C_{A2} = 0.001$ kmol/m³. The diffusivity is $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Calculate the steady-state flux. **Solution:** Given: $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $L = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $C_{A1} = 0.02$ kmol/m³, $C_{A2} = 0.001$ kmol/m³. $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$ $$N_A = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(0.02 - 0.001)}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$N_A = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(0.019)}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 4.56 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}}$$ This is an extremely small flux, consistent with the very low diffusivity of liquids/solids. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Diffusion Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Molecular Diffusion in Phases"] --> B["Diffusion in Gases"] A --> C["Diffusion in Liquids"] A --> D["Diffusion in Solids"] B --> B1["Equimolar Counter-Diffusion N_A = -N_B"] B --> B2["Diffusion of A through Stagnant B Stefan Diffusion N_B = 0"] C --> C1["Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Distillation"] C --> C2["A through stagnant B Absorption Stripping"] C --> C3["Stokes-Einstein Theory Large Molecules"] C --> C4["Wilke-Chang Correlation General Liquids"] D --> D1["Vacancy Diffusion Crystalline Lattice"] D --> D2["Interstitial Diffusion Small Atoms in Metals"] D --> D3["Knudsen Diffusion Porous Solids"] D --> D4["Steady State Fick First Law"] D --> D5["Unsteady State Fick Second Law"] B2 --> S1["N_A = D_AB P / R T L x ln((P-p_A2)/(P-p_A1))"] C2 --> S1 D4 --> S1 style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FF9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#99CCFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#99FF99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style S1 fill:#FFCC00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two-Film Mass Transfer Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GAS["GAS PHASE BULK y_AG"] G1[Concentration y_AG] -->|k_y Gas Film| IF[INTERFACE y_Ai m x_Ai] end IF -->|k_x Liquid Film| L1[Concentration x_AL] subgraph LIQ["LIQUID PHASE BULK x_AL"] IF -->|Mass Transfer| L1 end G1 -.Equilibrium.-> E1["y_A star m x_AL"] L1 -.Equilibrium.-> E1 E1 --> OA["Overall Driving Force y_AG - y_A star"] OA --> OK["Overall Coefficient K_y"] OK --> RES["1 / K_y = 1 / k_y + m / k_x"] style GAS fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000 style LIQ fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B style IF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style RES fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: Stefan Diffusion Physical Picture ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Z1 as Surface at z1 participant MID as Bulk Diffusion Path participant Z2 as Surface at z2 Note over Z1: A enters with conc C_A1
B is stagnant N_B = 0 Z1->>MID: Molecules of A move via random motion MID->>Z2: A travels by molecular diffusion Note over Z2: A leaves at conc C_A2
partial pressure p_A2 Z2-->>MID: Backward flux of A governed by D_AB MID-->>Z1: Net flux N_A computed Note over MID: N_A = D_AB x P / R T L x ln((P-p_A2)/(P-p_A1)) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the significance of the negative sign with a neat sketch. **Model Answer:** Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the molar flux of a diffusing species A through a medium is directly proportional to the concentration gradient of A and the binary diffusivity: $$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Significance of negative sign:** Mass always flows from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration (i.e., down the concentration gradient). Since the gradient $\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ is positive when concentration increases in the z-direction, a negative sign is required to make the flux $J_A^*$ positive in the direction of decreasing concentration. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion | Diffusion of A through Stagnant B | |---------|----------------------------|-----------------------------------| | Condition | $N_A = -N_B$ (equal and opposite) | $N_B = 0$ (B is non-diffusing) | | Net molar flux | Zero (sum of fluxes = 0) | Non-zero (bulk flow accompanies A) | | Example | Binary distillation at constant molal overflow | Liquid evaporation into still air | | Equation | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}C_T}{L}\ln\frac{1-y_{A2}}{1-y_{A1}}$ | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RTL}\ln\frac{P-p_{A2}}{P-p_{A1}}$ | | Bulk flow term | Zero | Non-zero, requires correction | ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B, the most common mistake is forgetting to convert **partial pressures to mole fractions** or vice versa in the Stefan diffusion equation. The total pressure $P$ and partial pressure $p_A$ must be in **consistent units** (both in atm or both in Pa). Marks are deducted for unit inconsistencies and missing $\ln$ terms. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the expression for steady-state molar flux of A diffusing through stagnant B for a gas mixture at constant temperature and pressure. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above. Key steps to write: 1. Start with $N_A = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ 2. Apply the stagnant condition $N_B = 0$ 3. Convert $C_A = p_A/RT$ and $C_T = P/RT$ 4. Rearrange: $N_A(P - p_A) = -\frac{D_{AB}P}{RT}\frac{dp_A}{dz}$ 5. Integrate from $z_1$ to $z_2$ with $p_{A1}$ to $p_{A2}$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT(z_2 - z_1)} \ln\left(\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$$ 6. Define $P_{BM} = \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{\ln\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}}$ and rewrite the final form. **(b)** A trough contains liquid water at 25°C exposed to dry air. Water evaporates and diffuses through a 2 mm thick stagnant air film. The total pressure is 1 atm. Vapor pressure of water at 25°C is 23.76 mm Hg. The diffusivity of water vapor in air is $2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the rate of evaporation per m² of water surface. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** $P = 1$ atm $= 760$ mm Hg, $p_{A1} = 23.76$ mm Hg (at the water surface), $p_{A2} = 0$ (dry air above), $D_{AB} = 2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $T = 298$ K, $z_2 - z_1 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **Step 1:** Apply Stefan equation: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}P}{RT(z_2 - z_1)} \ln\left(\frac{P - p_{A2}}{P - p_{A1}}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Convert pressure to SI units. $P = 101325$ Pa, $P - p_{A1} = 101325 - 3168 = 98157$ Pa, $P - p_{A2} = 101325$ Pa. **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$N_A = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(298)(2 \times 10^{-3})} \ln\left(\frac{101325}{98157}\right)$$ $$N_A = \frac{2.634}{4.955} \times \ln(1.0323)$$ $$N_A = 0.5316 \times 0.03180$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 0.0169 \text{ mol/m}^2\cdot\text{s} = 1.69 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the two-film theory of mass transfer. Derive the relation between the overall mass transfer coefficient and individual phase coefficients. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.3 above. Key points: 1. **Two-film theory** (Whitman, 1923): A stagnant film exists on either side of the gas-liquid interface, and all resistance to mass transfer is concentrated in these films. Bulk phases are perfectly mixed. 2. Define $k_y$ (gas-side), $k_x$ (liquid-side), and $K_y$ (overall gas-side). 3. Equilibrium at interface: $y_{Ai} = m x_{Ai}$. 4. Derive: $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$. **(b)** In a gas absorption column, the individual gas-phase coefficient is $k_y = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s and the liquid-phase coefficient is $k_x = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol/m²·s. The equilibrium relation is $y = 0.8x$. Calculate the overall mass transfer coefficient $K_y$ and identify the controlling phase. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** $k_y = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$, $k_x = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $m = 0.8$. **Step 1:** Use the overall coefficient relation: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$$ **Step 2:** Compute each term: $$\frac{1}{k_y} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 666.67$$ $$\frac{m}{k_x} = \frac{0.8}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 200$$ **Step 3:** Sum: $$\frac{1}{K_y} = 666.67 + 200 = 866.67$$ **Step 4:** Take reciprocal: $$K_y = 1.154 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$$ **Step 5:** Identify controlling phase: Since $\frac{1}{k_y} = 666.67 \gg \frac{m}{k_x} = 200$, the **gas phase is the controlling resistance** (about 77% of total resistance). The liquid phase offers relatively less resistance. $$\boxed{K_y = 1.154 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s};\ \text{Gas phase controls}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often forget that in the relation $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$, the factor $m$ (slope of equilibrium line) multiplies $\frac{1}{k_x}$ only when expressing $K_y$ on a gas-phase basis. When expressing on a liquid-phase basis, the relation is $\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m\,k_y}$. Mixing these two forms is a guaranteed mark loss. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3** - [ ] **Fick's First Law:** $J_A^* = -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$ — Negative sign indicates flow from high to low concentration. - [ ] **Fick's Second Law:** $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ — Used for unsteady-state diffusion. - [ ] **Magnitude of $D_{AB}$:** Gases $\sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s; Liquids $\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s; Solids $\sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s. - [ ] **Equimolar condition:** $N_A = -N_B$ → Bulk flow term vanishes → Fick's law applies directly. - [ ] **Stefan Diffusion:** $N_B = 0$ → Bulk flow correction is non-zero → Use $P_{BM}$ form. - [ ] **Wilke-Chang Equation:** For liquid-phase diffusivity estimation when experimental data is unavailable. - [ ] **Mass Transfer Coefficient:** $N_A = k_c(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ — Units are m/s. - [ ] **Two-Film Overall:** $\frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x}$; $\frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m\,k_y}$. - [ ] **Controlling Phase:** The phase with the **larger individual resistance** controls the overall transfer. For gas controlling, $K_y \approx k_y$. For liquid controlling, $K_y \approx k_x/m$. - [ ] **Common exam trap:** When using Stefan equation, always remember $p_A$ and $P$ must be in **same units** — never mix mm Hg with Pa in the log term. - [ ] **Error function** for unsteady-state diffusion in solids: $\text{erf}(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\int_0^x e^{-u^2}du$ — values tabulated. - [ ] **Henry's law** constant $m$ in two-film relations: $m = y/x$ at interface; small $m$ means gas is more soluble (liquid controls), large $m$ means gas controls.

topics.title: Theories of Mass Transfer and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theories of Mass Transfer and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Transfer Theories** are mathematical models that describe the rate at which a chemical species (solute) is transported from one phase to another across a phase boundary. They quantify the **interfacial mass transfer coefficient ($k_C$ or $k_L$ or $k_G$)** based on assumed physical mechanisms of transport near the interface.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3), the following **three classical theories** are syllabus-mandated:

1. **Two-Film Theory** (Whitman, 1923) — Steady-state molecular diffusion across two stagnant films.
2. **Penetration Theory** (Higbie, 1935) — Transient diffusion into a fluid element during its brief exposure at the interface.
3. **Surface Renewal Theory** (Danckwerts, 1951) — Stochastic (random) replacement of surface elements by fresh fluid from the bulk.

**The "mass transfer coefficient"** is a proportionality constant between the **driving force** (concentration difference) and the **molar flux** of species A:

$$N_A = k_C \cdot \Delta C_A$$

where the units depend on the chosen basis:
- $k_C$ in **m/s** (concentration-based, molar flux in $\text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$)
- $k_G$ in **kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹** (gas-phase, partial pressure driving force)
- $k_L$ in **m/s** (liquid-phase, molar concentration driving force)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Coffee Cup Model** ☕

Imagine you drop a sugar cube into a still cup of coffee. The sugar dissolves at the bottom (the **interface**) and slowly spreads upward.

- **Film Theory** says: There is a thin stagnant "blanket" of coffee right next to the sugar. Sugar molecules have to march one-by-one through this blanket by random thermal motion. Once they cross it, the bulk coffee carries them away by gentle convection.
- **Penetration Theory** says: Forget the blanket — think of small packets of coffee that briefly *touch* the sugar, absorb some sweetness for a moment, then are swept back into the bulk. The rate depends on **how long** each packet stays at the surface.
- **Surface Renewal Theory** says: Different packets stay for *different* random times. Some stay long, some are swept away instantly. We must average over an **exponential distribution of contact times**.

> **Why three theories?** Because real industrial equipment (packed columns, bubble columns) sits *between* these idealizations. The theories give us **closed-form engineering equations** that bracket the true behavior and scale laboratory data to plant conditions.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value/Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D_{AB}$ | Diffusivity of A in B | $\sim 10^{-9}$ to $10^{-5}$ **m²/s** |
| $\delta$ | Effective film thickness | $\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ **m** |
| $t_e$ | Exposure / contact time | 0.01 to 1 **s** |
| $s$ | Surface renewal rate | 1 to 100 **s⁻¹** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | 8.314 **J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| $P$ | Total pressure | **Pa** or **atm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory I — The Two-Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)

### Postulates (Assumptions)
- **Steady-state** mass transfer.
- Two **stagnant films** exist — one on the gas side, one on the liquid side — adjacent to the interface.
- Transport across each film occurs by **molecular diffusion only** (Fick's Law).
- Equilibrium exists **exactly at the interface** (no interfacial resistance).
- Bulk phases are **well-mixed** (convection dominates outside the film).

### Mathematical Framework
In the gas film, the steady flux of A through a stagnant B is given by the **Stefan diffusion equation**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta_G} \cdot \frac{(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i})}{(p_{B,M})}$$

where $p_{B,M}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B** across the film:

$$p_{B,M} = \frac{(p_{B,i} - p_{B,b})}{\ln\left(\dfrac{p_{B,i}}{p_{B,b}}\right)}$$

### Why the log-mean?
Because B is stagnant in the film — its flux is zero — but its mole fraction varies linearly with position. Hence its driving force must be a **logarithmic mean**, not an arithmetic mean.

### Definition of $k_G$ and $k_L$

$$\boxed{\;k_G = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta_G \cdot p_{B,M}}\;} \quad \text{(gas film coefficient)}$$

$$\boxed{\;k_L = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta_L \cdot x_{B,M}}\;} \quad \text{(liquid film coefficient)}$$

The **overall coefficient** $K_G$ (or $K_L$) combines both films in series:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \cdot k_G}$$

where $m = dy/dx$ is the **slope of the equilibrium curve**.

## 2.2 Theory II — The Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935)

### Postulates
- The interface is **renewed continuously** by fresh fluid elements arriving from the bulk.
- Each element stays at the interface for a **fixed exposure time $t_e$**.
- During exposure, mass transfer is **transient (unsteady) molecular diffusion** into a semi-infinite medium (Fick's Second Law).

### Derivation of $k_L$
Solving the unsteady diffusion equation with constant surface concentration $C_{A,i}$:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

with I.C. $C_A(z,0) = C_{A,b}$ and B.C.s $C_A(0,t) = C_{A,i}$ and $C_A(\infty,t) = C_{A,b}$.

The instantaneous flux at the interface ($z=0$) is:

$$N_A(t) = \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}} \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$$

Averaging over the exposure time $t_e$:

$$\overline{N_A} = \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e} N_A(t)\,dt$$

Performing the integration:

$$\overline{N_A} = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}} \cdot (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$$

Therefore, the **penetration-theory liquid-side mass transfer coefficient** is:

$$\boxed{\;k_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}\;}$$

### Engineering Insight
- $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ — note the **square-root dependence**, in contrast to film theory's **first-power dependence**.
- The shorter the exposure time (more turbulent contact), the higher the mass transfer rate.

## 2.3 Theory III — The Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951)

### Postulates
- Like penetration theory, surface elements are replaced by fresh bulk fluid.
- Unlike Higbie, the **age of each surface element follows an exponential distribution**.
- The probability of a surface element of age $\tau$ being replaced in time $d\tau$ is $s \cdot e^{-s\tau} d\tau$, where $s$ is the **fractional surface renewal rate** (units: $\text{s}^{-1}$).

### Derivation of $k_L$
The average flux is obtained by integrating the instantaneous penetration flux over the age distribution:

$$\overline{N_A} = \int_0^{\infty} N_A(\tau) \cdot s \cdot e^{-s\tau}\, d\tau$$

Substituting $N_A(\tau) = \sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi \tau)}(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi\tau}} \cdot s \cdot e^{-s\tau}\, d\tau$$

The integral is a standard Laplace-type integral equal to $\sqrt{D_{AB} s}$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}\;}$$

### Comparison with Penetration Theory
- Penetration: $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)} = 1.128 \sqrt{D_{AB}/t_e}$
- Surface Renewal: $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s}$

If we identify $t_e = 1/s$, the two formulas become **numerically similar** but conceptually different.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Board Examinations**

| # | Theory | Key Equation | Driving Force | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Film | $k_L = D_{AB}/\delta$ | $\Delta C$ | Steady, $\delta$ = film thickness |
| 2 | Film (gas) | $k_G = \dfrac{D_{AB}\cdot P}{R\,T\,\delta_G\, p_{B,M}}$ | $\Delta p_A$ | Stagnant B → log-mean $p_B$ |
| 3 | Penetration | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ | $\Delta C$ | Transient, fixed $t_e$ |
| 4 | Surface Renewal | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s}$ | $\Delta C$ | Random age distribution |
| 5 | Overall (gas) | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | $\Delta y$ | Series resistance |
| 6 | Overall (liq) | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G}$ | $\Delta x$ | Series resistance |
| 7 | Equilibrium Slope | $m = dy^*/dx$ | — | Henry: $y^* = m x$ |
| 8 | Fractional Resistance | $\% \text{ gas film} = \dfrac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \times 100$ | — | Identifies controlling film |

### Mass Transfer Analogies (Chilton–Colburn Type)

| Heat Transfer | Mass Transfer | Analogy Factor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Nu = hL/k$ | $Sh = k_C L/D_{AB}$ | Sherwood, $Sh$ |
| $Pr = C_p \mu/k$ | $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB})$ | Schmidt, $Sc$ |
| $Re = \rho vL/\mu$ | $Re = \rho vL/\mu$ | Reynolds, $Re$ |
| $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ | $j_M = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ | $j_H \approx j_M$ |

> **Engineering Utility**: These theories are used in designing **absorption columns** (gas treating with MEA/DEA), **stripping towers**, **humidification equipment**, and **liquid-liquid extractors**. By knowing which theory applies (controlled by Reynolds number, geometry, and physical properties), the engineer can predict $k_G$ or $k_L$ and size the equipment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Flux Through a Stagnant Gas Film (Film Theory)

**Statement:** Species A diffuses through a stagnant gas B across a film of thickness $\delta_G$ at steady state.

**Governing equation** (Fick's Law with no net flux of B):

$$N_A = - \frac{D_{AB}}{1 - x_A}\frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Using the ideal gas law $C_A = P/RT$ and defining $p_A = x_A P$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\cdot \frac{1}{1 - x_A}\frac{dx_A}{dz}$$

Boundary conditions:
- At $z = 0$ (interface): $x_A = x_{A,i} = p_{A,i}/P$
- At $z = \delta_G$ (bulk gas): $x_A = x_{A,b} = p_{A,b}/P$

Since A diffuses one way and B is stagnant, the flux $N_A$ is constant with $z$. Separating variables:

$$N_A\, dz = -\frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\cdot \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}$$

Integrating from $z = 0$ to $\delta_G$:

$$N_A \cdot \delta_G = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\int_{x_{A,i}}^{x_{A,b}}\frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}$$

Evaluating the integral (using $\int dx/(1-x) = -\ln(1-x)$):

$$N_A \cdot \delta_G = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T}\left[\ln(1 - x_{A,b}) - \ln(1 - x_{A,i})\right]$$

Since $1 - x_A = x_B$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_{B,i}}{x_{B,b}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G}\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B,i}}{p_{B,b}}\right)$$

Now using $p_B = P - p_A$, we recognize this as a **log-mean** expression. Algebraic manipulation:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B,i}}{p_{B,b}}\right) = \frac{p_{B,i} - p_{B,b}}{p_{B,M}}$$

with the **log-mean partial pressure of B**:

$$p_{B,M} = \frac{p_{B,b} - p_{B,i}}{\ln(p_{B,b}/p_{B,i})}$$

Final compact form:

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G}\cdot\frac{(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i})}{p_{B,M}}\;}$$

Equating to $N_A = k_G(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i})$:

$$k_G = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G\, p_{B,M}}$$

This derivation is the **most-frequently asked** long derivation in KTU Module 3.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Penetration Theory Instantaneous Flux

**Statement:** A fluid element of infinite depth is exposed to a constant surface concentration $C_{A,i}$ for time $t$.

**Governing PDE** (Fick's Second Law, 1-D):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB}\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

Initial and boundary conditions:

$$C_A(z,0) = C_{A,b} \quad ; \quad C_A(0,t) = C_{A,i} \quad ; \quad C_A(\infty,t) = C_{A,b}$$

Define $\theta(z,t) = \dfrac{C_A - C_{A,b}}{C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}}$, giving a similar PDE with $\theta(0,t)=1$, $\theta(\infty,t)=0$, $\theta(z,0)=0$.

**Method 1: Laplace Transform in $t$**
Taking Laplace transform of the PDE:

$$s\bar{\theta} = D_{AB}\frac{d^2\bar{\theta}}{dz^2}$$

General solution: $\bar{\theta} = A e^{-qz} + B e^{+qz}$ with $q = \sqrt{s/D_{AB}}$. Boundedness as $z \to \infty$ forces $B = 0$. At $z=0$, $\bar{\theta} = 1/s$, so $A = 1/s$:

$$\bar{\theta}(z,s) = \frac{1}{s}e^{-z\sqrt{s/D_{AB}}}$$

The instantaneous flux at $z=0$ is:

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB}\left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0}$$

In transformed domain:

$$\frac{d\bar{\theta}}{dz}\bigg|_{z=0} = -\frac{1}{s}\sqrt{\frac{s}{D_{AB}}} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{s\,D_{AB}}}$$

Inverse Laplace transform (using standard transform pairs):

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial z}\bigg|_{z=0} = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB}\,t}}$$

Therefore:

$$N_A(t) = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

**Averaging over exposure time $t_e$:**

$$\overline{N_A} = \frac{1}{t_e}\int_0^{t_e}(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}\,dt = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}\int_0^{1}\tau^{-1/2}d\tau$$

$$= 2(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;k_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation C — Surface Renewal Theory Average Flux

**Statement:** Surface elements of age $\tau$ are replaced at rate $s\cdot e^{-s\tau}d\tau$.

The expected flux, weighted by the age distribution:

$$\overline{N_A} = \int_0^{\infty} N_A(\tau) \cdot \phi(\tau)\,d\tau \quad ; \quad \phi(\tau) = s\,e^{-s\tau}$$

Using the penetration expression $N_A(\tau) = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi\tau)}$:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}}\int_0^{\infty}\tau^{-1/2}\cdot s\,e^{-s\tau}d\tau$$

Substitute $u = s\tau$, $du = s\,d\tau$:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}\,s}{\pi}}\int_0^{\infty}u^{-1/2}e^{-u}\,du$$

The integral is the **Gamma function** $\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}$:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}\,s}{\pi}}\cdot\sqrt{\pi} = (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})\sqrt{D_{AB}\,s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s}\;}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Theory Selection Guide

| Physical Situation | Dominant Theory | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gas absorption in a **laminar liquid jet** | Penetration | Well-defined contact time of jet element |
| **Packed column** with random packing | Surface Renewal | Stochastic surface exposure |
| **Bubble** rising slowly in liquid | Film | Quasi-steady interface, predictable $\delta$ |
| **Highly turbulent** gas-liquid contact | Surface Renewal | Continuous random renewal |
| **Solid dissolution** in agitated tank | Film | Steady-state diffusion layer |
| Gas absorption in **falling film** | Penetration | Well-defined exposure = film residence time |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Mass Transfer Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overall Process Goal: Transfer Species A from Phase 1 to Phase 2] --> B[Identify Two Phases and the Interface]
    B --> C[Identify the Driving Force delta C or delta p]
    C --> D[Choose a Physical Model]
    D --> D1[Film Theory: Steady Diffusion Across Stagnant Layer]
    D --> D2[Penetration Theory: Transient Diffusion with Fixed Exposure Time]
    D --> D3[Surface Renewal Theory: Random Age Distribution]
    D1 --> E[Compute Individual Film Coefficient kG or kL]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Overall Coefficient KG or KL using Series Resistance]
    F --> G[Compute Molar Flux NA equals KG times delta y or KL times delta x]
    G --> H[Integrate Over Contact Area to Find Total Rate]
    H --> I[Equipment Design: Packed Column, Tray Column, or Membrane Module]
    I --> J[Verify and Iterate]
```

## 4.2 Concentration Profile Across the Two Films

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Bulk Gas: yAG] --> P2[Gas Film: Linear pA profile from pAG to pAi]
    P2 --> P3[Interface: Equilibrium pAi equals m times xAi]
    P3 --> P4[Liquid Film: Linear xA profile from xAi to xAb]
    P4 --> P5[Bulk Liquid: xAb]
    P1 -.->|kG controls| P2
    P4 -.->|kL controls| P5
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow: How Each Theory Treats the Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Fluid Element Arrives at Interface] --> B{Which Theory?}
    B -->|Film Theory| C[Element Stays at Interface Indefinitely in a Stagnant Layer]
    B -->|Penetration Theory| D[Element Stays for Fixed Time te and Returns to Bulk]
    B -->|Surface Renewal| E[Element Has Random Age from Exponential Distribution with Mean 1 slash s]
    C --> F[Compute kL equals DAB over delta]
    D --> G[Compute kL equals 2 sqrt DAB over pi te]
    E --> H[Compute kL equals sqrt DAB times s]
    F --> I[Use in Equipment Sizing]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.4 Series Resistance Network (Overall Coefficient)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RG[Resistance 1 slash kG: Gas Film] --> RI[Interface: Zero Resistance, Equilibrium Holds]
    RI --> RL[Resistance m over kL: Liquid Film]
    RG -.->|in series| Rtot[Total Resistance 1 slash KG]
    RL -.->|in series| Rtot
    Rtot --> Flux[Molar Flux NA equals KG times delta y]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
State the **assumptions of the Two-Film theory** of mass transfer.

**Model Answer:**
1. Mass transfer occurs at **steady state**.
2. Two **stagnant films** exist on either side of the interface; bulk phases are well-mixed.
3. **Equilibrium exists exactly at the interface** with no interfacial resistance.
4. Transfer across each film occurs by **molecular diffusion alone** (obeys Fick's Law).
5. The films are of **constant thickness** at any given hydrodynamic condition.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Compare the **Penetration theory** and the **Surface Renewal theory** with respect to contact time.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Penetration Theory (Higbie) | Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Contact time | **Fixed** for all surface elements = $t_e$ | **Random**, exponential distribution |
| Age distribution | All elements have age $t_e$ | Probability $\phi(\tau) = s\,e^{-s\tau}$ |
| $k_L$ expression | $k_L = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_e)}$ | $k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s}$ |
| Physical basis | Each element is exposed for the same duration | Different elements exposed for different times |

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Film Theory Dominant

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **steady-state molar flux of A through a stagnant gas film B**, and hence obtain the **gas-film mass transfer coefficient $k_G$**. Explain the significance of the **log-mean partial pressure of B**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the absorption of **SO₂ from air using water** in a packed column, the following data are given:

- Bulk gas: $y_{SO_2} = 0.09$, $P = 101.3$ kPa
- Interface: $x_{SO_2} = 0.0012$
- Bulk liquid: $x_{SO_2,bulk} = 0.0006$
- $k_G = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ kmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·kPa⁻¹
- $k_L = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s
- Equilibrium: $y^* = 30\,x$

Compute:
(i) The flux of SO₂ based on **gas-film and liquid-film** coefficients.
(ii) The **overall coefficient $K_G$** and the **overall flux $N_A$**.
(iii) The **percentage resistance** offered by the gas and liquid films.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The detailed derivation is shown in **Section 3.1**. The key result:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G}\cdot\frac{(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i})}{p_{B,M}}$$

with

$$p_{B,M} = \frac{p_{B,b} - p_{B,i}}{\ln(p_{B,b}/p_{B,i})}$$

and

$$k_G = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G\, p_{B,M}}$$

**Significance of $p_{B,M}$:** Since B is stagnant, its flux is zero but its concentration varies along the film. The driving force for A must therefore be expressed with the **log-mean** of $p_B$, not the arithmetic mean. As $p_A$ increases, $p_B$ decreases, and the log-mean correctly captures this nonlinear variation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute partial pressures**

$$p_{A,b} = y_{A,b}\cdot P = 0.09 \times 101.3 = 9.117 \text{ kPa}$$
$$p_{A,i} = y_{A,i}\cdot P = (30 \times 0.0012)\times 101.3 = 3.647 \text{ kPa}$$
$$p_{B,b} = P - p_{A,b} = 101.3 - 9.117 = 92.183 \text{ kPa}$$
$$p_{B,i} = P - p_{A,i} = 101.3 - 3.647 = 97.653 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Log-mean of B**

$$p_{B,M} = \frac{p_{B,b} - p_{B,i}}{\ln(p_{B,b}/p_{B,i})} = \frac{92.183 - 97.653}{\ln(92.183/97.653)} = \frac{-5.470}{\ln(0.94398)} = \frac{-5.470}{-0.05767}$$

$$p_{B,M} = 94.85 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Individual film fluxes**

Gas-film flux:

$$N_A = k_G(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i}) = 1.2\times 10^{-3} \times (9.117 - 3.647) = 6.564 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

Liquid-film flux (need $C_{A,i}$ from $x_{A,i}$):

Assuming dilute solution, $C_{A,i} = x_{A,i}\cdot C_T$ where $C_T = \rho_L/M_{avg}$. For water, $C_T \approx 55.5$ kmol/m³, so $C_{A,i} = 0.0012 \times 55.5 = 0.0666$ kmol/m³, $C_{A,b} = 0.0006 \times 55.5 = 0.0333$ kmol/m³.

$$N_A = k_L(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) = 3.5\times 10^{-5}\times(0.0666 - 0.0333) = 1.166 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Overall coefficient $K_G$**

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L/C_T} = \frac{1}{1.2\times 10^{-3}} + \frac{30}{3.5\times 10^{-5}\times 55.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = 833.33 + 15435.95 = 16269.28$$

$$K_G = 6.147 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}\cdot\text{kPa}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Overall flux**

$$N_A = K_G(p_{A,b} - p_{A,i}) = 6.147\times 10^{-5} \times 5.470 = 3.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage resistance**

$$\% \text{ gas film} = \frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G}\times 100 = \frac{833.33}{16269.28}\times 100 = 5.12\%$$

$$\% \text{ liquid film} = 100 - 5.12 = 94.88\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The **liquid film controls** the mass transfer. To improve absorption rate, the engineer should focus on increasing $k_L$ (e.g., better liquid distribution, higher flow rate) rather than $k_G$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Penetration / Surface Renewal Dominant

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mass transfer coefficient based on the Penetration theory (Higbie)**. Discuss how it differs from the Film theory result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Oxygen is being absorbed from air into water in a small bubble column. The bubble interface is renewed with a **surface renewal rate $s = 0.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$** and the **diffusivity of O₂ in water is $D_{AB} = 2.1 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$**. The bulk O₂ concentration is $C_{A,b} = 0.20\,\text{kmol/m}^3$ and the interface concentration is $C_{A,i} = 0.28\,\text{kmol/m}^3$.

Calculate:
(i) The mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ using **Surface Renewal theory**.
(ii) The molar flux $N_A$.
(iii) The **equivalent exposure time** if Penetration theory were to give the same $k_L$.
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Detailed derivation is in **Section 3.2**. The key result:

$$k_L = 2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}}$$

**Differences from Film theory:**

| Aspect | Film Theory | Penetration Theory |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State | Steady state | Transient |
| $k_L$ dependence on $D_{AB}$ | $k_L \propto D_{AB}$ (first power) | $k_L \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$ (half power) |
| Driving force | Local concentration gradient | Cumulative uptake over $t_e$ |
| Surface renewal | Not considered | Fixed exposure time $t_e$ |
| Application | Slow laminar flow | Bubble columns, jets |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Surface Renewal Theory $k_L$**

$$k_L = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s} = \sqrt{2.1\times 10^{-9} \times 0.5} = \sqrt{1.05\times 10^{-9}}$$

$$k_L = 3.24 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Molar flux**

$$N_A = k_L(C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) = 3.24\times 10^{-5}\times(0.28 - 0.20)$$

$$N_A = 2.59 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Equivalent exposure time**

Equating $k_L$ from both theories:

$$2\sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e}} = \sqrt{D_{AB}\cdot s}$$

$$4\cdot \frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_e} = D_{AB}\cdot s$$

$$t_e = \frac{4}{\pi s} = \frac{4}{\pi \times 0.5} = 2.546 \text{ s}$$

> **Interpretation:** Under the surface renewal model with $s = 0.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$, the **average surface element age** is $1/s = 2$ s. The Higbie exposure time that would give the same $k_L$ is **2.546 s** — slightly larger, reflecting the difference between fixed-time and exponentially distributed exposure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k_G$ and $k_y$:** $k_y$ is mole-fraction based; $k_G$ is partial-pressure based. They differ by the factor $P$. Always use SI-consistent units.
> 2. **Forgetting the log-mean $p_{B,M}$:** When A is dilute, you may set $p_{B,M} \approx P$ (approx. 1–2% error). For moderate concentrations, this approximation **fails** and costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing $k_L$ units:** In absorption, the liquid-side flux uses $k_L(\Delta C)$; in terms of $k_x$, it becomes $k_L \cdot C_T$. Students forget to multiply by $C_T$ when reporting the overall resistance.
> 4. **Wrong equilibrium slope:** For Henry's law, $y = m x$ has $m = H/P$. For $\text{NH}_3$-water, $m$ is small; for $\text{CO}_2$-water, $m$ is large. The film controlling resistance depends critically on $m$.
> 5. **Penetration theory derivation:** Examiners specifically check whether students write the **unsteady Fick's Law**, apply **Laplace transform or error-function solution**, and then perform the **time-averaging integral**. Skipping the averaging step costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Surface Renewal age distribution:** The probability function is $\phi(\tau) = s e^{-s\tau}$, **not** $s e^{+s\tau}$. Sign errors are the most common slip.
> 7. **Forgetting to report units:** Every numerical answer in KTU papers **must** carry units. A correct number without units is marked partially (typically –1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Module 3 Theories of Mass Transfer**

- ✅ **Two-Film Theory** assumes a stagnant film of thickness $\delta$ on each side of the interface; gives $k \propto D_{AB}$.
- ✅ **Penetration Theory** assumes fixed exposure time $t_e$; gives $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}/t_e}$.
- ✅ **Surface Renewal Theory** assumes exponential age distribution with rate $s$; gives $k \propto \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$.
- ✅ **Stefan equation** (stagnant B): $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,\delta_G}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta p_A}{p_{B,M}}$.
- ✅ **Log-mean $p_{B,M}$** must be used when A is not extremely dilute; for dilute A, $p_{B,M} \approx P$.
- ✅ **Series resistance** model: $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$.
- ✅ **Controlling film:** If $\dfrac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} \to 1$, gas film controls; if $\dfrac{m/k_L}{1/K_G} \to 1$, liquid film controls.
- ✅ **Chilton–Colburn analogy** $j_H \approx j_M$ correlates heat and mass transfer in turbulent flow.
- ✅ **Selection rule:** Bubble columns and jets → Penetration/Surface Renewal; laminar flow and dissolved solids → Film Theory.
- ✅ **Engineering scale-up:** Surface renewal rate $s$ is empirically correlated to power input per unit mass: $s \propto (P/V)^\alpha$.
- ✅ **Units consistency:** Use SI throughout (m, s, kmol, Pa, J). KTU examiners penalize unit errors.
- ✅ **Key dimensionless number:** Schmidt number $Sc = \mu/(\rho D_{AB})$; for gases $Sc \approx 0.6$–$1.5$, for liquids $Sc \approx 100$–$1000$.
- ✅ **Henry's law form:** $y^* = m x$ where $m = H/P$ ($H$ = Henry's constant in pressure units).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — THEORIES OF MASS TRANSFER AND THEIR APPLICATIONS**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PBSFT504, Module 3.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interphase Mass Transfer and Overall Mass Transfer Coefficients in Binary Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Interphase Mass Transfer and Overall Mass Transfer Coefficients in Binary Systems # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Interphase Mass Transfer? In a binary system containing two components (say, **A** = solute, **B** = carrier), mass transfer of component **A** takes place **across a phase boundary (interface)**. This interface is the contact surface between two immiscible phases — most commonly a **gas–liquid interface** (e.g., absorption, humidification, dehumidification) or a **liquid–liquid interface** (e.g., liquid–liquid extraction). > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU Definition (Module 3):** > *Interphase mass transfer* is the transport of a solute species **A** from the bulk of one phase to the bulk of another phase across a fluid–fluid interface. In binary systems, the driving force is the departure of the bulk phase concentration from its **interfacial equilibrium value**, and the rate is governed by two individual film resistances acting in series. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine two adjacent rooms separated by a wall with a window: - **Room G (Gas Phase)** — has lots of smoke (solute A) - **Room L (Liquid Phase)** — has clean water absorbing the smoke - **The Window = Interface** — the place where contact happens Smoke has to overcome **two resistances**: 1. **Resistance in the gas film** (smoke has to travel from the middle of Room G up to the window) — characterized by $k_G$ or $k_y$. 2. **Resistance in the liquid film** (smoke has to dissolve and travel from the window into the middle of Room L) — characterized by $k_L$ or $k_x$. Just like two electrical resistors in series, these two **resistances add up** to give a **total resistance**, whose reciprocal is the **overall mass transfer coefficient** ($K_G$ or $K_L$). ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme This topic is the **theoretical backbone** of Module 3 (Mass Transfer Operations). It connects: - The **Two-Film Theory** (Whitman, 1923) - The concept of **equilibrium at the interface** - The **resistance-in-series** analogy with heat transfer - The derivation of **overall coefficients** from individual coefficients > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, Section 3):** > Students must be able to: (i) write flux equations in terms of individual and overall coefficients, (ii) derive the relationship between $K$ and $k$, and (iii) identify the **controlling resistance** based on the slope $m$ of the equilibrium line. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units (SI) | |--------|----------|--------------------| | $k_G$ | Individual gas-phase coefficient | kmol/(m²·s·Pa) | | $k_L$ | Individual liquid-phase coefficient | m/s (or kmol/(m²·s·kmol/m³)) | | $K_G$ | Overall gas-phase coefficient | kmol/(m²·s·Pa) | | $K_L$ | Overall liquid-phase coefficient | m/s | | $m$ | Slope of equilibrium line $y^* = mx$ | dimensionless | | $N_A$ | Molar flux of A | kmol/(m²·s) | | $P$ | Total pressure | **Pa (101325 Pa = 1 atm)** | --- # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conceptual Framework: The Two-Film Theory Whitman's **Two-Film Theory** postulates that: 1. Near the interface, two **stagnant films** exist — one in the gas phase and one in the liquid phase. 2. Mass transfer through these films occurs by **steady-state molecular diffusion**. 3. The bulk phases are **well-mixed** (no concentration gradient in the bulk). 4. **Thermodynamic equilibrium** prevails *exactly* at the interface. > [!NOTE] > For a binary system with solute **A** transferring from gas to liquid: > - $y_{A,b}$ = bulk gas mole fraction of A (or $P_{A,b}$ in partial pressure) > - $y_{A,i}$ = interfacial gas mole fraction of A (in equilibrium with $x_{A,i}$) > - $x_{A,b}$ = bulk liquid mole fraction of A > - $x_{A,i}$ = interfacial liquid mole fraction of A (in equilibrium with $y_{A,i}$) ## 2.2 The Two Critical Operating Equations **Equation 1 — Flux through the gas film:** $$N_A = k_G \left( P_{A,b} - P_{A,i} \right) = k_y \left( y_{A,b} - y_{A,i} \right)$$ **Equation 2 — Flux through the liquid film:** $$N_A = k_L \left( C_{A,i} - C_{A,b} \right) = k_x \left( x_{A,i} - x_{A,b} \right)$$ **Equation 3 — Interface equilibrium (linearized for dilute systems):** $$y_{A,i} = m \, x_{A,i} \quad \text{or} \quad P_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$$ These three equations, when combined, yield the **overall flux** in terms of an **overall driving force** between bulk phases. ## 2.3 Definition of Overall Coefficients > [!IMPORTANT] > An **overall mass transfer coefficient** lumps both individual film resistances into a single coefficient, expressed against the **bulk-to-bulk driving force** (excluding the interface). $$N_A = K_G \left( P_{A,b} - P_A^* \right) = K_L \left( C_A^* - C_{A,b} \right)$$ where: - $P_A^*$ = partial pressure of A in the gas phase that **would be in equilibrium** with the bulk liquid concentration $x_{A,b}$. - $C_A^*$ = concentration of A in the liquid that **would be in equilibrium** with the bulk gas $y_{A,b}$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference) | # | Equation | Description | |---|----------|-------------| | 1 | $N_A = k_G (P_{A,b} - P_{A,i})$ | Individual flux (gas film) | | 2 | $N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$ | Individual flux (liquid film) | | 3 | $P_{A,i} = m C_{A,i}$ | Interfacial equilibrium | | 4 | $N_A = K_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*)$ | Overall flux (gas basis) | | 5 | $N_A = K_L (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$ | Overall flux (liquid basis) | | 6 | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | KTU ⭐ — Gas-basis resistance additivity | | 7 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m k_G}$ | KTU ⭐ — Liquid-basis resistance additivity | | 8 | $K_G \cdot m = K_L$ | Conversion between $K_G$ and $K_L$ | | 9 | $K_y = \dfrac{K_G}{P}$ | Conversion between partial pressure and mole-fraction forms | | 10 | $K_x = K_L \cdot \rho_s$ | For dilute solutions (where $\rho_s$ = molar density of solution) | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Examination Rule:** > Equation 6 ($\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$) places $m$ on the **liquid-side resistance**, NOT the gas side. Many students flip this and lose marks. Memorize it using the mnemonic: **"m rides with L (the liquid)."** ## 2.5 The Two Limiting Cases (Controlling Resistance) | Case | Slope $m$ | Controlling Phase | Simplified Form | |------|-----------|------------------|-----------------| | Highly soluble gas (e.g., NH₃ in water) | $m \to 0$ | Gas film | $K_G \approx k_G$ | | Slightly soluble gas (e.g., O₂ in water) | $m \to \infty$ | Liquid film | $K_L \approx k_L$ | > [!TIP] > For the **liquid basis** in the $m \to 0$ case, $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m k_G} \to \infty$, meaning $K_L \to 0$. This is why reporting the coefficient on the **controlling-phase basis** is always preferred in design. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Controlling Phase | Why? | |-------------|-------------------|------| | Absorption of CO₂ in alkaline solutions | Liquid film | $m$ is large; reaction in liquid enhances $k_L$ | | Absorption of SO₂ in water | Gas film | $m$ is small; gas-side turbulence dominates | | Oxygen transfer to blood (lung model) | Liquid film | $m$ large; membrane resistance dominant | | Humidification of air over water | Gas film | $m$ small (low volatility) | --- # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ (Gas-Basis Overall Coefficient) > **Starting point:** Two-film theory equations for steady-state diffusion in a binary system. **Step 1: Write the individual flux equations through each film.** $$N_A = k_G (P_{A,b} - P_{A,i}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 2: Express the interfacial partial pressure using the linearized equilibrium relation.** From (ii), solve for the interfacial concentration: $$C_{A,i} = \frac{N_A}{k_L} + C_{A,b}$$ Using the equilibrium relation $P_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$ (valid for dilute systems where Henry's law applies): $$P_{A,i} = m \left( \frac{N_A}{k_L} + C_{A,b} \right) = \frac{m \, N_A}{k_L} + m \, C_{A,b}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the interfacial partial pressure into the gas-film equation.** Substituting $P_{A,i}$ into (i): $$N_A = k_G \left[ P_{A,b} - \left( \frac{m \, N_A}{k_L} + m \, C_{A,b} \right) \right]$$ **Step 4: Expand and collect all $N_A$ terms on the left-hand side.** $$N_A = k_G \, P_{A,b} - \frac{k_G \, m \, N_A}{k_L} - k_G \, m \, C_{A,b}$$ $$N_A + \frac{k_G \, m \, N_A}{k_L} = k_G \left( P_{A,b} - m \, C_{A,b} \right)$$ **Step 5: Factor $N_A$ on the left-hand side.** $$N_A \left( 1 + \frac{k_G \, m}{k_L} \right) = k_G \left( P_{A,b} - m \, C_{A,b} \right)$$ **Step 6: Recognize the bulk equilibrium partial pressure.** By definition, the partial pressure of A in the gas phase in equilibrium with the bulk liquid $C_{A,b}$ is: $$P_A^* = m \, C_{A,b}$$ So the right-hand side becomes $k_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*)$. **Step 7: Solve for $N_A$ in the form $N_A = K_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*)$.** $$N_A = \frac{k_G}{1 + \dfrac{k_G \, m}{k_L}} \left( P_{A,b} - P_A^* \right) = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}} \left( P_{A,b} - P_A^* \right)$$ **Step 8: Define the overall coefficient and write the final result.** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** The total resistance $\frac{1}{K_G}$ is the sum of the gas-film resistance $\frac{1}{k_G}$ and the liquid-film resistance $\frac{m}{k_L}$. This is the **electrical resistance analogy** — two resistors in series. ## 3.2 Derivation of $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m k_G}$ (Liquid-Basis Overall Coefficient) **Step 1: Start from the same two-film equations.** $$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ $$N_A = k_G (P_{A,b} - P_{A,i}) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 2: Solve for the interfacial liquid concentration using gas-film equation.** From (i): $P_{A,i} = P_{A,b} - \frac{N_A}{k_G}$ From equilibrium: $C_{A,i} = \frac{P_{A,i}}{m} = \frac{1}{m} \left( P_{A,b} - \frac{N_A}{k_G} \right)$ **Step 3: Substitute into the liquid-film equation.** $$N_A = k_L \left[ \frac{1}{m} \left( P_{A,b} - \frac{N_A}{k_G} \right) - C_{A,b} \right]$$ **Step 4: Expand and collect $N_A$.** $$N_A = \frac{k_L \, P_{A,b}}{m} - \frac{k_L \, N_A}{m \, k_G} - k_L \, C_{A,b}$$ $$N_A + \frac{k_L \, N_A}{m \, k_G} = \frac{k_L}{m} \left( P_{A,b} - m \, C_{A,b} \right)$$ **Step 5: Factor and identify $C_A^*$.** The bulk equilibrium concentration is $C_A^* = \frac{P_{A,b}}{m}$. $$N_A \left( 1 + \frac{k_L}{m \, k_G} \right) = k_L \left( C_A^* - C_{A,b} \right)$$ **Step 6: Solve for $N_A = K_L (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$.** $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \, k_G}}$$ ## 3.3 Conversion Between $K_G$ and $K_L$ Starting from: $$N_A = K_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*) = K_L (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$$ Using $P_A^* = m \, C_{A,b}$ and $C_A^* = P_{A,b} / m$: $$K_G \, m \, (C_A^* - C_{A,b}) = K_L \, (C_A^* - C_{A,b})$$ $$\boxed{K_L = m \, K_G \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad K_G = \frac{K_L}{m}}$$ ## 3.4 Numerical Example — Working Problem (For Practice) > **Problem:** A gas mixture containing dilute NH₃ is contacted with water in an absorption column. The equilibrium relation is $y = 1.0 \, x$ (slope $m = 1.0$). The individual coefficients are: > - $k_G = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$ > - $k_L = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$ > - The system is dilute, so $C_A \approx \rho_s \, x_A$ with $\rho_s = 55 \text{ kmol/m}^3$. > **Find $K_G$ and $K_L$, and identify the controlling resistance.** **Solution using $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$:** We need $k_L$ in compatible units. Note that $k_L$ in m/s assumes $C$ in kmol/m³, which is consistent with $\rho_s x_A$ in kmol/m³. The form $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ assumes $k_L$ has units of kmol/(m²·s·(kmol/m³)) = m/s, **and** $k_G$ has units of kmol/(m²·s·Pa). These are dimensionally consistent for the additive form. Calculate liquid-side resistance: $$\frac{m}{k_L} = \frac{1.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \times 10^{3} \text{ (m}^2\text{·s·Pa/kmol)}$$ Gas-side resistance: $$\frac{1}{k_G} = \frac{1}{2.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ (m}^2\text{·s·Pa/kmol)}$$ Total: $$\frac{1}{K_G} = 5.0 \times 10^{7} + 6.667 \times 10^{3} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{7}$$ $$K_G \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$$ > **Conclusion:** The gas-side resistance dominates (≈ 99.987% of total). This is the **controlling resistance**. Using $K_L = m K_G$ for the unit conversion: $K_L = 1.0 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$ — to convert to m/s, note that $K_L [\text{m/s}] = K_G \cdot R T \cdot m \cdot$ correction for units. For this case study's didactic purpose, the **qualitative outcome** (gas-side control) is the key takeaway. ## 3.5 Summary of the Three Master Equations (Analytical Matrix) | Master Equation | When to Use | Driving Force Reference | |-----------------|-------------|------------------------| | $\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$ | Gas-phase controlled systems | Gas bulk → bulk liquid (gas-equilibrium pressure) | | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{m k_G}$ | Liquid-phase controlled systems | Liquid bulk → bulk gas (liquid-equilibrium concentration) | | $K_L = m \, K_G$ | Unit conversion only | — | --- # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Film Conceptual Architecture (Block-Level Flow) The diagram below maps the **resistance-in-series** flow from the gas bulk to the liquid bulk, showing the relationship between individual and overall coefficients. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Gas Bulk
yA_bulk or PA_bulk] -->|Resistance 1/kG| B[Interface
yA_i in equilibrium with xA_i] B -->|Resistance m over kL| C[Liquid Bulk
xA_bulk or CA_bulk] C --> D[Equilibrium Reference
PA star = m times CA_bulk] D -.->|Overall driving force PA_bulk minus PA star| A B -.->|Overall resistance 1 over KG| A A -->|Overall Resistance 1 over KG| D ``` **Interpretation of the diagram:** - The **top path** (A → B → C) represents the **actual physical transport** through the two films in series. - The **bottom path** (A → D) represents the **equivalent overall transport** using bulk-to-bulk driving force. - $K_G$ lumps both film resistances into a single coefficient for design use. ## 4.2 Detailed Resistance Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph G[Gas Phase Region] G1[BULK GAS
yA_bulk
uniform concentration] G2[Gas Film
Stagnant Layer
yA_bulk to yA_i
Resistance equals 1 over kG] end subgraph I[Interfacial Equilibrium] I1[INTERFACE
yA_i paired with xA_i
via yA_i equals m times xA_i] end subgraph L[Liquid Phase Region] L1[Liquid Film
Stagnant Layer
xA_i to xA_bulk
Resistance equals m over kL] L2[BULK LIQUID
xA_bulk
uniform concentration] end G1 --> G2 G2 --> I1 I1 --> L1 L1 --> L2 I1 -.equilibrium.- G2 L2 -.equilibrium with gas.- G1 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Identifying the Controlling Resistance ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given kG, kL, m] --> B{Is m very small
less than 0.01} B -- Yes --> C[Gas Film Controls
KG approximately equals kG
1 over KL is very large] B -- No --> D{Is m very large
greater than 100} D -- Yes --> E[Liquid Film Controls
KL approximately equals kL
1 over KG is very large] D -- No --> F[Both Resistances Matter
Calculate 1 over KG equals 1 over kG plus m over kL
Calculate 1 over KL equals 1 over kL plus 1 over m kG] C --> G[End] E --> G F --> G ``` ## 4.4 Concentration Profile Schematic (Conceptual Picture) The figure below depicts a conceptual sketch of concentration vs. distance across the interface, mapping the actual driving forces in each film. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DIST[Distance Axis] direction LR D1[Gas Bulk] --- D2[Gas Film
yA decreases from yA_bulk to yA_i] --- D3[Interface
yA_i paired with xA_i] --- D4[Liquid Film
xA decreases from xA_i to xA_bulk] --- D5[Liquid Bulk] end D1 -.-> N1[NA equals kG times yA_bulk minus yA_i] D3 -.-> N2[NA equals kL times xA_i minus xA_bulk] D1 -.-> N3[NA equals KG times yA_bulk minus yA star] D5 -.-> N4[NA equals KL times xA star minus xA_bulk] ``` --- # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the two-film theory of mass transfer. What assumptions are made in this theory?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Two stagnant films** exist on either side of the interface (one in each phase) (1 mark). 2. Mass transfer through these films is by **steady-state molecular diffusion**; bulk phases are well-mixed (1 mark). 3. **Thermodynamic equilibrium** exists at the interface itself (1 mark). --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define overall mass transfer coefficient based on the gas phase ($K_G$) and explain the physical significance of $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - $K_G$ is defined as the coefficient that relates the flux $N_A$ to the **bulk gas partial pressure minus the equilibrium partial pressure** ($P_A^*$) that would be in equilibrium with the bulk liquid (1 mark). - The reciprocal $\frac{1}{K_G}$ represents the **total resistance** to mass transfer (1 mark). - It equals the sum of the **gas-film resistance** ($\frac{1}{k_G}$) and the **liquid-film resistance** ($\frac{m}{k_L}$), analogous to two electrical resistors in series (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** Within Module 3, students may choose between Question A and Question B. Each long answer is 14 marks, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a) Starting from the individual film flux equations, derive the relationship $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ for a binary gas–liquid system. State clearly all assumptions used.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Steady-state, isothermal operation. 2. Dilute binary system — bulk flow contribution negligible. 3. Linearized equilibrium at interface: $P_{A,i} = m C_{A,i}$. 4. Two-film theory (Whitman) holds. **Derivation (5 marks):** Starting with: $$N_A = k_G (P_{A,b} - P_{A,i}) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$N_A = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b}) \quad \text{...(2)}$$ From (2): $C_{A,i} = C_{A,b} + \frac{N_A}{k_L}$ Using equilibrium: $P_{A,i} = m C_{A,i} = m C_{A,b} + \frac{m N_A}{k_L}$ Substituting in (1): $$N_A = k_G \left( P_{A,b} - m C_{A,b} - \frac{m N_A}{k_L} \right)$$ $$N_A + \frac{k_G m N_A}{k_L} = k_G (P_{A,b} - m C_{A,b})$$ $$N_A \left( 1 + \frac{k_G m}{k_L} \right) = k_G (P_{A,b} - m C_{A,b})$$ Recognizing $P_A^* = m C_{A,b}$: $$N_A = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}} (P_{A,b} - P_A^*) = K_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*)$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$ --- **(b) A gas mixture containing dilute SO₂ is absorbed in water. The individual mass transfer coefficients are $k_G = 3.0 \times 10^{-8}$ kmol/(m²·s·Pa) and $k_L = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. The equilibrium line slope is $m = 0.05$. Calculate $K_G$, $K_L$, and identify the controlling resistance.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Compute $\frac{1}{K_G}$ (3 marks):** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{3.0 \times 10^{-8}} + \frac{0.05}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$\frac{1}{K_G} = 3.333 \times 10^{7} + 125 = 3.333 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^2\text{·s·Pa/kmol}$$ $$K_G = 3.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$$ **Compute $K_L$ (2 marks):** $$K_L = m \, K_G = 0.05 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-8} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)} \times \text{(unit conversion factor)}$$ For the comparison of resistances, use the **gas-basis** form: $$\frac{1}{k_G} = 3.333 \times 10^{7}, \quad \frac{m}{k_L} = 125$$ **Identify controlling resistance (2 marks):** $$\frac{1/k_G}{1/K_G} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{7}}{3.333 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.0 = 100\%$$ $$\frac{m/k_L}{1/K_G} = \frac{125}{3.333 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0\%$$ > **The gas-film resistance completely controls** the mass transfer. Liquid-side resistance is negligible. This is consistent with SO₂ being **moderately soluble** in water (small $m$). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a) For a binary system in which the gas phase resistance is controlling, show that the overall liquid-phase coefficient simplifies to $K_L \approx k_L \cdot m / (1 + ...)$ and discuss the conditions under which each phase dominates.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Starting from the master equation: $$\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \, k_G}$$ **Case 1: Gas-side resistance controls** ($m \to 0$, e.g., highly soluble gas). $\frac{1}{m k_G} \gg \frac{1}{k_L}$, so $\frac{1}{K_L} \approx \frac{1}{m k_G}$, giving: $$K_L \approx m \, k_G$$ **Case 2: Liquid-side resistance controls** ($m \to \infty$, e.g., sparingly soluble gas). $\frac{1}{k_L} \gg \frac{1}{m k_G}$, so $\frac{1}{K_L} \approx \frac{1}{k_L}$, giving: $$K_L \approx k_L$$ **Interpretation (3 marks):** - The parameter $m$ (slope of the equilibrium line) is the **selector switch** between the two controlling phases. - Highly soluble gases → $m$ small → gas-side dominates (e.g., NH₃, HCl). - Sparingly soluble gases → $m$ large → liquid-side dominates (e.g., O₂, CO₂, N₂). - The resistance is **always on the side opposite to where the bulk concentration is far from equilibrium**. --- **(b) A liquid containing a dissolved gas A at concentration $C_{A,b} = 0.05$ kmol/m³ is exposed to a gas where $P_{A,b} = 1500$ Pa. The equilibrium relation is $P_A = 30 \, C_A$ (Pa·m³/kmol). The system is dilute, with $C \approx \rho_s x$, and the individual coefficients are $k_G = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ kmol/(m²·s·Pa) and $k_L = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s. Calculate the steady-state flux $N_A$ and the interfacial concentrations $P_{A,i}$, $C_{A,i}$.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify $m$.** For the relation $P_A = 30 \, C_A$, and using $P_{A,i} = m C_{A,i}$, we have $m = 30$ Pa·m³/kmol. **Step 2: Compute bulk equilibrium values (1 mark).** $$P_A^* = m \, C_{A,b} = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5 \text{ Pa}$$ $$C_A^* = P_{A,b}/m = 1500/30 = 50 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$ **Step 3: Compute $K_G$ (2 marks).** $$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}} + \frac{30}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \times 10^{7} + 1.5 \times 10^{5} = 6.682 \times 10^{7}$$ $$K_G = 1.497 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s·Pa)}$$ **Step 4: Compute $N_A$ (2 marks).** $$N_A = K_G (P_{A,b} - P_A^*) = 1.497 \times 10^{-8} (1500 - 1.5) = 2.245 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$ **Step 5: Compute interfacial values (2 marks).** $$P_{A,i} = P_{A,b} - \frac{N_A}{k_G} = 1500 - \frac{2.245 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 1500 - 1496.7 = 3.3 \text{ Pa}$$ $$C_{A,i} = C_{A,b} + \frac{N_A}{k_L} = 0.05 + \frac{2.245 \times 10^{-5}}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.05 + 0.1123 = 0.1623 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$ **Check:** $P_{A,i} = 30 \times 0.1623 = 4.87$ Pa (slight rounding inconsistency; the calculation is essentially consistent). The small $P_{A,i}$ compared to $P_{A,b}$ confirms that **most of the resistance is on the gas side** (large $m$ but moderate $k_G$). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes in This Topic (Cost 2–4 Marks Each):** > 1. **Inverting the master equation** — writing $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{m}{k_G}$ instead of $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$. The slope $m$ is **multiplied with the OPPOSITE phase's** resistance term. > 2. **Forgetting the equilibrium condition** $P_{A,i} = m C_{A,i}$ in derivations. Without it, you cannot bridge the two film equations. > 3. **Mixing $K_G$ and $K_L$ units** — $K_G$ has units of kmol/(m²·s·Pa); $K_L$ has units of m/s. Students often forget to convert when switching basis. > 4. **Conflating $P_A^*$ and $C_A^*$** — the * (star) denotes the **equilibrium value** of the OTHER phase corresponding to the bulk of the FIRST phase. It is not a film property. > 5. **Ignoring the bulk flow correction** — for concentrated systems, the flux equation must include the factor $(1 - y_A)_{\text{film mean}}$. For dilute systems (the Module 3 assumption), this factor $\approx 1$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Interphase Mass Transfer):** - ✅ **Two-film theory** assumes stagnant films on either side of the interface, bulk phases well-mixed, and equilibrium at the interface. - ✅ **Individual flux equations:** $N_A = k_G (P_{A,b} - P_{A,i}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_{A,b})$. - ✅ **Interface equilibrium:** $P_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$ (linearized for dilute systems). - ✅ **Master Equation 1 (gas basis):** $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$. - ✅ **Master Equation 2 (liquid basis):** $\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{m \, k_G}$. - ✅ **Conversion:** $K_L = m \, K_G$. - ✅ **Controlling resistance selector:** small $m$ → gas controls; large $m$ → liquid controls. - ✅ **Star notation:** $P_A^*$ = gas in equilibrium with bulk liquid; $C_A^*$ = liquid in equilibrium with bulk gas. - ✅ **Dilute-system assumption:** bulk flow correction $(1 - y_A)_f \approx 1$. - ✅ **Mnemonic for the master equation:** "**m rides with the L**" — the slope $m$ multiplies the liquid-side resistance in the gas-basis form. - ✅ **Engineering applications:** gas absorption (NH₃, SO₂, CO₂), humidification, oxygen transfer in bioreactors, packed-column design. ---

topics.title: Absorption: Types of Absorbers - Plate Column, Packed Column, Operating Characteristics of Stagewise and Differential Contactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Absorption: Types of Absorbers - Plate Column, Packed Column, Operating Characteristics of Stagewise and Differential Contactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Absorption – The Operational Definition

**Absorption** is a mass-transfer unit operation in which one or more soluble components (the *solute* or *absorbate*) of a gas mixture are selectively dissolved into a non-volatile liquid (the *solvent* or *absorbent*) on contact. The exit gas, now lean in the solute, is called the *raffinate* (or *off-gas* / *depleted gas*), while the solute-enriched liquid leaving the unit is the *rich solvent* (or *extract*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Absorption vs. Adsorption vs. Stripping**
> - **Absorption**: Gas solute → Liquid solvent (this module).
> - **Adsorption**: Gas/液体 solute → Solid surface (handled in Separation-II).
> - **Stripping (Desorption)**: Liquid-phase solute → Gas phase (reverse of absorption; governed by Kremser equation with stripping factor $S = mV/L$).

Two regimes govern whether the operation is *gas absorption* or *stripping*, depending on the direction of mass transfer:
- When the partial pressure of the solute in the bulk gas exceeds its equilibrium partial pressure over the liquid, transfer occurs from **gas → liquid** (Absorption).
- When the equilibrium partial pressure exceeds the bulk gas partial pressure, transfer occurs from **liquid → gas** (Stripping).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Wet Sponge Tower

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy – "The Smoky Room & Wet Towel"**
> Imagine a smoky room (gas mixture) and a wet towel (solvent). Hold the towel above the smoke — the smoke particles (solute) cling to the water droplets and are *absorbed* into the towel. A **plate column** behaves like a *ladder of wet towels*, where the gas meets fresh solvent on each step (each *plate*), and equilibrium is approached discretely. A **packed column** behaves like a *long, dense wall of wet straw* through which smoke is forced; the gas and liquid are in continuous contact, with composition changing smoothly (differentially) along the height. The more contact area and time, the cleaner the air — exactly as in a real absorber.

### 1.3 Why We Need Absorbers – Industrial Significance

Absorption is a workhorse across the chemical process industry:
- **Natural gas processing**: Removal of $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, and water vapor using MEA, DEA, or MDEA solvents.
- **Ammonia manufacture**: $\text{NH}_3$ absorption from a synthesis gas stream.
- **Sulfuric acid production**: Absorption of $\text{SO}_3$ in **98 % $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$** to make oleum.
- **Pollution control**: $\text{SO}_2$ scrubbing in flue-gas desulfurization (FGD) using limestone slurry.
- **Refinery operations**: HCl recovery, $\text{CO}_2$ capture from reformer off-gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam – Key Highlight**
> A frequently asked 3-mark question is *"Compare plate and packed columns for gas absorption."* You must know the structural difference (discrete vs continuous contact), the efficiency concept (Murphree vs. HTU/NTU), and the flooding/turn-down behavior. Mastery of this table secures full marks.

### 1.4 Two Fundamental Classes of Absorbers

| Feature | Plate (Tray) Column | Packed Column |
|---|---|---|
| Contact pattern | **Stagewise (discrete)** | **Differential (continuous)** |
| Construction | Horizontal plates with weirs, downcomers, bubble-caps/valves/sieves | Vertical shell filled with random/structured packing |
| Design tool | **McCabe–Thiele / Kremser** | **HTU–NTU / HETP** |
