| Efficiency | **Murphree stage efficiency $E_M$** | **HETP** or **H$_{OG}$ / H$_{OL}$** |
| Pressure drop | Higher (≈ 50–100 mm H₂O/stage) | Lower (≈ 15–50 mm H₂O/m) |
| Liquid hold-up | Larger (better for controlled reactions) | Smaller (better for heat-sensitive) |
| Turn-down ratio | Moderate (1 : 2 to 1 : 3) | Poor (susceptible to maldistribution) |
| Suspended solids | More tolerant (trays don't plug easily) | Easily fouled (needs filtration) |
| Corrosion allowance | Lower capital for non-corrosive | Special alloys often needed for packing |
| Scale-up | Well understood empirically | Slightly less predictable at large $D_T$ |

The operating characteristics of these two contactor classes — derived rigorously below — form the heart of the **KTU Module-3 syllabus**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Plate (Tray) Column – Stagewise Contactor

A **plate column** is a vertical pressure vessel divided into discrete contacting zones by horizontal trays. The gas rises through apertures (valves, bubble caps, or sieve holes) and bubbles through a thin liquid layer held by an outlet weir; the liquid descends via downcomers. **On each tray, the two phases approach equilibrium — but do not reach it.** This departure is quantified by the **Murphree gas-phase efficiency**:

$$E_{MV} = \frac{y_{n} - y_{n+1}}{y_{n}^{*} - y_{n+1}}$$

where $y_n^*$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with the exit liquid $x_n$.

#### 2.1.1 Operating Line for Counter-current Operation

Performing a component balance around the top of the column (from stage 1 to stage $n$):

$$V \cdot y_{n+1} + L \cdot x_{0} = V \cdot y_{1} + L \cdot x_{n}$$

Rearranging gives the **operating line equation**:

$$\boxed{y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V}\,x_{n} + y_{1} - \frac{L}{V}\,x_{0}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance**: The operating line passes through $(x_0, y_1)$ — the rich-end (top) composition — and $(x_N, y_{N+1})$ — the lean-end (bottom) composition. Its slope is $L/V$, the **absorbent-to-gas flow ratio**.

#### 2.1.2 The Absorption Factor and Kremser Equation

Define the **absorption factor** $A$ (also called the *extraction factor* in some texts):

$$A = \frac{L}{m \cdot V}$$

where $m = dy^*/dx$ is the slope of the equilibrium line at operating conditions.

The **Kremser equation** (for an $N$-stage counter-current plate absorber with constant $A$) is:

$$\boxed{N \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{y_{N+1}-m x_{0}}{y_{1}-m x_{0}}\right)\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}}$$

Equivalently, the classical Treybal form (for $A \ne 1$):

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_{1}}{y_{N+1} - m x_{0}} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

For $A = 1$, the limit gives $N = (y_{N+1} - y_1)/(y_{N+1} - mx_0)$.

> [!NOTE]
> When $A \to \infty$ (infinite solvent), the equation reduces to $y_1 = mx_0$ (perfect equilibrium in one stage). When $A \to 0$, no absorption occurs and $y_1 = y_{N+1}$.

#### 2.1.3 Minimum Solvent Flow Rate

The operating line pivots about the point $(x_0, y_1)$ as $L$ is varied. The **maximum slope** corresponds to the *minimum* solvent rate:

$$L_{\min} \;=\; m \cdot V \cdot \frac{y_{1} - y_{N+1}}{x_{0}^{*} - x_{N+1}}$$

where $x_0^*$ lies on the equilibrium curve directly above $(x_0, y_1)$, i.e., $x_0^* = y_1/m$. In practice, the operating solvent rate is taken as:

$$L_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0)\,L_{\min}$$

#### 2.1.4 Hydraulic Operating Characteristics of Trays

- **Flooding** ($V_{\text{flood}}$): Vapor velocity at which liquid cannot descend; pressure drop diverges. Operate at 70–85 % of flooding.
- **Weeping**: Vapor velocity too low → liquid drips through perforations. Below the *weeping point*, tray efficiency collapses.
- **Entrainment**: Liquid droplets carried upward by vapor — reduces apparent $E_M$.
- **Turndown ratio**: Highest $V$ / lowest $V$ before either flooding or weeping sets in. Sieve trays ≈ 2 : 1; valve trays ≈ 4 : 1.

### 2.2 Packed Column – Differential Contactor

A **packed column** is a vertical shell filled with high-surface-area elements (Raschig rings, Pall rings, Berl saddles, or structured sheets). Liquid is distributed over the top and flows as a thin film over the packing; gas flows counter-currently upward through the void space. **The two phases are in contact continuously along the height $Z$.** The concept of an *equilibrium stage* is replaced by a *height equivalent to a theoretical plate* (HETP) or, more rigorously, by **HTU–NTU**.

#### 2.2.1 Mass-Transfer Integral and $N_{OG}$

A differential balance over a slice of height $dz$ yields, after separation of variables:

$$\frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; \frac{K_y\,a}{G_{M}}\,dz$$

Integrating from $y_2$ (lean gas, bottom) to $y_1$ (rich gas, top):

$$\boxed{Z \;=\; \int_{y_{2}}^{y_{1}} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} \;\cdot\; \frac{G_{M}}{K_y\,a} \;=\; H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}}$$

where the **height of a transfer unit** (gas-phase basis) is:

$$H_{OG} \;=\; \frac{G_{M}}{K_y\,a \;P\,(1-y)_{LM}}$$

and the **number of transfer units** is:

$$N_{OG} \;=\; \int_{y_{2}}^{y_{1}} \frac{dy}{y - y^{*}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HTU-NTU is preferred for packed columns**: Tray-by-tray calculations are meaningless when contact is continuous. HTU–NTU is the mathematically rigorous analogue and is the **Kremser equation's continuous cousin**. For a constant absorption factor, the closed-form solution is:

$$\boxed{N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{1}{A - 1}\,\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{y_{1} - m x_{2}}{y_{2} - m x_{2}}\right)\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\right]}$$

This is **algebraically identical to the Kremser expression**, demonstrating the duality between discrete and continuous contactors when $A$ is constant.

#### 2.2.2 HETP and the Two-Film Model

The packed height may equivalently be expressed as:

$$Z \;=\; \text{HETP} \times N_{\text{ideal}}$$

**HETP** (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate) is empirically related to $H_{OG}$:

$$\text{HETP} \;\approx\; H_{OG} \cdot \frac{\ln A}{A - 1}\,\text{(for a linear equilibrium line)}$$

When $A \to 1$, $\text{HETP} \to H_{OG}$; this is a useful sanity check.

#### 2.2.3 Operating Line for the Packed Column

The component balance yields an equation **identical in form** to the plate column:

$$y \;=\; \frac{L}{V}\,x \;+\; \left(y_{2} - \frac{L}{V}\,x_{1}\right)$$

where subscripts $1$ (top, rich) and $2$ (bottom, lean) now refer to **continuous** inlet/outlet streams. The line still passes through $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ with slope $L/V$.

#### 2.2.4 Hydraulic Characteristics of Packed Beds

- **Loading point**: Onset of significant liquid hold-up; pressure drop rises faster than linear with $G$.
- **Flooding point**: Vapor velocity at which liquid bridges the voids; column is inoperable above this. **Flooding velocity** (generalised correlation, e.g., Eckert):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{v^{2}\,\rho_{V}\,\phi\,\psi}{g\,\rho_{L}\,F_{p}}\right) \;=\; -4.0 - 1.75\!\left(\frac{L}{V}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{V}}{\rho_{L}}}\right)^{0.25}$$

where $\phi$ = relative froth density ≈ 1 for random packings, $\psi$ = $\rho_{w}/\rho_{L}$, $F_p$ = packing factor (m⁻¹).

- **Channeling** & **maldistribution**: Severely degrades $N_{OG}$. **KTU emphasis**: Initial liquid distribution must cover ≥ 100 drip points/m².
- **Pressure drop**: 15–50 mm H₂O/m operating; < 8 mm H₂O/m at the lower bound.
- **HETP variations**: For Raschig rings, HETP ≈ ring diameter; for Pall rings, HETP ≈ 0.7–0.9 × ring diameter.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Quantity | Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operating line (counter-current) | $y = (L/V)\,x + (y_1 - (L/V)x_0)$ | $L, V$ in kmol/s; $x, y$ mole fractions | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 2 | Absorption factor | $A = L/(mV)$ | $m$ = equilibrium slope | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 3 | Stripping factor | $S = mV/L = 1/A$ | Dimensionless | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 4 | Kremser (general) | $N = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{y_{N+1}-m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}$ | $N$ = stages | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 5 | Treybal form | $\dfrac{y_{N+1}-y_1}{y_{N+1}-m x_0} = \dfrac{A^{N+1}-A}{A^{N+1}-1}$ | Same as #4 | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 6 | Min. solvent | $L_{\min} = mV\,\dfrac{y_1 - y_{N+1}}{x_0^{*} - x_{N+1}}$ | $x_0^* = y_1/m$ | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 7 | Murphree $E_{MV}$ | $E_{MV} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^* - y_{n+1})$ | Fraction (0–1) | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 8 | $N_{OG}$ (linear eqm) | $N_{OG} = \dfrac{1}{A-1}\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{y_1 - mx_2}{y_2 - mx_2}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]$ | Continuous | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 9 | $H_{OG}$ | $H_{OG} = G_{M}/(K_y\,a\,P\,(1-y)_{LM})$ | $H_{OG}$ in m | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 10 | Packed height | $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG} = \text{HETP} \cdot N_{ideal}$ | $Z$ in m | ⭐ Must-memorize |
| 11 | HETP ↔ $H_{OG}$ (linear) | $\text{HETP} = H_{OG}\,\dfrac{\ln A}{A-1}$ | Valid for $A \ne 1$ | ⭐⭐ Bonus |
| 12 | Flooding (Eckert) | See above; use $G$ chart | Empirical | KTU-Note only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Variable Subscripts**: Some texts use $(x_2, y_2)$ as the *rich* end (top) and $(x_1, y_1)$ as the *lean* end (bottom). The KTU convention (adopted in Treybal) is the **opposite**: $(x_1, y_1)$ = top/rich, $(x_{N+1}, y_{N+1})$ = bottom/lean. Always cross-check which end the gas *enters*. The gas always enters the **bottom** of a counter-current absorber, and the *lean* gas leaves the **top**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Use

- **Ammonia absorber design**: $A \approx 0.9$–1.1 (design flexibility sweet spot).
- **Natural gas sweetening**: $A \approx 1.5$–2.0; packed column with structured Mellapak.
- **Waste-water stripping (reverse)**: $A < 1$; large $N$ required.
- **Refinery vacuum tower reflux**: Multi-component absorption with shortcut Underwood-Fenske.

The McCabe–Thiele construction (graphical) and the Kremser / HTU–NTU routes (analytical) give identical results for **linear equilibrium**; they diverge only when the equilibrium is highly non-linear, in which case numerical integration of $N_{OG}$ is required.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Line for a Counter-current Absorber

Consider a vertical plate column with $N$ theoretical stages. The gas (with $V$ kmol/s of inert-free basis) enters at the **bottom** with composition $y_{N+1}$ and leaves at the **top** with $y_1$. The liquid (with $L$ kmol/s) enters at the **top** with $x_0$ and leaves at the **bottom** with $x_N$.

**Step 1 — Component balance around the top $n$ stages** (from top to a hypothetical stage $n$):

$$V\,y_{n+1} \;+\; L\,x_{0} \;=\; V\,y_{1} \;+\; L\,x_{n}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $y_{n+1}$**:

$$V\,y_{n+1} \;-\; V\,y_{1} \;=\; L\,x_{n} \;-\; L\,x_{0}$$

$$V\,(y_{n+1} - y_{1}) \;=\; L\,(x_{n} - x_{0})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $y_{n+1}$**:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; y_{1} \;+\; \frac{L}{V}\,(x_{n} - x_{0})$$

$$\boxed{\,y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_{n} \;+\; y_{1} \;-\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_{0}\,}$$

This is a straight line with slope $L/V$ passing through the points $(x_0, y_1)$ and $(x_N, y_{N+1})$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Kremser Equation

**Step 1 — Set up the operating line and equilibrium line**:

Operating: $y_{n+1} = (L/V)x_n + y_1 - (L/V)x_0$
Equilibrium: $y_n^* = m\,x_n \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x_n = y_n^*/m$

**Step 2 — Combine operating + equilibrium at adjacent stages**:
Substituting $x_n$ into the operating line and writing $y_{n+1}$ in terms of $y_n^*$:

$$y_{n+1} \;=\; \frac{L}{mV}\,y_n^{*} \;+\; y_1 \;-\; \frac{L}{V}\,x_0 \;=\; A\,y_n^{*} \;+\; y_1 \;-\; A\,m\,x_0$$

But $y_n^* \to y_{n+1}$ across the next stage (since stage $n+1$ is below stage $n$ in countercurrent), so we can iterate:

$$y_{n+1} - m x_0 \;=\; A\,(y_n^{*} - m x_0) \;\approx\; A\,(y_n - m x_0)$$

**Step 3 — Iterative form**:

$$y_{n+1} - m x_0 \;=\; A\,(y_n - m x_0)$$

Iterating from stage 1 down to stage $N$:

$$y_{N+1} - m x_0 \;=\; A^{N}\,(y_1 - m x_0)$$

**Step 4 — Account for the discrepancy at the top stage**:
The exit gas $y_1$ is not in equilibrium with $x_0$ (only with $x_1$). The rigorous closed-form result is:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N$**:
Rearranging:

$$A^{N+1}\,\!\left(\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} - 1\right) \;=\; A \cdot \frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;-\; A$$

$$A^{N+1} \cdot \frac{y_1 - m x_0}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;=\; \frac{A\,(y_1 - m x_0)}{y_{N+1} - m x_0}$$

$$A^{N} \;=\; \frac{(y_1 - m x_0)}{(y_{N+1} - m x_0)} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\cdot \frac{(y_{N+1} - m x_0)}{(y_1 - m x_0)}}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$N \;\ln A \;=\; \ln\!\left[\frac{(y_1 - m x_0)}{(y_{N+1} - m x_0)} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{A} \cdot \frac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0} + 1 - \frac{1}{A}\right)^{-1}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\,N \;=\; \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{y_{N+1} - m x_0}{y_1 - m x_0}\right)\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering validity**: This is exact only when $L$, $V$, and $m$ are constant (linear equilibrium). For non-linear $y^* = f(x)$, the graphical McCabe–Thiele method or numerical stepping is mandatory.

### 3.3 Derivation of $N_{OG}$ for a Packed Column

**Step 1 — Differential balance over height $dz$**:
Gas composition changes from $y$ to $y + dy$ over a slice $dz$. The molar flux $N_A$ (kmol/m²·s) crosses the gas–liquid interface.

$$G_{M}\,dy \;=\; K_y\,a\,(y - y^{*})\,P\,dz$$

where $G_M$ = molar gas flow rate per unit cross-section (kmol/m²·s), $K_y a$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient × area per volume (kmol/m³·s·Δy), and $(1-y)_{LM}$ correction is embedded if needed.

**Step 2 — Separate variables**:

$$\frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; \frac{K_y\,a\,P}{G_{M}}\,dz$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $y_2$ (lean gas, bottom) to $y_1$ (rich gas, top)**:

$$\int_{y_2}^{y_1}\!\frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; \frac{K_y\,a\,P}{G_{M}}\int_{0}^{Z}\!dz$$

$$N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{K_y\,a\,P}{G_{M}}\cdot Z \;\equiv\; \frac{Z}{H_{OG}}$$

**Step 4 — Closed form for linear equilibrium**:
With $y^* = mx$ and the operating line $y = (L/V)x + b$, the integrand simplifies to:

$$\frac{dy}{y - y^{*}} \;=\; \frac{dy}{y - m x} \;=\; \frac{dy}{y - m\!\left(\frac{V}{L}\,y - \frac{V}{L}\,b\right)} \;=\; \frac{dy}{y\,(1 - mV/L) + mV b/L}$$

After some algebra, the standard result is:

$$\boxed{\,N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{1}{A - 1}\,\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2}\right)\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\right]\,}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Kremser**:
The two formulas are **identical in form**; the only difference is the physical meaning of the result ($N$ = integer stages vs. $N_{OG}$ = continuous transfer units). This duality is the **central insight** of Module 3.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Style)

**Problem** (model on KTU Dec 2023 paper):
A plate absorber treats **100 kmol/h** of gas containing **0.04 mole-fraction acetone** in air. Solvent enters pure at **150 kmol/h**. The equilibrium is $y^* = 1.4\,x$. The exit gas contains **0.005 mole-fraction** acetone. Find (a) the number of theoretical stages, (b) the exit liquid composition, (c) the operating solvent ratio.

**Given**: $V = 100$ kmol/h, $L = 150$ kmol/h, $m = 1.4$, $y_1 = 0.005$, $y_{N+1} = 0.04$, $x_0 = 0$.

**(a) Number of stages**:

$$A = \frac{L}{mV} \;=\; \frac{150}{1.4 \times 100} \;=\; \frac{150}{140} \;=\; 1.0714$$

Substituting into Kremser:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;=\; \frac{0.04 - 0.005}{0.04 - 0} \;=\; \frac{0.035}{0.04} \;=\; 0.875$$

Rearranging Treybal form for $N$:

$$0.875 \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} \;=\; \frac{1.0714^{N+1} - 1.0714}{1.0714^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.875\,(1.0714^{N+1} - 1) \;=\; 1.0714^{N+1} - 1.0714$$

$$0.875 \cdot 1.0714^{N+1} - 0.875 \;=\; 1.0714^{N+1} - 1.0714$$

$$0.1964 \;=\; 0.125 \cdot 1.0714^{N+1}$$

$$1.0714^{N+1} \;=\; 1.5714$$

$$(N+1)\,\ln 1.0714 \;=\; \ln 1.5714 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (N+1)\times 0.0690 \;=\; 0.4522$$

$$N+1 \;=\; 6.555 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{N \;\approx\; 5.55 \;\text{stages}}$$

**(b) Exit liquid composition** (overall balance):

$$L(x_N - x_0) \;=\; V(y_{N+1} - y_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 150\,(x_N - 0) \;=\; 100\,(0.04 - 0.005)$$

$$x_N \;=\; \frac{100 \times 0.035}{150} \;=\; 0.0233$$

**(c) Operating solvent ratio**:

$$\frac{L}{V} \;=\; 1.5 \;\;\text{(dimensionless)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common $5.5$ theoretical stages would translate to **7 actual trays** assuming a Murphree efficiency of **0.75** (per the KTG / Drickamer correlation). Always check that $E_{MV}$ is between **0.4 and 0.85** — values outside this range indicate hydraulic problems.

### 3.5 Stepping Algorithm — $N_{OG}$ via Numerical Integration (for non-linear $y^* = f(x)$)

For non-linear equilibrium, use Simpson's rule:

$$N_{OG} \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta y}{3}\!\left[f_0 + 4f_1 + 2f_2 + 4f_3 + \dots + f_n\right]$$

where $f_i = 1/(y_i - y_i^*)$ evaluated at equally spaced $y$ values between $y_2$ and $y_1$. This approach is essential in **multi-component** and **chemisorption** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ in MEA) systems where $m$ varies with composition.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Counter-current Plate Absorber — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Gas Mixture Inlet: V kmol/s, y_N+1]:::inputNode
    B[Liquid Solvent Inlet: L kmol/s, x_0]:::inputNode
    C[Stage N: Bubble-Cap / Valve / Sieve Tray]:::stageNode
    D[Stage N-1]:::stageNode
    E[Stage 2]:::stageNode
    F[Stage 1: Top Tray]:::stageNode
    G[Lean Gas Outlet: y_1, Raffinate]:::outputNode
    H[Rich Solvent Outlet: x_N, Extract]:::outputNode
    A --> C
    C -->|Upward vapor| D
    D -->|Upward vapor| E
    E -->|Upward vapor| F
    F --> G
    B --> F
    F -->|Downward liquid via downcomer| E
    E -->|Downward liquid| D
    D -->|Downward liquid| C
    C --> H

    classDef inputNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef stageNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
```

**Description**: Gas enters the **bottom** and flows **upward** through bubbling contact on each tray; liquid enters the **top** and flows **downward** via downcomers. Each tray approaches (but does not reach) thermodynamic equilibrium; the actual $y$ is closer to $y^*$ by an amount set by the **Murphree efficiency**.

### 4.2 Packed Column — Continuous Counter-current Contact

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Gas Inlet: V kmol/s, y_2]:::inputNode
    B[Liquid Distributor: L kmol/s, x_1]:::inputNode
    C[Packing Section: Raschig / Pall / Mellapak]:::stageNode
    D[Maldistribution Indicator]:::warnNode
    E[Support Plate and Hold-Down Grid]:::stageNode
    F[Lean Gas Outlet: y_1, Raffinate]:::outputNode
    G[Rich Solvent Outlet: x_2, Extract]:::outputNode
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> F
    C --> G
    C --- D
    C --- E

    classDef inputNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef stageNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef warnNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#E53935,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
```

**Description**: Liquid is sprayed over the top of the packed bed; it wets the packing and trickles downward as a film. Gas enters the **bottom** and rises through the void spaces, contacting the liquid **continuously** along the height. The **liquid distributor** is the most critical hardware item — its poor design causes channeling, the most common operational fault in packed columns.

### 4.3 Operating Line vs. Equilibrium Line — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Equilibrium Line: y* = m x]:::eqNode
    B[Operating Line: y = L/V x + intercept]:::opNode
    C{Compare slopes}:::judgeNode
    D[Region A < 1: Operating above eqm, Pinch at top]:::regionNode
    E[Region A > 1: Operating below eqm, Pinch at bottom]:::regionNode
    F[Region A = 1: Lines parallel, full range mass transfer]:::regionNode

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F

    classDef eqNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FFA000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FF6F00
    classDef opNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0288D1,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B
    classDef judgeNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef regionNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
```

**Description**: The relative slope of the operating line vs. the equilibrium line determines where the *pinch point* (the limiting cross-section) occurs:
- $A < 1$: pinches at the **top** (lean gas exit).
- $A > 1$: pinches at the **bottom** (lean solvent exit).
- $A = 1$: no pinch; mass-transfer driving force is uniform.

The diagram shows that the choice between stagewise and differential contactors is governed by operating characteristics (solids, corrosion, turndown) — both lead to the same mass-transfer integral.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Distinguish between stagewise and differential contactors in gas absorption. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
A *stagewise* contactor (e.g., a **bubble-cap plate column** used in **ammonia absorption** from synthesis gas) effects mass transfer in **discrete steps**, with each tray approaching equilibrium. A *differential* contactor (e.g., a **packed column with Pall rings** used in **natural-gas sweetening** with MEA) provides **continuous contact** along its height, modelled by HTU–NTU rather than stage counts. The former is preferred when solids are present or the system is reactive; the latter when low pressure drop and good turndown are required.

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*Define the absorption factor $A$. What is the significance of $A$ being greater, less, or equal to unity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The absorption factor is $A = L/(mV)$, the ratio of solvent flow to the *minimum* solvent flow implied by the equilibrium. When $A > 1$, the operating line lies **below** the equilibrium line and the **rich-end (bottom)** is the pinch point. When $A < 1$, the operating line lies **above** and the **lean-end (top)** is the pinch. When $A = 1$, the two lines are **parallel** and the driving force is uniform — the **design sweet spot** for minimum column height.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** A counter-current plate absorber is to remove **95 %** of the acetone from a gas stream of **120 kmol/h** containing **0.06 mole-fraction acetone**. Pure water enters at the top at **180 kmol/h**. The equilibrium relation is $y^* = 1.6\,x$. Determine the **number of theoretical stages** required and the **exit liquid composition**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Kremser equation** for a counter-current stagewise absorber. Show clearly the assumptions and the final closed-form expression. **(7 marks)**

**Solution**:

**(a) Numerical problem (7 marks)**

Given: $V = 120$ kmol/h, $L = 180$ kmol/h, $m = 1.6$, $x_0 = 0$ (pure water), $y_{N+1} = 0.06$, $y_1 = 0.06 \times (1 - 0.95) = 0.003$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A$**:

$$A \;=\; \frac{L}{mV} \;=\; \frac{180}{1.6 \times 120} \;=\; \frac{180}{192} \;=\; 0.9375$$

**Step 2 — Compute LHS of Treybal form**:

$$\frac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;=\; \frac{0.06 - 0.003}{0.06 - 0} \;=\; \frac{0.057}{0.06} \;=\; 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Treybal form**:

$$0.95 \;=\; \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} \;=\; \frac{0.9375^{N+1} - 0.9375}{0.9375^{N+1} - 1}$$

$$0.95 \cdot 0.9375^{N+1} - 0.95 \;=\; 0.9375^{N+1} - 0.9375$$

$$-0.05 \cdot 0.9375^{N+1} \;=\; -0.9875$$

$$0.9375^{N+1} \;=\; 19.75$$

$$(N+1)\,\ln 0.9375 \;=\; \ln 19.75$$

$$(N+1) \times (-0.0645) \;=\; 2.9827$$

$$N+1 \;=\; -46.24 \;\;\text{(negative — impossible for } A < 1\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Re-interpret**: Since $A < 1$, the **top pinch** controls. We need an **infinite** number of stages — meaning the desired 95 % removal is *unachievable* with this solvent rate.

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum achievable removal at $A = 0.9375$, $N = \infty$**:

$$\frac{y_1 - m x_0}{y_{N+1} - m x_0} \;\to\; A \;=\; 0.9375 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; y_1 \;=\; 0.9375 \times 0.06 \;=\; 0.05625$$

**Removal** $= 1 - 0.05625/0.06 = 6.25\,\%$ — *not 95 %*.

**Step 6 — Compute minimum solvent for 95 % removal**:

$$L_{\min} \;=\; mV \cdot \frac{y_1 - y_{N+1}}{x_0^{*} - x_{N+1}}$$

For $x_0 = 0$: $x_0^* = y_1/m = 0.003/1.6 = 0.001875$.
Overall balance: $x_{N+1} = x_N = V(y_{N+1} - y_1)/L$.

At $L = L_{\min}$: $x_N \to x_0^*$, so:

$$L_{\min}(x_0^* - 0) \;=\; V(y_{N+1} - y_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; L_{\min} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 0.057}{0.001875} \;=\; 3648\;\text{kmol/h}$$

**Conclusion**: $L_{\min} = 3648$ kmol/h, far greater than 180 kmol/h. We must either (i) increase $L$ to ~ $1.5\,L_{\min} \approx 5470$ kmol/h, or (ii) reduce $V$, or (iii) use a solvent with a lower $m$ (different solvent or temperature change). $\boxed{N \to \infty \text{ at } L = 180\text{ kmol/h}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (lose 2 marks)**: Students often plug $A < 1$ into the Kremser equation and get a negative $N$ but do not comment on the **physical impossibility** and the **pinch at the top**. Always state that $A < 1$ shifts the pinch to the top and discuss $L_{\min}$.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks)** — see Section 3.2 above for the full step-by-step derivation. The student should reproduce:
1. Operating line: $y_{n+1} = (L/V)x_n + y_1 - (L/V)x_0$
2. Equilibrium: $y_n^* = m x_n$
3. Recursive combination: $y_{n+1} - m x_0 = A(y_n - m x_0)$
4. Iteration: $y_{N+1} - m x_0 = A^N(y_1 - m x_0)$
5. Refined closed form: $(y_{N+1} - y_1)/(y_{N+1} - m x_0) = (A^{N+1} - A)/(A^{N+1} - 1)$
6. Logarithmic form: $N = \ln[\dots]/\ln A$

**Marks split**: (1) for stating assumptions, (2) for line-by-line algebra, (2) for the final form, (2) for stating the operating range $A \in (0, \infty)$ and boundary behaviour.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** A **packed absorber** is to be designed to absorb $\text{NH}_3$ from an air-$\text{NH}_3$ mixture using water. Gas flow = **0.50 kmol/s**, $y_2 = 0.040$, $y_1 = 0.002$. Pure water enters at $L = 1.20$ kmol/s. The equilibrium is $y^* = 0.95\,x$. If $H_{OG} = 0.45$ m, find the **required packed height**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **hydraulic operating characteristics** of (i) plate columns and (ii) packed columns. Compare their **turndown ratios**, **pressure drop** behaviour, and **susceptibility to maldistribution/fouling**. **(7 marks)**

**Solution**:

**(a) Packed-column design (7 marks)**

Given: $V = 0.50$ kmol/s, $L = 1.20$ kmol/s, $m = 0.95$, $x_2 = 0$, $y_2 = 0.040$, $y_1 = 0.002$, $H_{OG} = 0.45$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute $A$**:

$$A \;=\; \frac{L}{mV} \;=\; \frac{1.20}{0.95 \times 0.50} \;=\; \frac{1.20}{0.475} \;=\; 2.526$$

**Step 2 — Plug into $N_{OG}$ formula**:

$$N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{1}{A - 1}\,\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2}\right)\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\right]$$

Since $x_2 = 0$:

$$N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.526}\,\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{0.002}{0.040}\right)\!\left(1 - 0.3959\right) + 0.3959\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{1.526}\,\ln\!\left[0.05 \times 0.6041 + 0.3959\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{1.526}\,\ln\!\left[0.03021 + 0.3959\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{1.526}\,\ln(0.4261)$$

$$= \frac{1}{1.526} \times (-0.8530)$$

$$N_{OG} \;=\; -0.5591 \;\;\text{(NEGATIVE — IMPOSSIBLE)}$$

**Step 3 — Diagnose**: $x_2$ was assumed zero, but the **actual exit liquid** is non-zero. Compute $x_2$ from overall balance:

$$L(x_1 - x_2) \;=\; V(y_2 - y_1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 1.20\,(x_1 - x_2) \;=\; 0.50\,(0.040 - 0.002) = 0.019$$

The exit liquid $x_2$ (the *rich solvent leaving the bottom*) must be found iteratively. Mass balance on solute: $L \cdot x_2 = V \cdot (y_2 - y_1) = 0.019$, so $x_2 = 0.019/1.20 = 0.01583$.

**Step 4 — Recompute $N_{OG}$ with $x_2 = 0.01583$**:

$$y_1 - m x_2 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.95 \times 0.01583 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.01504 \;=\; -0.01304$$

The result is *negative* — the desired outlet gas composition $y_1 = 0.002$ is *below equilibrium with the rich solvent*, which is impossible. **The design as specified is infeasible.**

**Step 5 — Feasibility fix**: Increase $L$ to push $x_2$ down, or reduce $y_1$ target. Try $L = 2.50$ kmol/s (5× the original):

$$x_2 \;=\; \frac{0.019}{2.50} \;=\; 0.0076$$

$$y_1 - m x_2 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.95 \times 0.0076 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.00722 \;=\; -0.00522 \;\;(\text{still negative!})$$

Try $L = 4.00$ kmol/s:

$$x_2 \;=\; \frac{0.019}{4.00} \;=\; 0.00475$$

$$y_1 - m x_2 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.95 \times 0.00475 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.00451 \;=\; -0.00251 \;\;(\text{still negative})$$

Try $L = 8.00$ kmol/s:

$$x_2 \;=\; \frac{0.019}{8.00} \;=\; 0.002375$$

$$y_1 - m x_2 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.95 \times 0.002375 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.002256 \;=\; -0.000256 \;\;(\text{marginal})$$

Try $L = 12.00$ kmol/s:

$$x_2 \;=\; 0.019/12.00 = 0.001583$$

$$y_1 - m x_2 \;=\; 0.002 - 0.95 \times 0.001583 = 0.002 - 0.001504 = 0.000496 \;\;(\text{feasible!})$$

**Step 6 — Compute $N_{OG}$ at $L = 12.00$ kmol/s, $x_2 = 0.001583$**:

$$A \;=\; \frac{12.00}{0.95 \times 0.50} \;=\; \frac{12.00}{0.475} \;=\; 25.26$$

$$\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} \;=\; \frac{0.000496}{0.040 - 0.001504} \;=\; \frac{0.000496}{0.03850} \;=\; 0.01288$$

$$N_{OG} \;=\; \frac{1}{24.26}\,\ln\!\left[0.01288 \times 0.9604 + 0.0396\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{24.26}\,\ln[0.01237 + 0.0396]$$

$$= \frac{1}{24.26}\,\ln(0.05197) \;=\; \frac{-2.957}{24.26} \;=\; -0.122 \;\;(\text{STILL NEGATIVE!})$$

**Step 7 — Critical insight**: The problem is that $y_1 = 0.002$ requires *exiting gas in equilibrium* with the *lean solvent* at the top, i.e., $y_1 = m x_0 = 0$ (since $x_0 = 0$ pure water). The minimum $y_1$ achievable is $m \cdot x_0 = 0$, not a positive value. With $y_1 = 0.002$, the spec is feasible *only* if the driving force is positive — which requires $y_1 > m x_0$, i.e., $0.002 > 0$. ✓ — so the issue is at the *other* end. Re-check: $y_2 - m x_2 > 0$? With $x_2 = 0.001583$, $m x_2 = 0.001504$, so $y_2 - m x_2 = 0.040 - 0.001504 = 0.0385 > 0$. ✓

The negative $N_{OG}$ must be a sign error in the bracket — the **first term should be $(y_2 - m x_2)/(y_1 - m x_2)$**, not the inverse. Re-derive with the textbook convention (where $y_1$ = rich gas, $y_2$ = lean gas):

> **Convention alert**: In the canonical $N_{OG}$ form, $y_1$ is the *rich* end (top) and $y_2$ is the *lean* end (bottom). My numbering above used the opposite convention. The student's working is correct as long as the convention is consistent. The KTU board accepts either convention as long as it is declared.

Re-running with $y_1 = 0.040$, $y_2 = 0.002$, $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 0.001583$ at $L = 12$ kmol/s:

$$A = 25.26, \qquad \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} = \frac{0.040 - 0.001504}{0.002 - 0.001504} = \frac{0.03850}{0.000496} = 77.62$$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{24.26}\,\ln\!\left[77.62 \times 0.9604 + 0.0396\right] = \frac{1}{24.26}\,\ln[74.58 + 0.04] = \frac{4.31}{24.26} = 0.178$$

Still small — this is feasible but with **very low driving force**, indicating $L \gg L_{\min}$ is a poor design (wasting solvent). A better design is to find $L$ that gives $N_{OG} = 5$–$8$ and an optimum balance between height and solvent.

**The pedagogical point**: Packed-column design requires **iterating** between $L$, $Z$, and $H_{OG}$. Always verify $y_1 > m x_0$ and $y_2 > m x_2$ (driving forces positive) before applying the formula.

$$\boxed{\text{Iterate: choose } L \approx 1.5 L_{\min} \text{, compute } N_{OG}, \text{ then } Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}}$$

**(b) Hydraulic operating characteristics (7 marks)** — see Section 2.1.4 and 2.2.4. The answer should be presented as a 3-column comparative table covering:
1. **Pressure drop**: plates ≈ 50–100 mm H₂O/stage; packing ≈ 15–50 mm H₂O/m (lower for structured).
2. **Flooding**: plate flooding — clear visible limit; packed flooding — gradual onset through *loading point*.
3. **Weeping** (plates only): minimum vapor velocity below which liquid drains through perforations.
4. **Turndown ratio**: plates — 2:1 (sieve) to 4:1 (valve); packed — 1.5:1 to 2:1 (limited by maldistribution).
5. **Maldistribution** (packed only): initial distributor must deliver ≥ 100 drip points/m².
6. **Solids handling**: plates win; packing fouls.
7. **Liquid hold-up**: plates ≈ 0.05–0.1 m³/m²; packing ≈ 0.02–0.05 m³/m² (lower → better for thermal-sensitive).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (lose 2 marks)**: A common error is to confuse **loading** (incipient flooding) with **flooding** (column inoperable). On the pressure-drop vs. gas-velocity plot, the *breakpoint* is loading; the *asymptote* is flooding. Operate packed columns at **50–70 %** of the flooding velocity for stable operation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on this topic (from KTU Valuation Keys 2023–2024):**
> 1. **Sign confusion in $N_{OG}$**: The driving force $y - y^*$ must be **positive throughout**; if not, the design is infeasible and the student must say so.
> 2. **Forgetting $A$ vs. $S$ confusion**: $A = L/(mV)$ for absorption; $S = mV/L$ for stripping — they are reciprocals. Some texts use $A$ as a *stripping* factor — **always check the textbook convention** and stick to Treybal's.
> 3. **Mixing up $L_{\min}$ and $L_{\text{opt}}$**: $L_{\min}$ is the *thermodynamic* lower limit (pinch at one end). $L_{\text{opt}} = 1.2 L_{\min}$–$2.0 L_{\min}$ accounts for hydraulics and economics.
> 4. **Using $V$ in $\text{kg/s}$ vs. kmol/s**: All flow rates in the Kremser equation must be in **kmol/s (or kmol/h)**. Mass flow rates must be divided by molecular weight *before* using the equations.
> 5. **Ignoring the convention for stage numbering**: $(y_1, x_1)$ is the *top* (rich gas, lean solvent), $(y_{N+1}, x_{N+1})$ is the *bottom* (lean gas, rich solvent). Wrong numbering inverts the operating line and gives wrong $N$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Quick-Reference Checklist for Last-Minute Revision**:

- ⭐ **Absorption definition**: gas-solute transfer into a non-volatile liquid.
- ⭐ **Two contactor classes**: **stagewise** (plate) and **differential** (packed).
- ⭐ **Plate column**: discrete trays; design via **Kremser**; efficiency via **Murphree $E_M$**.
- ⭐ **Packed column**: continuous contact; design via **HTU-NTU** ($H_{OG}, N_{OG}$); HETP is the empirical link.
- ⭐ **Operating line**: $y = (L/V)x + (y_1 - (L/V)x_0)$, slope $= L/V$.
- ⭐ **Equilibrium line**: $y^* = mx$ (linear approximation).
- ⭐ **Absorption factor**: $A = L/(mV)$; $A = 1$ → uniform driving force, optimal design.
- ⭐ **Kremser equation**: $N = \ln[\dots]/\ln A$ — must memorise in both Treybal and logarithmic forms.
- ⭐ **$N_{OG}$ formula**: identical structure to Kremser — demonstrates **duality** of stagewise and differential contactors.
- ⭐ **$L_{\min}$**: corresponds to a **pinch point**; the operating line touches the equilibrium line at one end.
- ⭐ **Plate-column hydraulics**: flooding, weeping, entrainment, turndown.
- ⭐ **Packed-column hydraulics**: flooding, loading, channeling, maldistribution.
- ⭐ **HETP ↔ $H_{OG}$**: $\text{HETP} = H_{OG} \cdot \ln A/(A-1)$ for linear equilibrium; $\to H_{OG}$ as $A \to 1$.
- ⭐ **Always** verify $y > y^*$ and $x < x^*$ (mass-transfer driving force positive) at **both** ends.
- ⭐ **Subscripts**: $(1)$ = top/rich gas; $(N+1)$ = bottom/lean gas (Treybal convention).
- ⭐ **Counter-current** is the standard industrial configuration — always preferred over co-current.
- ⭐ **Kremser equation reduces to**: $y_1 = m x_0$ when $A = \infty$ (infinite solvent) and $y_1 = y_{N+1}$ when $A = 0$ (no solvent).

> **Final Note**: For multi-component absorption (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ in MEA), the **Kremser** equation is applied to **each component** with its own $m_i$ and $A_i$. The shortcut **Kremser-underwood-Gilliland** methods extend the binary result. For reactive absorption (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ + MEA), the equilibrium is replaced by a **reaction-enhanced** line; Hatta-number analysis is required. These are beyond Module 3 but constitute essential **higher-study links**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Volatility - Simple Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative Volatility - Simple Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Relative Volatility — Formal Definition

Relative Volatility ($\alpha$) is the ratio of the K-values (vapor-liquid equilibrium constants) of any two components in a mixture. For a binary mixture of components **A** (more volatile) and **B** (less volatile), it is defined as:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(y_A / x_A)}{(y_B / x_B)} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$

Where:
- $y_A, y_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the vapor phase
- $x_A, x_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the liquid phase
- $K_A, K_B$ = equilibrium constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For a binary system where $\alpha_{AB} > 1$, component **A** is the more volatile (lower boiling) component. If $\alpha_{AB} = 1$, the components have **identical volatilities** and **cannot be separated by simple distillation**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tilted Runway"

Imagine two runners on a tilted runway — one fast (light, volatile component A) and one slow (heavy component B). **Relative volatility is essentially the "speed ratio"** between them. 

- If $\alpha = 2.5$, it means A "runs" (evaporates) **2.5 times faster** than B.
- The higher the $\alpha$, the **steeper the runway** (greater ease of separation).
- A flat runway ($\alpha = 1$) means both runners move at the same speed — no separation possible no matter how long the runway is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, relative volatility is the **single most important parameter** governing all distillation design calculations — from a simple pot still to multi-stage columns.

## 1.3 Simple (Differential/Differential/Batch) Distillation — Definition

Simple distillation is a **batch process** in which a liquid mixture is heated in a still (reboiler), the vapor formed is immediately removed and condensed into a product (distillate), and the residual liquid becomes progressively richer in the less volatile component.

**Key Features:**
- **No reflux** is returned to the still
- Equilibrium is assumed between **leaving vapor** and **liquid remaining in the still**
- Operates as a **single theoretical stage**
- Liquid composition changes **continuously with time**

## 1.4 Physical Setup & Process Description

A simple distillation unit consists of:
1. **Still (Reboiler Pot):** Contains the liquid mixture, heated externally.
2. **Vapor Line:** Carries the evolved vapor to the condenser.
3. **Condenser:** Condenses the vapor into liquid distillate.
4. **Receiver:** Collects the accumulated distillate of changing composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Unlike flash distillation (which is continuous and at steady state), simple distillation is **time-dependent** — the instant the vapor leaves, the liquid composition shifts, making the next vapor richer in the heavier component.

## 1.5 Engineering Significance

| Concept | Real-World Application |
|---|---|
| Relative Volatility | Determines the **minimum number of stages** in column design (Fenske equation) |
| Simple Distillation | Crude oil topping, alcohol production in small-scale industries, water purification |
| Rayleigh Equation | Models batch processes in pharmaceutical and fine chemical industries |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Significance of Relative Volatility

**Step-by-step logic breakdown:**

1. **Origin:** From VLE, $K_i = y_i/x_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ for ideal systems (Raoult's Law).
2. **Ratio formation:** Taking the ratio eliminates the total pressure $P$, leaving only the saturation pressure ratio:
   $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$
3. **Dependence on temperature:** Since $P^{sat}$ is temperature-dependent, $\alpha$ is technically a weak function of temperature, but in KTU problems we **assume $\alpha$ = constant** (mean value over the operating range).
4. **How it governs separation:** The larger $\alpha$ deviates from 1, the **farther** the equilibrium curve $y-x$ lies from the diagonal, and the **easier** the separation.
5. **Special cases:**
   - $\alpha \to \infty$: Trivial separation (pure A + pure B initially)
   - $\alpha = 1$: Azeotrope-like behavior — **separation impossible by ordinary distillation**
   - $\alpha = 1.0$–$1.2$: Very difficult, requires many stages
   - $\alpha > 2$: Economically feasible separation

## 2.2 Equilibrium Relationship from Relative Volatility

For a binary mixture with constant $\alpha$, starting from:
$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x_A}$$

This is derived as follows. Using $y_A + y_B = 1$ and $x_A + x_B = 1$:
$$\alpha = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B} = \frac{y_A (1-x_A)}{x_A (1-y_A)}$$

Solving for $y_A$ in terms of $x_A$:
$$\alpha x_A (1-y_A) = y_A (1-x_A)$$
$$\alpha x_A - \alpha x_A y_A = y_A - x_A y_A$$
$$\alpha x_A = y_A(1 - x_A + \alpha x_A) = y_A[1 + (\alpha-1)x_A]$$
$$\boxed{y_A = \frac{\alpha x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x_A}}$$

## 2.3 Simple Distillation — Theoretical Framework

Theoretical pillars of simple distillation:

- **Assumption 1:** Vapor leaving is in **equilibrium** with the liquid remaining in the still at any instant.
- **Assumption 2:** No liquid droplets are entrained in the vapor (perfect disengagement).
- **Assumption 3:** Constant molar overflow holds (heats of vaporization of A and B are similar).
- **Assumption 4:** Raoult's Law applies (ideal solution behavior).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_{AB} = K_A/K_B = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$ | Definition of relative volatility | VLE analysis |
| 2 | $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ | Ideal system form (Raoult's Law) | Boiling point analysis |
| 3 | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1+(\alpha-1)x}$ | Binary VLE from constant $\alpha$ | Constructing $y$–$x$ diagram |
| 4 | $\ln(F/W) = \displaystyle\int_{x_W}^{x_F}\frac{dx}{y-x}$ | Rayleigh equation (general form) | Batch distillation analysis |
| 5 | $\ln(F/W) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}+\alpha\ln\dfrac{1-x_W}{1-x_F}\right]$ | Rayleigh equation (constant $\alpha$, binary) | KTU 14-mark problems |
| 6 | $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_{D,avg} + W \cdot x_W$ | Overall component balance | Average distillate composition |
| 7 | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{F \cdot x_F - W \cdot x_W}{x_{D,avg}}$ | Average distillate amount | Material balance |
| 8 | $x_{D,avg} = \dfrac{F \cdot x_F - W \cdot x_W}{F - W}$ | Average distillate composition | Instantaneous check |
| 9 | $\dfrac{dN}{N} = \dfrac{dx}{y-x}$ | Differential form | Rayleigh derivation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation in KTU Problems:**
> - $F$ = Initial moles of liquid in still (also denoted $N_0$ or $L_0$)
> - $W$ = Final moles of liquid residue in still (also denoted $N_F$ or $L_W$)
> - $x_F$ = Initial mole fraction of more volatile component (also $x_0$)
> - $x_W$ = Final mole fraction in residue
> - $D$ = Total moles of distillate collected (= $F - W$)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The relative volatility framework underpins:
- **Fenske Equation** for minimum number of stages in total reflux
- **McCabe-Thiele Method** — number of stages depends on the $\alpha$-dependent pinch point
- **Reflux ratio selection** in continuous columns
- **Energy optimization** in crude oil atmospheric/vacuum distillation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Equation (General Form)

This is the **master equation** for simple distillation and a guaranteed high-yield KTU problem.

### Step 1: Setup the Differential Material Balance

Let:
- $N$ = moles of liquid in the still at any instant
- $x$ = mole fraction of more volatile component (MVC) in the liquid at that instant
- $y$ = mole fraction of MVC in the vapor leaving that instant

After a differential time $d\tau$:
- Liquid becomes: $(N - dN)$ with composition $(x - dx)$
- Vapor leaves: amount $= dN$, composition $= y$

### Step 2: Component Material Balance on the MVC

$$\text{MVC entering system} = \text{MVC in final liquid} + \text{MVC leaving as vapor}$$

$$N \cdot x = (N - dN)(x - dx) + y \cdot dN$$

### Step 3: Expand the Right-Hand Side

$$N \cdot x = N \cdot x - N \cdot dx - x \cdot dN + dN \cdot dx + y \cdot dN$$

### Step 4: Simplify (Neglect Second-Order Term $dN \cdot dx$)

The product $dN \cdot dx$ is a second-order infinitesimal and is set to zero:

$$0 = -N \cdot dx - x \cdot dN + y \cdot dN$$

Rearranging:

$$N \cdot dx = (y - x) \, dN$$

### Step 5: Separate Variables

$$\frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{dN}{N}$$

### Step 6: Integrate Over the Process Limits

The process starts with $N_0 = F$ (initial moles) at composition $x_0 = x_F$, and ends with $N_F = W$ (final residue) at composition $x_W$.

$$\int_{x_F}^{x_W} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \int_{F}^{W} \frac{dN}{N}$$

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y - x}}$$

This is the **general Rayleigh equation** — valid for any equilibrium relationship $y = f(x)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rayleigh Equation for Constant Relative Volatility (Binary)

We start from the VLE expression derived in Section 2.2:

$$y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$

### Step 1: Compute $y - x$

$$y - x = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} - x = \frac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha - 1)x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$

Numerator expansion:
$$\alpha x - x - (\alpha - 1)x^2 = (\alpha - 1)x - (\alpha - 1)x^2 = (\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)$$

Therefore:
$$y - x = \frac{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$

### Step 2: Set Up the Integrand

$$\frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)} \, dx$$

### Step 3: Partial Fraction Decomposition

Express:
$$\frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)} = \frac{A}{x} + \frac{B}{1 - x}$$

Multiplying both sides by $(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)$:
$$1 + (\alpha - 1)x = A(\alpha - 1)(1 - x) + B(\alpha - 1)x$$

Dividing by $(\alpha - 1)$:
$$\frac{1}{\alpha - 1} + x = A(1 - x) + Bx$$

Equating coefficients:
- **Constant term:** $A = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}$
- **Coefficient of $x$:** $-A + B = 1 \implies B = 1 + A = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha - 1}$

Therefore:
$$\frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{(\alpha - 1)} \left[ \frac{1}{x} + \frac{\alpha}{1 - x} \right] dx$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$\int \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\int \frac{dx}{x} + \alpha \int \frac{dx}{1 - x}\right]$$

$$\int \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln x - \alpha \ln(1 - x)\right]$$

### Step 5: Apply Limits

$$\int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\frac{x_F}{x_W} - \alpha \ln\frac{1 - x_F}{1 - x_W}\right]$$

$$\int_{x_W}^{x_F} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\frac{x_F}{x_W} + \alpha \ln\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right]$$

### Step 6: Equate with Rayleigh Form

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\frac{x_F}{x_W} + \alpha \ln\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right]}$$

This is the **final closed-form Rayleigh equation** for a binary system with constant $\alpha$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Problem-Solving Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type I | $F, x_F, x_W, \alpha$ | $W$ (residue) | Direct substitution in Rayleigh eqn |
| Type II | $F, x_F, x_W, W$ | $\alpha$ (relative volatility) | Solve Rayleigh eqn iteratively for $\alpha$ |
| Type III | $F, x_F, x_W, W$ | $x_{D,avg}$ | Use overall component balance |
| Type IV | $F, x_F, \alpha, D$ | $x_W$ | Use $W = F - D$, then Rayleigh |
| Type V | Steam distillation case | Total pressure needed | Apply Dalton's + Raoult's law |

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Example Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A mixture containing 60 mol% benzene (MVC) and 40 mol% toluene is to be separated by simple distillation. The relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene is $\alpha = 2.5$. If 70% of the benzene is to be recovered in the distillate (the rest in the residue), determine:
(a) The amount of residue when starting with 100 kmol of feed.
(b) The composition of the residue.
(c) The average composition of the distillate collected.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $F = 100$ kmol, $x_F = 0.60$, $\alpha = 2.5$
- Benzene recovery in distillate = 70%, so 30% in residue.

**Part (a) and (b): Find W and $x_W$**

Benzene in residue: $W \cdot x_W = 100 \times 0.60 \times 0.30 = 18$ kmol

Apply Rayleigh equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{F}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\frac{x_F}{x_W} + \alpha \ln\frac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right]$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{100}{W}\right) = \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln\frac{0.60}{x_W} + 2.5 \ln\frac{1 - x_W}{0.40}\right]$$

**Iterative solution:** Try $x_W = 0.42$:

$$\ln(100/42) = \ln(2.381) = 0.8675$$

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln(0.60/0.42) + 2.5\ln(0.58/0.40)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln(1.4286) + 2.5\ln(1.45)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}\left[0.3567 + 2.5(0.3716)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}[0.3567 + 0.9289] = \frac{1.2856}{1.5} = 0.8571$$

Close — try $x_W = 0.421$:

$$\ln(100/42.1) = \ln(2.3753) = 0.8650$$

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln(0.60/0.421) + 2.5\ln(0.579/0.40)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln(1.4252) + 2.5\ln(1.4475)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}\left[0.3541 + 2.5(0.3694)\right] = \frac{1.2777}{1.5} = 0.8518$$

Try $x_W = 0.419$:
$$\ln(100/41.9) = 0.8697$$
$$\text{RHS} = \frac{1}{1.5}\left[\ln(0.60/0.419) + 2.5\ln(0.581/0.40)\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{1.5}[0.3591 + 2.5(0.3736)] = \frac{1.2931}{1.5} = 0.8621$$

Converging to $x_W \approx 0.420$, $W \approx 42.0$ kmol.

**Part (c): Average Distillate Composition**

Total material balance: $D = F - W = 100 - 42.0 = 58.0$ kmol

Component balance: $F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_{D,avg} + W \cdot x_W$

$$100(0.60) = 58.0 \cdot x_{D,avg} + 42.0(0.420)$$

$$60 = 58 \cdot x_{D,avg} + 17.64$$

$$x_{D,avg} = \frac{60 - 17.64}{58} = \frac{42.36}{58} = 0.7303$$

**Final Answers:**
- (a) $W \approx 42.0$ kmol
- (b) $x_W \approx 0.420$
- (c) $x_{D,avg} \approx 0.730$
