# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the reboiler as a theoretical stage.** The McCabe–Thiele count must add 1 for the partial reboiler.
> 2. **Confusing x_D and y_{n+1}** in the operating-line derivation — be crystal clear which is vapour and which is liquid, and which is leaving vs. entering.
> 3. **Using the wrong q-value** for the feed — if the feed is subcooled, q > 1, not 1. The q-line is *not* always vertical.
> 4. **Mixing up R_min and R_opt** — KTU usually asks for R_opt = 1.2 to 1.5 R_min, *not* R_min itself.
> 5. **Skipping units** in intermediate steps — always state kmol h⁻¹ or kg h⁻¹ explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify** that the intersection of the two operating lines lies on the q-line (sanity check).
> 7. **Using the Fenske equation with reboiler-included N_min vs. N_min excluding reboiler** inconsistently — pick one convention and stick to it.
> 8. **Calculation errors in the Gilliland correlation** — the 0.75 and 0.5668 exponents are exact; do not round them.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define the reflux ratio R. Why is the operating reflux ratio kept above the minimum reflux ratio R_min?

**Model Answer:**

> The **reflux ratio R** is defined as the ratio of the molar flow rate of liquid returned to the top of the column (reflux, L) to the molar flow rate of distillate product (D) withdrawn:
> R = L / D (dimensionless).

> At **R = R_min**, the rectifying and stripping operating lines intersect on the equilibrium curve, requiring an **infinite number of theoretical stages** to achieve the specified separation. Operating at R_min is therefore physically impossible. In practice, the operating R is kept **above R_min** (typically at R_opt = 1.2 to 1.5 R_min) to provide a finite, economically reasonable number of stages. The economic optimum is the value of R that minimises the **total annualised cost (TAC)**, which is the sum of capital cost (decreases as R increases, because N decreases) and operating cost (increases as R increases, because reboiler and condenser duties increase). Operating at exactly R_min or at total reflux is unfeasible — R_min gives infinite stages; total reflux gives zero production.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Explain the significance of the q-line in McCabe–Thiele analysis. How does the feed condition (q) affect the q-line slope?

**Model Answer:**

> The **q-line** (feed condition line) is the locus of all possible intersection points of the rectifying and stripping operating lines as the feed tray location is varied. Geometrically, the q-line passes through **(z_F, z_F)** on the 45° line and has a slope of **q / (q − 1)**. The intersection point of the two operating lines **must** lie on the q-line, so the q-line determines the position of this intersection and hence the slope of the stripping operating line. Therefore, the q-line dictates the **feed-stage composition**, the **number of stages above and below the feed**, and ultimately the **total number of theoretical stages**.

> The **q-value** (molar liquid fraction of the feed) depends on the feed's thermal state:
> - **Saturated liquid feed (q = 1):** q-line is **vertical** at x = z_F.
> - **Saturated vapour feed (q = 0):** q-line is **horizontal** at y = z_F.
> - **Subcooled liquid (q > 1):** q-line has **positive finite slope** steeper than vertical (i.e., rising to the right).
> - **Superheated vapour (q < 0):** q-line has **negative slope**, falling from upper-left to lower-right.
> - **Two-phase (0 < q < 1):** q-line has positive slope between 0 and 1.

> The q-line slope therefore serves as a **diagnostic for the feed condition** and is essential for the correct geometric construction of the operating lines on the y–x diagram.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Primary Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

A binary mixture of methanol (MVC) and water (LVC) is to be separated in a continuous distillation column operating at 1 atm. The following data are given:

- Feed: F = 150 kmol h⁻¹, z_F = 0.45 (mole fraction methanol in feed)
- Distillate purity: x_D = 0.96
- Bottoms purity: x_W = 0.04
- Feed condition: saturated liquid (q = 1)
- Operating reflux ratio: R = 1.5 R_min
- Relative volatility: α = 3.45 (assumed constant)

**Solve:**

**(a)** Compute the distillate flow D, bottoms flow W, and minimum reflux ratio R_min using the Underwood / geometric method. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the rectifying and stripping operating-line equations, and the number of theoretical stages using the Gilliland correlation. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — D, W, and R_min:**

**Step 1: Material balance to find D and W.**

Overall balance: D + W = 150 kmol h⁻¹

Methanol balance: 0.96 D + 0.04 W = 0.45 × 150 = 67.5

Subtracting 0.04 × first from the second:

$$
0.96 D + 0.04 W - 0.04 D - 0.04 W = 67.5 - 6
$$

$$
0.92 D = 61.5 \implies D = 66.85 \text{ kmol h}^{-1}
$$

$$
W = 150 - 66.85 = 83.15 \text{ kmol h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: R_min from geometric construction.**

For q = 1, the q-line is vertical at x = z_F = 0.45. The intersection with the equilibrium curve gives the q-point (x_q, y_q):

At x = 0.45, using the constant-α equation:

$$
y_q = \frac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} = \frac{3.45 \times 0.45}{1 + 2.45 \times 0.45} = \frac{1.5525}{2.1025} = 0.7384
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} = \frac{0.96 - 0.7384}{0.7384 - 0.45} = \frac{0.2216}{0.2884} = 0.7684
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\min} \approx 0.768}
$$

**Part (b) — Operating lines and number of stages:**

**Step 1: Operating reflux R = 1.5 × R_min = 1.5 × 0.768 = 1.153.**

**Step 2: Rectifying operating line.**

Slope: R / (R + 1) = 1.153 / 2.153 = 0.5355

Intercept: x_D / (R + 1) = 0.96 / 2.153 = 0.4459

$$
\boxed{y_{\text{rect}} = 0.5355 \, x + 0.4459}
$$

**Step 3: Stripping operating line.**

For q = 1: L′ = L + F = R·D + F = 1.153 × 66.85 + 150 = 227.08 kmol h⁻¹

V′ = V = D(R + 1) = 66.85 × 2.153 = 143.93 kmol h⁻¹

Slope: L′ / V′ = 227.08 / 143.93 = 1.5777

Intercept: −(W x_W) / V′ = −(83.15 × 0.04) / 143.93 = −0.02311

$$
\boxed{y_{\text{strip}} = 1.5777 \, x - 0.02311}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the intersection is on the q-line x = 0.45.**

Rectifying at x = 0.45: y = 0.5355 × 0.45 + 0.4459 = 0.2410 + 0.4459 = 0.6869

Stripping at x = 0.45: y = 1.5777 × 0.45 − 0.02311 = 0.7100 − 0.02311 = 0.6869 ✔

**Step 5: N_min by Fenske equation.**

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.96}{0.04} \cdot \dfrac{0.96}{0.04}\right]}{\ln 3.45}
$$

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln(24 \times 24)}{\ln 3.45} = \frac{\ln(576)}{\ln 3.45} = \frac{6.357}{1.238} = 5.13
$$

**Step 6: Gilliland correlation.**

$$
X = \frac{R - R_{\min}}{R + 1} = \frac{1.153 - 0.768}{1.153 + 1} = \frac{0.385}{2.153} = 0.1788
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 (1 - X^{0.5668}) = 0.75 (1 - 0.1788^{0.5668}) = 0.75 (1 - 0.3540) = 0.75 \times 0.6460 = 0.4845
$$

Solving for N:

$$
N = \frac{N_{\min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \frac{5.13 + 0.4845}{1 - 0.4845} = \frac{5.615}{0.5155} = 10.89
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 11 \text{ theoretical stages (excluding reboiler)}}
$$

**Final Summary for Part B(a)+(b):** D = 66.85 kmol h⁻¹, W = 83.15 kmol h⁻¹, R_min = 0.768, R_opt = 1.153, N_min = 5.13, N ≈ 11 stages. The feed tray is located at the 6th or 7th stage from the top (judged by McCabe–Thiele stepping).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

A saturated-liquid feed containing 50 mol% n-hexane (MVC) and 50 mol% n-heptane (LVC) is distilled at 1 atm to give a distillate of 90 mol% n-hexane and bottoms of 5 mol% n-hexane. The feed flow rate is 200 kmol h⁻¹ and the relative volatility α = 2.16 (assumed constant).

**Solve:**

**(a)** Compute the minimum number of theoretical stages (N_min) by Fenske, and the minimum reflux ratio (R_min) by the McCabe–Thiele geometric method. State the operating-line equations at R = 1.4 R_min. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the Gilliland correlation, determine the number of theoretical stages at R = 1.4 R_min. Also compute the feed-tray location and discuss the effect of feed thermal condition on the q-line slope. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — N_min, R_min, and operating-line equations:**

**Step 1: N_min by Fenske.**

$$
N_{\min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{0.90}{0.10} \cdot \dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.16} = \frac{\ln(9 \times 19)}{\ln 2.16} = \frac{\ln(171)}{\ln 2.16} = \frac{5.142}{0.7701} = 6.68
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\min} \approx 6.68}
$$

**Step 2: R_min by geometric construction (q = 1).**

At x = z_F = 0.50, the equilibrium composition is:

$$
y_q = \frac{2.16 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.16 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.080}{1.580} = 0.6835
$$

$$
R_{\min} = \frac{0.90 - 0.6835}{0.6835 - 0.50} = \frac{0.2165}{0.1835} = 1.180
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\min} \approx 1.180}
$$

**Step 3: Operating reflux R = 1.4 R_min = 1.4 × 1.180 = 1.652.**

**Step 4: Material balance.**

D + W = 200

0.90 D + 0.05 W = 0.50 × 200 = 100

Subtracting: 0.85 D = 90, so D = 105.88 kmol h⁻¹, W = 94.12 kmol h⁻¹.

**Step 5: Operating lines.**

Rectifying line:

$$
y = \frac{1.652}{2.652} x + \frac{0.90}{2.652} = 0.6229 \, x + 0.3393
$$

Stripping line (q = 1):

L′ = L + F = 1.652 × 105.88 + 200 = 374.91 kmol h⁻¹

V′ = V = 105.88 × 2.652 = 280.79 kmol h⁻¹

$$
y = \frac{374.91}{280.79} x - \frac{94.12 \times 0.05}{280.79} = 1.3352 \, x - 0.01676
$$

**Part (b) — Gilliland, feed location, and effect of feed condition:**

**Step 1: Gilliland correlation.**

$$
X = \frac{R - R_{\min}}{R + 1} = \frac{1.652 - 1.180}{1.652 + 1} = \frac{0.472}{2.652} = 0.1780
$$

$$
Y = 0.75 (1 - X^{0.5668}) = 0.75 (1 - 0.1780^{0.5668}) = 0.75 (1 - 0.3532) = 0.4851
$$

$$
N = \frac{N_{\min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \frac{6.68 + 0.4851}{1 - 0.4851} = \frac{7.165}{0.5149} = 13.92
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 14 \text{ theoretical stages (excluding reboiler)}}
$$

**Step 2: Feed-tray location (by McCabe–Thiele stepping).**

The feed stage is the stage at which the operating line switches from rectifying to stripping — i.e., the stage whose step "crosses" the intersection point P of the two operating lines. For this problem, by graphical construction (or by analytical approximation), the feed is on the **8th stage from the top** (with the reboiler counted as stage 1 from the bottom).

**Step 3: Effect of feed thermal condition on q-line slope.**

| Feed Condition | q | q-line Slope | Geometric Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saturated liquid | 1 | ∞ (vertical) | Vertical line x = z_F |
| Saturated vapour | 0 | 0 (horizontal) | Horizontal line y = z_F |
| Subcooled liquid | > 1 | Positive, > 1 | Rises steeply to the right |
| Superheated vapour | < 0 | Negative, between 0 and 1 | Falls to the right |
| Two-phase | 0 < q < 1 | Positive, between 0 and 1 | Moderate upward slope |

**Effect on design:** Changing the feed condition changes the q-line slope, which changes the position of the intersection P of the two operating lines. This in turn changes the **slope of the stripping operating line** and the **feed-stage composition**, ultimately changing the **total number of stages** required. Subcooled-liquid feeds (q > 1) shift P to the right, steepening the stripping line and increasing the stripping-section stages. Superheated-vapour feeds (q < 0) shift P to the left, flattening the stripping line and reducing the stripping-section stages but increasing the rectifying-section stages. The **optimum feed condition** is typically the one that makes the intersection P occur exactly at the feed-stage composition, minimising the total number of stages.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Distillation with Reflux: 20-Point Rapid-Revision Checklist**

1. **Reflux ratio** R = L / D (dimensionless). Operating R = 1.2 to 1.5 R_min.
2. **Rectifying operating line:** y = [R/(R+1)] x + x_D/(R+1). Passes through (x_D, x_D).
3. **Stripping operating line:** y = (L′/V′) x − (W x_W / V′). Passes through (x_W, x_W).
4. **q-line:** y = [q/(q−1)] x − z_F/(q−1). Passes through (z_F, z_F) and through the intersection of the two operating lines.
5. **q = 1** → vertical q-line (saturated liquid). **q = 0** → horizontal (saturated vapour).
6. **L′ = L + qF**, **V′ = V − (1−q)F**. Always use these in the stripping section.
7. **Fenske equation** gives N_min at total reflux. Depends only on α, x_D, x_W (thermodynamic limit).
8. **R_min (geometric):** R_min = (x_D − y_q)/(y_q − x_q), where (x_q, y_q) is the intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve.
9. **Underwood equation** gives R_min analytically (constant molar overflow, constant α).
10. **Gilliland correlation:** Y = 0.75(1 − X^0.5668), X = (R − R_min)/(R + 1), Y = (N − N_min)/(N + 1).
11. **Optimum R** balances CAPEX (high R = low N = low column cost) vs. OPEX (high R = high Q_R, Q_C = high utility cost).
12. **Partial reboiler counts as one theoretical stage** in the McCabe–Thiele count.
13. **Feed-tray location:** switch from rectifying to stripping operating line when a step crosses the q-line (or equivalently, crosses the intersection point P).
14. **The intersection P of the two operating lines always lies on the q-line** — use this as a sanity check.
15. **Constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption:** L and V constant within each section, but L′ ≠ L and V′ ≠ V in general.
16. **Constant relative volatility (α)** assumption: y = αx / [1 + (α−1)x], used in Fenske and analytic R_min.
17. **Material balances:** D + W = F; D x_D + W x_W = F z_F. Solve these *first*, before any other calculation.
18. **Subcooled liquid (q > 1):** q-line slope is finite positive, *not* vertical. Common KTU pitfall.
19. **Superheated vapour (q < 0):** q-line slope is *negative*. The intersection P moves to the left, increasing rectifying-section stages.
20. **Real-world applications:** Petroleum refining (crude columns), cryogenic air separation (Linde cycle), ethanol dehydration, pharmaceutical solvent recovery, fine-chemicals purification, isotope separation.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Azeotropic Distillation

### Formal Definition
**Azeotropic Distillation** is a specialized distillation technique employed to separate liquid mixtures that form **azeotropes** — compositions at which the liquid and vapor phases have identical compositions, making separation by simple distillation impossible. An **entrainer** (a third component) is added to the mixture to deliberately form a *new* azeotrope with one of the original components, thereby shifting the relative volatility and enabling separation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Azeotropes occur when the activity coefficients deviate strongly from Raoult's Law, producing either **minimum-boiling (homogeneous) azeotropes** or **maximum-boiling (heterogeneous) azeotropes**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine trying to separate a perfectly mixed salt and sugar solution by repeatedly evaporating and condensing water — both dissolve so well that they always vaporize together (like an azeotrope). Now, if you add a "magic third ingredient" (the **entrainer**) that bonds preferentially with one of them, you can effectively "liberate" the other component and pull it out. The entrainer acts like a *bait* — it traps one component and allows the other to vaporize freely.

### Types of Azeotropes
| Type | Behavior | Example System |
|------|----------|----------------|
| Minimum-Boiling (Homogeneous) | Boils at a temperature *lower* than either pure component | Ethanol–Water (95.6 mol% ethanol at 78.2 °C) |
| Maximum-Boiling (Heterogeneous) | Boils at a temperature *higher* than either pure component | Hydrochloric Acid–Water (20.2 wt% HCl at 108.6 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric used to quantify azeotrope "tightness" is the **azeotropic composition** (x_az, y_az), reported in mole fraction, and the **azeotropic pressure** (P_az), typically at **101.325 kPa** (1 atm).

---

## 1.2 Extractive Distillation

### Formal Definition
**Extractive Distillation** is a distillation technique used to separate mixtures whose components have *very close* boiling points (relative volatility α ≈ 1) and therefore cannot be separated economically by ordinary distillation. A **high-boiling, non-volatile solvent** (the *extractive agent*) is introduced near the top of the column to selectively alter the activity coefficients of the components, dramatically increasing relative volatility.

### Conceptual Analogy
Consider two best friends (close-boiling liquids) who refuse to leave each other. You cannot separate them by ordinary "evaporation" (simple distillation). But if you introduce a *new friend* (the solvent) who is very close to **Friend A** and ignores **Friend B**, Friend B is left "free" and can be evaporated out easily. The solvent essentially "captures" one component through selective molecular interaction, freeing the other.

### Industrial Examples
- Separation of **butenes** (close boiling, α ≈ 1.03) using **furfural** as solvent.
- Separation of **BTX aromatics** (benzene, toluene, xylenes) from aliphatic hydrocarbons using **sulfolane** or **NMP**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solvatochromic Behavior:** The solvent is recovered in a separate **solvent recovery column** and recycled — it never leaves the system. Solvent-to-feed ratio is typically **3:1 to 6:1** by mass.

---

## 1.3 McCabe–Thiele Method

### Formal Definition
The **McCabe–Thiele Method** is a rigorous graphical technique developed by **Warren L. McCabe and Ernest W. Thiele (1925)** for determining the **number of theoretical stages (theoretical plates)** required to achieve a specified separation in a binary distillation column. It uses stepwise construction between the **equilibrium curve** and the **operating lines** on an x–y diagram.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a *staircase* descending from the top of a column to the bottom. Each "step" represents one theoretical plate. You start on the **operating line** (a real-world mass balance line) and step *across* to the **equilibrium curve** (the thermodynamic "ceiling"), then *back* to the operating line. Each round trip = **one theoretical stage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** A *theoretical stage* is a hypothetical contact device where the exiting vapor and liquid streams reach **thermodynamic equilibrium**. Real columns have *Murphree efficiencies* less than **100%**, so actual plates > theoretical plates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Azeotropic & Extractive Distillation Are Needed

### Limitations of Simple Distillation
- Azeotropes → identical liquid and vapor composition → **no separation possible**
- Close-boiling mixtures → relative volatility α → **1.0** → huge plate requirement, uneconomical
- Pinch points near feed composition (q-line intersects equilibrium curve)

### Theoretical Foundation
The basis of both techniques lies in **Le Chatelier's Principle** applied to vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE):

$$y_i = \frac{\gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}$$

where:
- $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient of component *i* (Raoult's Law modified)
- $x_i$ = mole fraction in liquid
- $P_i^{sat}$ = vapor pressure of pure component *i*
- $P_{total}$ = total system pressure

By introducing a third component (entrainer/solvent), $\gamma_i$ values change drastically, breaking azeotropes or increasing relative volatility $\alpha_{12}$.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Azeotropic Distillation

1. **Identify the azeotrope** in the binary mixture (e.g., ethanol–water at 95.6 mol% ethanol).
2. **Select an entrainer** that forms a *new* heterogeneous (two-liquid-phase) azeotrope with one of the original components.
3. **Operate the entrainer column** — the new azeotrope (low-boiling) exits the top as vapor, condenses, and forms two immiscible liquid layers in a **decanter**.
4. **Recover** the desired pure component from one liquid layer; return the entrainer-rich layer to the column as reflux.
5. **Recover** the entrainer in a separate recovery column for recycling.

### Entrainer Selection Criteria
- Should form a **heterogeneous minimum-boiling azeotrope** with one component
- Should have **low miscibility** with the other component
- Should be **chemically stable**, **non-toxic**, and **inexpensive**
- Should have a **low latent heat of vaporization**

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Extractive Distillation

1. **Identify the close-boiling binary** (e.g., 1-butene / isobutene, α ≈ 1.03).
2. **Select a high-boiling polar solvent** (e.g., furfural, acetone, water, DMF).
3. **Feed the solvent** near the **top of the column**, above the main feed.
4. The solvent preferentially **hydrogen-bonds or interacts** with the more polar component, lowering its effective vapor pressure.
5. The **less-interactive component** becomes the distillate (top product).
6. The **solvent + interactive component** leaves the bottom and is separated in a second column.
7. The **solvent is recycled** to the extractive column.

### Solvent Selection Criteria
- **Selectivity** β > 1.0 (high β ⇒ easier separation)
- **High boiling point** (> 50 °C above the heavy key component)
- **Low volatility** (negligible solvent in distillate)
- **Thermal stability** and **non-corrosive**
- **Easy recovery** and low cost

---

## 2.4 McCabe–Thiele Method — The Six Construction Elements

### Element 1: Equilibrium Curve (x vs y)
Plotted from experimental VLE data or predicted using **Margules, Van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, or UNIQUAC** equations. A pinch point indicates an azeotrope.

### Element 2: 45° Reference Line
The line $y = x$ — used to identify where $x_n = y_n$ (no separation).

### Element 3: Rectifying Section Operating Line
Derived from mass balance above the feed plate:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

where $R = L/D$ is the **reflux ratio** (reboiled liquid L / distillate D).

### Element 4: Stripping Section Operating Line
Derived from mass balance below the feed plate:

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{L'-V'} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{L'-V'}$$

where $L'$ = liquid flow in stripping section, $V'$ = vapor flow in stripping section, $W$ = bottoms rate, $x_W$ = bottoms composition.

### Element 5: q-line (Feed Line)
Derived from feed condition:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

where $q$ = moles of liquid formed per mole of feed:
- **Saturated liquid feed** → $q = 1$
- **Saturated vapor feed** → $q = 0$
- **Subcooled liquid** → $q > 1$
- **Superheated vapor** → $q < 0$

### Element 6: Stepping Procedure
Starting at $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° line, step horizontally to the equilibrium curve (to find $x_{n+1}$), then vertically to the operating line (to find $y_{n+1}$). Continue until the bottom composition $x_W$ is crossed.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables |
|---|---------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $y_i = \frac{\gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | Modified Raoult's Law (VLE) |
| 2 | $\alpha_{12} = \frac{K_1}{K_2} = \frac{\gamma_1 P_1^{sat}}{\gamma_2 P_2^{sat}}$ | Relative volatility |
| 3 | $y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$ | Binary VLE from relative volatility |
| 4 | $y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Rectifying operating line |
| 5 | $y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{L'-V'} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{L'-V'}$ | Stripping operating line |
| 6 | $y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$ | q-line equation |
| 7 | $R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$ | Minimum reflux ratio (for $q \leq 1$) |
| 8 | $\left(\frac{L}{V}\right)_{min} = \frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1}$ | Min slope of rectifying line |
| 9 | $N_{min} = $ steps at total reflux (q-line not used) | Min. theoretical stages (Fenske) |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | Murphree / Overall efficiency |
| 11 | $\beta = \frac{(\alpha_{12})_with\ solvent}{(\alpha_{12})_without\ solvent}$ | Solvent selectivity (extractive) |
| 12 | $\frac{L'}{V'} = \frac{L + qF}{L + qF - (1-q)F} = \frac{L+qF}{V+(q-1)F}$ | Stripping line slope relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This technique is used to design **crude oil atmospheric & vacuum distillation columns**, **ethanol dehydration plants**, **air separation units**, and **cryogenic LNG fractionators** — making it one of the most industrially relevant tools in chemical engineering.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Operating Line

### Given
- Distillate flow rate = $D$ (kmol/h), composition $x_D$
- Reflux flow rate = $L$ (kmol/h)
- Vapor flow from top plate = $V = L + D$
- Liquid composition on plate $n$ = $x_n$
- Vapor composition leaving plate $n$ = $y_{n+1}$

### Mass Balance Around the Top Envelope (above plate $n$)

**Total material balance:**
$$V = L + D$$

**Component (more volatile) balance:**
$$V y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$$

### Step 1: Substitute $V = L + D$

$$(L + D) y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$$

### Step 2: Expand and rearrange

$$L y_{n+1} + D y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$$

$$L y_{n+1} - L x_n = D x_D - D y_{n+1}$$

$$L (y_{n+1} - x_n) = D (x_D - y_{n+1})$$

### Step 3: Define Reflux Ratio $R = L/D$

Divide both sides by $D$:

$$\frac{L}{D} (y_{n+1} - x_n) = x_D - y_{n+1}$$

$$R (y_{n+1} - x_n) = x_D - y_{n+1}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $y_{n+1}$

$$R y_{n+1} - R x_n = x_D - y_{n+1}$$

$$R y_{n+1} + y_{n+1} = x_D + R x_n$$

$$y_{n+1} (R + 1) = x_D + R x_n$$

$$\boxed{y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The slope of the rectifying line is $\frac{R}{R+1}$, and the y-intercept is $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$. As $R \to \infty$ (total reflux), the slope → 1, and the line merges with the 45° diagonal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Stripping Operating Line

### Given
- Bottoms flow rate = $W$ (kmol/h), composition $x_W$
- Stripping-section liquid flow = $L'$
- Stripping-section vapor flow = $V'$
- Reboiler generates vapor $V' = L' - W$

### Mass Balance Around the Bottom Envelope (below plate $m$)

**Total:** $L' = V' + W$

**Component balance:**
$$L' x_m = V' y_{m+1} + W x_W$$

### Step 1: Substitute $L' = V' + W$

$$(V' + W) x_m = V' y_{m+1} + W x_W$$

### Step 2: Expand and collect terms

$$V' x_m + W x_m = V' y_{m+1} + W x_W$$

$$V' (x_m - y_{m+1}) = W (x_W - x_m)$$

### Step 3: Solve for $y_{m+1}$

$$x_m - y_{m+1} = \frac{W}{V'}(x_W - x_m)$$

$$y_{m+1} = x_m - \frac{W}{V'}(x_W - x_m)$$

$$y_{m+1} = x_m \left(1 + \frac{W}{V'}\right) - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

$$y_{m+1} = x_m \left(\frac{V' + W}{V'}\right) - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

### Step 4: Note that $L' = V' + W$

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

Substitute $V' = L' - W$:

$$\boxed{y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{L'-W} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{L'-W}}$$

> **Engineering Note:** Stripping line slope $\frac{L'}{L'-W} > 1$ — it is *steeper* than the rectifying line. Both lines intersect at the q-line at the feed point $(x_F, y_F)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the q-line Equation

### Definition of q
$$q = \frac{\text{moles of saturated liquid formed}}{\text{mole of feed}}$$

### Mass Balance Around the Feed Plate

Let $L, V$ = rectifying flows; $L', V'$ = stripping flows.
Let $F$ = feed rate.

**Continuity of liquid:** $L' = L + qF$
**Continuity of vapor:** $V = V' + (1-q)F$

### Component Balance on Feed Plate
$$V y_{n+1} + F z_F = V' y_n + L' x_n$$

### Substituting Continuity Equations

Using $V' = V - (1-q)F$ and $L' = L + qF$, and after algebraic simplification (standard textbook derivation), one obtains:

$$(q-1) V y_{n+1} = q V (y_n - x_n) + F(z_F - x_n)$$

Dividing by $V$ and rearranging (substituting $V y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$ from rectifying balance), we obtain the elegant form:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}}$$

### Special Feed Conditions
| Feed State | q-value | q-line slope | q-line passes through |
|------------|---------|--------------|------------------------|
| Saturated liquid | 1.0 | ∞ (vertical) | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Saturated vapor | 0.0 | 0 (horizontal) | $(x_F, y_F)$ |
| Subcooled liquid | > 1.0 | between 1 and ∞ | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Liquid–Vapor mixture (q = 0.5) | 0.5 | −1.0 | $(x_F, x_F)$ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — McCabe–Thiele Stage Calculation

**Problem:** A distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture. Given:
- $x_F = 0.40$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W = 0.05$
- Reflux ratio $R = 1.5 R_{min}$
- Saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$)
- Relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ (constant)

### Step 1: Equilibrium Curve
$$y = \frac{2.47 x}{1 + 1.47 x}$$

| $x$ | $y$ |
|------|------|
| 0.0 | 0.000 |
| 0.1 | 0.225 |
| 0.2 | 0.401 |
| 0.3 | 0.543 |
| 0.4 | 0.658 |
| 0.5 | 0.752 |
| 0.6 | 0.830 |
| 0.7 | 0.892 |
| 0.8 | 0.939 |
| 0.9 | 0.973 |
| 1.0 | 1.000 |

### Step 2: Minimum Reflux Ratio (q = 1, vertical q-line)
The q-line is $x = 0.40$ (vertical). At $x_F = 0.40$, the equilibrium $y^* = 0.658$:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.658}{0.658 - 0.40} = \frac{0.292}{0.258} = 1.132$$

### Step 3: Operating Reflux Ratio
$$R = 1.5 \times 1.132 = 1.698$$

### Step 4: Rectifying Operating Line
- Slope = $\frac{R}{R+1} = \frac{1.698}{2.698} = 0.629$
- y-intercept = $\frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{0.95}{2.698} = 0.352$

$$y = 0.629 x + 0.352$$

### Step 5: Stripping Operating Line (passes through $(x_W, x_W) = (0.05, 0.05)$ and intersects the rectifying line at the q-line $x = 0.40$)

At $x = 0.40$, the rectifying line gives $y = 0.629(0.40) + 0.352 = 0.604$.

Stripping line passing through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and $(0.40, 0.604)$:

Slope = $\frac{0.604 - 0.05}{0.40 - 0.05} = \frac{0.554}{0.35} = 1.583$

$$y = 1.583(x - 0.05) + 0.05 = 1.583 x - 0.029$$

### Step 6: Stepping Procedure
Starting at $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ on the 45° line:
- Step 1: Horizontal to equilibrium curve → $x_1 \approx 0.880$
- Step 2: Vertical to rectifying line → $y_2 \approx 0.911$
- ... continue until $x < x_W = 0.05$

> **Result:** $N_{theoretical} \approx 11$ stages (including reboiler as one theoretical stage), with the feed introduced on **Stage 5 or 6** (whichever is optimal — the stage where stepping crosses the q-line).

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparison of Azeotropic vs. Extractive Distillation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Close-Boiling or Azeotropic Binary Feed] --> B{Following conditions?}
    B -- Azeotrope Present --> C[Azeotropic Distillation Path]
    B -- Close Boiling No Azeotrope --> D[Extractive Distillation Path]
    C --> C1[Step 1: Select Entrainer That Forms New Low-Boiling Azeotrope]
    C1 --> C2[Step 2: Operate Entrainer Column at High Reflux]
    C2 --> C3[Step 3: Top Vapor Condenses and Forms Two Liquid Phases in Decanter]
    C3 --> C4[Step 4: Separate Organic and Aqueous Layers in Decanter]
    C4 --> C5[Step 5: Recover Pure Component from One Layer]
    C5 --> C6[Step 6: Return Entrainer Rich Layer as Reflux]
    D --> D1[Step 1: Select High Boiling Polar Solvent]
    D1 --> D2[Step 2: Feed Solvent Near Top of Main Column]
    D2 --> D3[Step 3: Solvent Selectively Interacts with Polar Component]
    D3 --> D4[Step 4: Less Interactive Component Exits as Distillate]
    D4 --> D5[Step 5: Bottom Stream Enters Solvent Recovery Column]
    D5 --> D6[Step 6: Recovered Solvent Recycled to Main Column]
    C6 --> E[Final Pure Products]
    D6 --> E
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — McCabe–Thiele Method Construction Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Collect VLE Data and Design Specifications] --> S2[Step 1: Plot Equilibrium Curve on XY Diagram]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Draw 45 Degree Reference Line]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Mark Distillate Composition and Bottoms Composition on 45 Degree Line]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Compute Minimum Reflux Ratio R min Using Q Line and Equilibrium Curve]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Select Operating Reflux Ratio as 1.2 to 1.5 times R min]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Draw Rectifying Operating Line Through X D with Slope R Over R Plus 1]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Draw Q Line Through Feed Composition with Slope Q Over Q Minus 1]
    S8 --> S9[Step 8: Locate Intersection of Rectifying Line and Q Line]
    S9 --> S10[Step 9: Draw Stripping Operating Line Through This Intersection and X W Point]
    S10 --> S11[Step 10: Step Down from X D Between Operating Line and Equilibrium Curve]
    S11 --> S12[Step 11: Count Number of Steps to Find Theoretical Stages N]
    S12 --> S13[Step 12: Identify Feed Stage as the Step Crossing the Q Line]
    S13 --> S14[Step 13: Include Reboiler as One Theoretical Stage if Applicable]
    S14 --> S15[End: Report Total Theoretical Stages and Optimal Feed Location]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define azeotrope. Why is it not possible to separate an azeotropic mixture by simple distillation? Mention any one industrial example where azeotropic distillation is used.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
An **azeotrope** is a liquid mixture of two or more components that boils at a constant temperature and has the same composition in both liquid and vapor phases. At the azeotropic point, the relative volatility becomes unity ($\alpha = 1$), which means the liquid and vapor compositions are identical — the mixture behaves as a single substance and cannot be fractionated by simple distillation regardless of the number of stages.

**Industrial Example:** **Dehydration of ethanol** using **benzene** (or **cyclohexane**) as the entrainer. The ethanol–water mixture forms a minimum-boiling azeotrope at 95.6 mol% ethanol (78.2 °C). Benzene forms a new ternary minimum-boiling azeotrope with water and ethanol, allowing pure ethanol to be recovered as the bottoms product.

---

### Question 2
**Q: State the principle of extractive distillation. List the four essential properties of a good solvent used in extractive distillation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
**Principle:** In extractive distillation, a **high-boiling, non-volatile solvent** is introduced near the top of the column. The solvent selectively interacts with one component (the *heavy key*) through hydrogen bonding or polarity matching, altering its activity coefficient and increasing the **relative volatility** between the components. The component that does *not* interact with the solvent escapes as the distillate, while the solvent-rich mixture leaves from the bottom and is separated in a recovery column.

**Four Properties of a Good Solvent:**
1. **High selectivity** ($\beta > 1$) — must enhance relative volatility significantly.
2. **High boiling point** — at least 30–50 °C above the heavy key component to remain non-volatile.
3. **Thermal and chemical stability** — must not decompose at reboiler temperatures.
4. **Easy recoverability and low cost** — cheap, non-corrosive, and easily separable from the heavy key.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — McCabe–Thiele Method
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**A continuous distillation column is to be designed to separate a benzene–toluene mixture. The feed contains 40 mol% benzene, the distillate should contain 95 mol% benzene, and the bottoms should contain 5 mol% benzene. The feed is a saturated liquid, and the reflux ratio is to be 1.5 times the minimum. The relative volatility α = 2.47 may be assumed constant.**

**(a) Construct the McCabe–Thiele diagram and determine the number of theoretical stages required. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Locate the optimum feed plate and verify whether the assumption of constant molar overflow is valid. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Construction & Stage Count**

**Step 1: Equilibrium curve equation**
$$y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} = \frac{2.47 x}{1 + 1.47 x}$$

Generate equilibrium data:
- At $x = 0.05$: $y = \frac{2.47 \times 0.05}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.1235}{1.0735} = 0.1151$
- At $x = 0.40$: $y = \frac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222$
- At $x = 0.95$: $y = \frac{2.3465}{2.3965} = 0.9791$

**Step 2: Q-line for saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$)**
The q-line is vertical: $x = x_F = 0.40$

**Step 3: Minimum reflux ratio**
The rectifying operating line at $R_{min}$ passes through $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and intersects the q-line $x = 0.40$ at the equilibrium curve.

At $x = 0.40$: $y^* = 0.6222$ (from step 1)

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.6222}{0.6222 - 0.40} = \frac{0.3278}{0.2222} = 1.475$$

**Step 4: Operating reflux ratio**
$$R = 1.5 \times 1.475 = 2.213$$

**Step 5: Rectifying operating line**
- Slope = $\frac{R}{R+1} = \frac{2.213}{3.213} = 0.689$
- y-intercept = $\frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{0.95}{3.213} = 0.296$

Equation: $y = 0.689 x + 0.296$

**Step 6: Intersection with q-line at $x = 0.40$**
$$y = 0.689(0.40) + 0.296 = 0.2756 + 0.296 = 0.5716$$

**Step 7: Stripping operating line** through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and $(0.40, 0.5716)$:
- Slope = $\frac{0.5716 - 0.05}{0.40 - 0.05} = \frac{0.5216}{0.35} = 1.490$
- Equation: $y = 1.490 x - 0.0245$

**Step 8: Stepping** from $(0.95, 0.95)$ yields approximately **$N_{theoretical} \approx 10$ stages** (including the reboiler).

#### Part (b): Optimum Feed Plate & Constant Molar Overflow

**Optimum Feed Plate:** Identify the step at which the construction crosses the q-line ($x = 0.40$). In a typical stepping, the feed is introduced on the stage where the stepping transitions from the rectifying section to the stripping section — typically **Stage 5 or 6** from the top.

**Verification of Constant Molar Overflow:** The assumption is valid if:
1. **Molar latent heats of vaporization are equal** for both components (true for benzene–toluene; both ≈ 30 kJ/kmol).
2. **Sensible heat changes are negligible** compared to latent heat (true when $\Delta T$ across the column is small).
3. **Heat of mixing is negligible** (true for ideal or near-ideal benzene–toluene system).

For benzene–toluene, all three conditions are satisfied, so the **constant molar overflow (CMO) assumption is valid**, and the operating lines are straight as drawn.

---

### QUESTION B — Azeotropic & Extractive Distillation
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the principle of azeotropic distillation. Discuss the criteria for entrainer selection with reference to the ethanol–water system. (7 Marks)**
**(b) A mixture of acetone and methanol forms a minimum-boiling azeotrope at atmospheric pressure. Propose a suitable entrainer to break the azeotrope and explain the mechanism with VLE curves. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a): Azeotropic Distillation — Ethanol–Water System**

**Principle:** Azeotropic distillation uses a third component (entrainer) that forms a *new* azeotrope with one or both of the original components, making separation possible. The entrainer is selected such that the new azeotrope is **heterogeneous** (i.e., it separates into two immiscible liquid phases upon condensation).

**Block Diagram:**
```
        ┌───────────────────────────┐
        │     Azeotropic Column     │
        │                           │
Feed ──▶│   (Entrainer added near   │──▶ Bottom: Pure Ethanol
(EtOH+ │    top of column)          │
 H2O)  │                           │
        │   ┌────────────────┐      │
        └──▶│ Condenser      │◀─────┘
            └────┬───────────┘
                 │ Vapor
                 ▼
            ┌─────────┐
            │ Decanter│
            └────┬────┘
        Aqueous  │  Organic
        layer    │  layer (return as
        (water)  │   reflux/entrainer)
                 ▼
        Solvent Recovery
```

**Entrainer Selection Criteria (for Ethanol–Water):**
1. **Forms a heterogeneous ternary minimum-boiling azeotrope** with water (e.g., benzene forms a ternary azeotrope at 64.85 °C with composition: benzene 74.1%, ethanol 18.5%, water 7.4%).
2. **Low miscibility with water** — enables easy phase separation in the decanter.
3. **Higher volatility than ethanol** — exits with the top product.
4. **Easy recovery and low cost**.
5. **Non-toxic, non-corrosive, thermally stable**.

**Mechanism:** Benzene + water + ethanol form a new ternary azeotrope (B.P. = 64.85 °C) which is *lower* than the binary ethanol–water azeotrope (78.2 °C). This new azeotrope leaves the top, condenses, and forms two liquid phases in the decanter. The aqueous phase (water-rich) is removed; the benzene-rich phase is returned as reflux. Pure **anhydrous ethanol** is recovered as the bottoms product.

#### Part (b): Breaking the Acetone–Methanol Azeotrope

**Problem:** Acetone (B.P. 56.1 °C) and methanol (B.P. 64.7 °C) form a minimum-boiling azeotrope at ~80 mol% acetone, B.P. 55.7 °C — making separation by simple distillation impossible.

**Proposed Entrainer:** **Water (H₂O)** is a classical entrainer for this system. Water is highly polar and forms strong hydrogen bonds with methanol, drastically reducing methanol's effective vapor pressure.

**Mechanism with VLE curves:**

1. **Without water:** Acetone–methanol forms a low-boiling azeotrope. The x–y curve touches the 45° line at the azeotropic point — **no separation possible**.

2. **With water added:** The activity coefficient of methanol $\gamma_{MeOH}$ increases sharply (water–methanol hydrogen bonding is strong), while $\gamma_{acetone}$ changes only slightly. This breaks the azeotrope — the x–y curve no longer touches the 45° line, and **acetone can be recovered as a pure distillate** because the modified equilibrium curve now shows acetone as the more volatile component throughout the composition range.

3. Methanol and water leave the bottom and are separated in a second (solvent recovery) column. Water is recycled.

**Alternative Entrainers:** **Ethylene glycol**, **1,2-propanediol**, or **DMSO** can also be used for this separation in extractive distillation mode.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the reboiler as a theoretical stage** — it always counts as one equilibrium stage. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Drawing the q-line incorrectly** — students often compute $q$ but draw the line through the *wrong* intercept. For $q = 1$, the q-line is **vertical at $x_F$**, not through the origin.
> 3. **Confusing $R_{min}$ formula** — the correct form is $\frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$, *not* $\frac{x_D - x_F}{y_F^* - x_F}$. A sign error is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using wrong reflux ratio convention** — KTU uses $R = L/D$ (external reflux ratio). Some textbooks use $R = L/V$. Always state the convention.
> 5. **In azeotropic distillation, not mentioning the role of the decanter** — this is the *defining feature* of heterogeneous azeotropic distillation. Omission loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **In extractive distillation, not drawing the solvent feed location** — solvent enters *near the top*, *above* the main feed. This is a common descriptive question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Azeotropic Distillation — Key Points
- Used to **break azeotropes** that cannot be separated by simple distillation.
- Requires a **third component (entrainer)** that forms a *new* low-boiling azeotrope.
- Industrial use: **Ethanol dehydration** with benzene/cyclohexane.
- **Heterogeneous** vs **homogeneous** azeotropic distillation.
- **Decanter** is essential for phase separation in heterogeneous systems.

### Extractive Distillation — Key Points
- Used for **close-boiling mixtures** with α ≈ 1 (no azeotrope).
- Uses a **high-boiling, non-volatile solvent** fed near the top.
- **Solvent selectivity** β is the key performance metric.
- Solvent is **recovered and recycled** in a separate column.
- Industrial examples: butene isomer separation with **furfural**; BTX aromatics with **sulfolane**.

### McCabe–Thiele Method — Key Points
- Graphical method to find **theoretical stages** in binary distillation.
- Requires: **equilibrium curve**, **rectifying line**, **stripping line**, **q-line**, and **45° line**.
- **Reflux ratio $R = L/D$** — operating parameter selected as 1.2–1.5 × $R_{min}$.
- **$R_{min}$ formula:** $R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$ (for $q = 1$).
- **Q-line slope:** $\frac{q}{q-1}$ — depends on feed thermal condition.
- **Constant molar overflow** assumption makes operating lines straight.
- **Optimum feed plate** = the stage where stepping crosses the q-line.
- **Reboiler counts as one theoretical stage**.

### Critical Numerical Values to Memorize
- $R_{min}$ formula: $\frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$
- Rectifying slope: $\frac{R}{R+1}$
- q-line slope: $\frac{q}{q-1}$
- Ethanol–water azeotrope: **95.6 mol% EtOH, 78.2 °C**
- Benzene–toluene α ≈ **2.47** at 1 atm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always (1) draw a *clear labeled* x–y diagram with all 5 lines, (2) show *stepping* with clear arrows, (3) state the feed stage location, and (4) mention the **reboiler as one theoretical stage** in your final count. Marks are awarded stepwise, so even partial constructions fetch partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total, Minimum and Optimum Reflux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total, Minimum and Optimum Reflux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Reflux in a Distillation Column?

In continuous distillation, **reflux (L)** is the condensed overhead vapor that is returned back to the top of the fractionating column. It is the lifeblood of the column because without sufficient reflux, the ascending vapor cannot be adequately scrubbed of its heavier components, and no separation would occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** The **reflux ratio (R)** is formally defined as the ratio of the liquid returned as reflux to the overhead vapor product (distillate) withdrawn:
> $$R = \frac{L}{D}$$
> where **L** = molar flow rate of reflux (kmol/h) and **D** = molar flow rate of distillate (kmol/h).

## 1.2 The Three Limiting Reflux Conditions

A distillation column does not operate at *just one* reflux ratio. Designers evaluate three special boundary conditions to bracket the operating envelope:

| Condition | Reflux Ratio | Number of Stages | Column Diameter |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Total Reflux (R → ∞)** | Infinity | Minimum (N_min) | Maximum |
| **Minimum Reflux (R_min)** | Lowest possible | Infinite (N = ∞) | Minimum |
| **Optimum Reflux (R_opt)** | ~ 1.2 to 1.5 × R_min | Finite (N_opt) | Economic optimum |

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Washing Machine" Picture

Imagine you are washing dirty clothes (separating solute from solvent):
- **Total Reflux** = You keep rinsing and recirculating the same water over and over. After infinite rinses, the clothes become perfectly clean, BUT you never actually take any clean clothes out, and the washing machine becomes huge.
- **Minimum Reflux** = You use the bare minimum amount of rinse water. The clothes get *almost* clean, but you would need an impossibly tall washing machine to finish the job.
- **Optimum Reflux** = You choose a rinse-water flow rate and machine size such that the *total cost of equipment + water + energy* is the lowest. This is what real engineers design for.

## 1.3 Why Are These Three Conditions Important?

> [!NOTE]
> Every real distillation column must operate with a finite reflux ratio lying **between R_min and ∞**. The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner routinely tests the student's ability to:
> 1. Plot operating lines at **Total Reflux** to find the *minimum number of theoretical stages* (N_min) using the McCabe–Thiele stepping method.
> 2. Use the **q-line** intersection with the equilibrium curve to compute **R_min**.
> 3. Apply the **Gilliland correlation** (or Erbar-Maddox) to find the **optimum number of stages** at the optimum reflux ratio.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Section Operating Line

For the rectifying (top) section of the column, performing a component material balance around the envelope from the top tray down to any tray *n*:

$$V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

Since by definition in the rectifying section: $V = L + D$, and dividing throughout by $V$:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line (ROL)**. Its slope is $\frac{R}{R+1}$ and its y-intercept is $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Stripping Section Operating Line

Similarly, for the stripping (bottom) section, the material balance yields:

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

where $L' = L + qF$ and $V' = L' - W$. The slope is $\frac{L'}{V'}$ and the x-intercept is $x_W$.

## 2.3 The q-Line Equation (Feed Condition Line)

The intersection point of the rectifying and stripping lines is governed by the **thermal condition of the feed**, parameterised by *q*:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

| Feed Condition | q value | q-line Slope |
|----------------|---------|--------------|
| Cold liquid | q > 1 | Between 0 and 1 |
| Saturated liquid | q = 1 | Vertical line x = z_F |
| Two-phase | 0 < q < 1 | Positive slope > 1 |
| Saturated vapor | q = 0 | Horizontal line y = z_F |
| Superheated vapor | q < 0 | Negative slope |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Significance |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Reflux Ratio | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Rectifying Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Slope = R/(R+1) |
| 3 | Stripping Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ | Slope = L'/V' |
| 4 | q-line Equation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Defines feed stage |
| 5 | Total Reflux Condition | $R \to \infty$ ⟹ operating line: $y = x$ | N = N_min (Fenske Eq.) |
| 6 | Minimum Reflux Ratio | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*}$ | From q-line intercept |
| 7 | Optimum Reflux Ratio | $R_{opt} \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_{min}$ | Economic optimum |
| 8 | Underwood Equation (Multicomponent) | $\sum \dfrac{\alpha_i z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q$ | Solved for $\theta$ |
| 9 | Gilliland Correlation | $\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = f\!\left(\dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}\right)$ | Graphical/Eduljee form |
| 10 | Eduljee Approximation | $\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = 0.75 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}\right)^{0.5668}\right]$ | Direct calculation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Total Reflux** is used at *column startup* to establish equilibrium profiles before product withdrawal begins.
- **R_min** acts as the *theoretical lower bound* — it dictates the *minimum energy* (reboiler duty) needed.
- **R_opt** is what every process engineer actually designs for; the rule-of-thumb **1.3 R_min** is widely used in petroleum refinery FCC main fractionators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Total Reflux Operating Line

We start with the rectifying operating line:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Step 1:** As $R \to \infty$, examine the slope term. Divide numerator and denominator by R:

$$\frac{R}{R+1} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{R}}$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit:

$$\lim_{R \to \infty} \frac{R}{R+1} = \lim_{R \to \infty} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{1}{1+0} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Examine the y-intercept $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$. As R → ∞:

$$\lim_{R \to \infty} \frac{x_D}{R+1} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into the operating line:

$$\boxed{y_{n+1} = x_n}$$

This is the **45° diagonal** — no separation is being removed, all material is internally recycled. The number of stages required under total reflux is the **minimum possible** and is calculated using the **Fenske equation** for multicomponent or simply by stepping from $x_D$ to $x_W$ on the McCabe–Thiele diagram.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Minimum Reflux Ratio

**Step 1:** Sketch the McCabe–Thiele diagram. The rectifying line passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° line with slope $R/(R+1)$.

**Step 2:** The q-line is drawn from $(z_F, z_F)$ with slope $q/(q-1)$.

**Step 3:** The intersection of the rectifying line and the q-line occurs at the **pinch point** $(x^*, y^*)$ where it touches the equilibrium curve.

**Step 4:** Apply the rectifying line equation at this intersection point $(x^*, y^*)$:

$$y^* = \frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} x^* + \frac{x_D}{R_{min}+1}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to solve for R_min. Multiply through by $(R_{min}+1)$:

$$y^*(R_{min}+1) = R_{min} x^* + x_D$$

$$R_{min}(y^* - x^*) = x_D - y^*$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested formula** in KTU Module 4. Memorise it!

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Optimum Reflux Ratio via Total Cost Optimisation

**Step 1:** The total annualized cost of the distillation system has two opposing contributions:

$$C_{total}(R) = C_{equipment}(R) + C_{operating}(R)$$

**Step 2:** As R increases from R_min:
- Number of stages N decreases → column shell cost ↓
- But vapor boil-up V increases → reboiler + condenser duty ↑ → operating cost ↑

**Step 3:** Differentiate the total cost with respect to R and set to zero for the optimum:

$$\frac{d C_{total}}{d R} = \frac{d C_{equipment}}{d R} + \frac{d C_{operating}}{d R} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Because this derivative has no closed-form analytical solution, the empirical result (based on decades of industrial design data) gives the working rule:

$$\boxed{R_{opt} \approx 1.2 \, R_{min} \text{ to } 1.5 \, R_{min}}$$

A commonly used design value is **R_opt = 1.3 R_min**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (more volatile) and toluene is distilled at 1 atm. Feed: $z_F = 0.50$, Distillate: $x_D = 0.95$, Bottoms: $x_W = 0.05$. The feed is a saturated liquid (q = 1). The equilibrium curve pinch occurs at $(x^*, y^*) = (0.50, 0.72)$ on the equilibrium curve at the q-line intersection.

**Part (a):** Calculate R_min.

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*} = \frac{0.95 - 0.72}{0.72 - 0.50} = \frac{0.23}{0.22} \approx 1.045$$

**Part (b):** Find R_opt assuming 1.3 R_min.

$$R_{opt} = 1.3 \times 1.045 \approx 1.36$$

**Part (c):** At total reflux, using Fenske's equation (α_avg ≈ 2.47):

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \frac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{0.95}{0.05} \cdot \frac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{5.89}{0.904} \approx 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}$$

**Part (d):** Use Eduljee approximation to find N_opt:

$$\frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \frac{1.36 - 1.045}{1.36 + 1} = \frac{0.315}{2.36} \approx 0.1335$$

$$\frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = 0.75 \left[1 - (0.1335)^{0.5668}\right] = 0.75\left[1 - 0.323\right] = 0.75 \times 0.677 = 0.508$$

$$N - 7 = 0.508(N + 1) \Rightarrow N - 7 = 0.508N + 0.508 \Rightarrow 0.492 N = 7.508 \Rightarrow N \approx 15.3 \approx 16 \text{ stages}$$

This 16-stage design is then rounded up and the feed stage identified graphically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Feed Mixture F zF"] --> B["Feed Stage Location"]
    B --> C["Rectifying Section Top"]
    C --> D["Condenser Duty Qc"]
    D --> E["Reflux Drum"]
    E --> F["Distillate Product D xD"]
    E --> G["Reflux Stream L back to column"]
    G --> C
    B --> H["Stripping Section Bottom"]
    H --> I["Reboiler Duty Qr"]
    I --> J["Bottoms Product W xW"]
    I --> K["Vapor boil up V back to column"]
    K --> H
    L["Operating Parameter R = L by D"] --> M{"Reflux Regime Decision"}
    M --> N["Total Reflux R infinite"]
    M --> O["Minimum Reflux Rmin"]
    M --> P["Optimum Reflux Ropt"]
    N --> N1["Operating line y equals x"]
    N --> N2["Minimum stages Nmin Fenske"]
    O --> O1["Pinch point on eq curve"]
    O --> O2["Rmin equals xD minus ystar over ystar minus xstar"]
    P --> P1["Gilliland or Erbar Maddox"]
    P --> P2["Economic balance"]
    P1 --> P3["Optimum stages Nopt"]
    P2 --> P3
    N2 --> Q["Design Output Nopt and Ropt"]
    O2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description of the McCabe–Thiele Diagram Behaviour

On the **x-y equilibrium plot**, the three reflux regimes can be visually distinguished:

- **Total Reflux (R = ∞):** The rectifying line *coincides* with the 45° diagonal. The stepping process between the operating line (y = x) and the equilibrium curve yields the **fewest possible stages**.
- **Minimum Reflux (R = R_min):** The rectifying and stripping operating lines both pass through the *intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve*. The stepping process requires an **infinite number of stages** because the steps asymptotically converge to the pinch point.
- **Optimum Reflux (R = R_opt):** The operating lines lie in an intermediate region. The stepping process between operating lines and the equilibrium curve gives a **finite, economically optimised number of stages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As R increases from R_min toward ∞, the slope of the rectifying line increases from a minimum toward 1, the stepping process converges faster, and N decreases. But the column diameter must increase to handle the larger vapor load V = D(R+1).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *minimum reflux ratio* and *total reflux* in distillation. Why can a distillation column not be operated exactly at R_min or at total reflux?

**Model Answer:**

**Minimum Reflux Ratio (R_min):** It is the *smallest* reflux ratio at which the desired separation between distillate and bottoms compositions is theoretically achievable. At this condition, the intersection of the rectifying operating line with the q-line lies *on* the equilibrium curve, creating a pinch point that requires an *infinite* number of theoretical stages.

**Total Reflux:** It is the operating condition at which *no product is withdrawn* from the column — all the condensate is returned as reflux and all the vapor from the reboiler is recirculated. The reflux ratio is mathematically infinite (R = ∞). The operating line collapses to the 45° diagonal y = x, and the minimum number of stages (N_min) is obtained.

**Why not operate at these extremes:**
- At R = R_min: requires infinite trays → physically impossible.
- At total reflux: produces zero product → no separation output.

The actual operating reflux must lie **between R_min and ∞**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the Underwood equation and explain the role of the parameter q in the q-line equation.

**Model Answer:**

**Underwood Equation (for multicomponent minimum reflux):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{\alpha_i \, z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q$$

where $\alpha_i$ is the relative volatility of component *i*, $z_{F,i}$ is its feed mole fraction, and $\theta$ is the Underwood root (between the relative volatilities of the LK and HK). This equation is solved for $\theta$, and the minimum reflux is computed via:

$$R_{min} = \sum_{i} \frac{\alpha_i \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

**Role of q:** The parameter q represents the *molar fraction of feed that is liquid*. It determines the slope of the q-line ($q/(q-1)$), which in turn dictates the **pinch point location** on the equilibrium curve. Different feed thermal conditions (cold liquid, saturated liquid, two-phase, saturated vapor, superheated vapor) yield different q values and therefore different R_min values, even for identical compositions.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The most common mark-losing mistakes on this topic are:
> 1. Forgetting to convert the feed condition into the q-value before drawing the q-line.
> 2. Mis-identifying the pinch point as the intersection of the rectifying and stripping lines — it is actually the intersection of the **q-line with the equilibrium curve**.
> 3. Using R instead of $R_{min}$ in the Gilliland correlation. Always use $R_{min}$ as the reference lower bound.
> 4. Confusing N_min (Fenske result) with N_opt (Gilliland result) — these are different physical quantities.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A continuous distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture at 1 atm. Given:

- Feed: $z_F = 0.40$, q = 1 (saturated liquid), $F = 100$ kmol/h
- Distillate: $x_D = 0.95$
- Bottoms: $x_W = 0.05$
- Equilibrium data (benzene more volatile): The pinch point at the q-line/equilibrium intersection is $(x^*, y^*) = (0.40, 0.62)$.
- Average relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene: $\alpha_{avg} = 2.47$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate R_min and the rectifying operating line equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate N_min using Fenske's equation, and the optimum number of stages using the Gilliland correlation (with Eduljee approximation) at R_opt = 1.3 R_min.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):**

Step 1: Apply the R_min formula at the pinch point:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*} = \frac{0.95 - 0.62}{0.62 - 0.40} = \frac{0.33}{0.22} = 1.50$$

Step 2: Calculate the slope of the rectifying operating line:

$$\frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} = \frac{1.50}{2.50} = 0.60$$

Step 3: Calculate the y-intercept:

$$\frac{x_D}{R_{min}+1} = \frac{0.95}{2.50} = 0.38$$

Step 4: Write the rectifying operating line:

$$\boxed{y = 0.60 \, x + 0.38}$$

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1: Apply Fenske's equation for N_min:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \frac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

Substitute:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{0.95}{0.05} \cdot \frac{0.95}{0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{0.904} = \frac{5.89}{0.904} \approx 6.51$$

Round up to **7 stages** (N_min ≈ 7).

Step 2: Compute R_opt:

$$R_{opt} = 1.3 \times 1.50 = 1.95$$

Step 3: Compute the Gilliland X-coordinate:

$$X = \frac{R_{opt} - R_{min}}{R_{opt} + 1} = \frac{1.95 - 1.50}{1.95 + 1} = \frac{0.45}{2.95} \approx 0.1525$$

Step 4: Apply Eduljee approximation for the Y-coordinate:

$$Y = 0.75 \left[1 - X^{0.5668}\right] = 0.75 \left[1 - (0.1525)^{0.5668}\right]$$

Compute $(0.1525)^{0.5668}$:

$$\ln(0.1525) \times 0.5668 = (-1.881) \times 0.5668 = -1.066$$
$$(0.1525)^{0.5668} = e^{-1.066} \approx 0.3444$$

Therefore:

$$Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.3444) = 0.75 \times 0.6556 \approx 0.4917$$

Step 5: Back-calculate N_opt:

$$\frac{N_{opt} - N_{min}}{N_{opt} + 1} = 0.4917$$

$$N_{opt} - 7 = 0.4917(N_{opt} + 1)$$

$$N_{opt} - 7 = 0.4917 \, N_{opt} + 0.4917$$

$$0.5083 \, N_{opt} = 7.4917$$

$$N_{opt} = \frac{7.4917}{0.5083} \approx 14.74 \approx 15 \text{ stages}$$

**Final Answer:** R_min = 1.50, ROL: y = 0.60x + 0.38, N_min = 7 stages, N_opt ≈ 15 stages.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A methanol–water mixture is distilled at atmospheric pressure. Given:

- Feed: $z_F = 0.30$, feed is a two-phase mixture with q = 0.6, $F = 50$ kmol/h
- Distillate: $x_D = 0.98$
- Bottoms: $x_W = 0.02$
- Average relative volatility of methanol with respect to water: $\alpha_{avg} = 3.30$
- The q-line intersects the equilibrium curve at $(x^*, y^*) = (0.40, 0.78)$.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine R_min and the equation of the q-line.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute N_min using Fenske's equation and find R_opt such that the operating cost equals the equipment cost contribution when the optimum satisfies $R_{opt}/R_{min} = 1.25$.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**

Step 1: Compute R_min:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*} = \frac{0.98 - 0.78}{0.78 - 0.40} = \frac{0.20}{0.38} \approx 0.5263$$

Step 2: Compute the slope of the q-line:

$$\frac{q}{q-1} = \frac{0.6}{0.6 - 1} = \frac{0.6}{-0.4} = -1.50$$

Step 3: Compute the q-line intercept:

$$-\frac{z_F}{q-1} = -\frac{0.30}{-0.4} = 0.75$$

Step 4: Write the q-line equation:

$$\boxed{y = -1.50 \, x + 0.75}$$

**Verification:** At $x = z_F = 0.30$: $y = -1.50(0.30) + 0.75 = -0.45 + 0.75 = 0.30$ ✓ (passes through $(z_F, z_F)$).

---

**Part (b):**

Step 1: Fenske's equation:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \frac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\frac{0.98}{0.02} \cdot \frac{0.98}{0.02}\right]}{\ln 3.30}$$

$$= \frac{\ln(2401)}{1.194} = \frac{7.783}{1.194} \approx 6.52 \approx 7 \text{ stages}$$

Step 2: Compute R_opt:

$$R_{opt} = 1.25 \times R_{min} = 1.25 \times 0.5263 \approx 0.658$$

Step 3: Gilliland X-coordinate:

$$X = \frac{R_{opt} - R_{min}}{R_{opt} + 1} = \frac{0.658 - 0.5263}{0.658 + 1} = \frac{0.1317}{1.658} \approx 0.0794$$

Step 4: Eduljee Y-coordinate:

$$Y = 0.75\left[1 - (0.0794)^{0.5668}\right]$$

Compute $(0.0794)^{0.5668}$:

$$\ln(0.0794) = -2.534$$
$$-2.534 \times 0.5668 = -1.436$$
$$e^{-1.436} \approx 0.2382$$

$$Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.2382) = 0.75 \times 0.7618 = 0.5714$$

Step 5: Solve for N_opt:

$$N_{opt} - 7 = 0.5714(N_{opt} + 1)$$
$$N_{opt} - 7 = 0.5714 N_{opt} + 0.5714$$
$$0.4286 N_{opt} = 7.5714$$
$$N_{opt} = \frac{7.5714}{0.4286} \approx 17.67 \approx 18 \text{ stages}$$

**Final Answer:** R_min ≈ 0.526, q-line: y = −1.50x + 0.75, N_min = 7 stages, R_opt ≈ 0.658, N_opt ≈ 18 stages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Total, Minimum and Optimum Reflux**

- ✅ **Reflux ratio** $R = L/D$ — dimensionless, always positive.
- ✅ **Total reflux**: R → ∞, operating line y = x, minimum stages by **Fenske equation**.
- ✅ **Minimum reflux**: operating line passes through pinch point $(x^*, y^*)$ where q-line meets equilibrium curve → infinite stages required.
- ✅ **Key formula (most important)**: $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y^*}{y^* - x^*}$.
- ✅ **q-line** defines feed thermal condition: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$.
- ✅ **q = 1** → vertical q-line at $x = z_F$ (saturated liquid feed).
- ✅ **Optimum reflux** is the economic compromise between column cost (decreases with R) and operating cost (increases with R).
- ✅ **Design rule**: $R_{opt} \approx 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \times R_{min}$; commonly $R_{opt} = 1.3 R_{min}$.
- ✅ **Gilliland correlation** in Eduljee form: $\dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = 0.75\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}\right)^{0.5668}\right]$.
- ✅ **Underwood equation** is used for multicomponent R_min calculation.
- ✅ Always convert molar compositions, mass compositions, and feed thermal conditions *before* applying any formula.
- ✅ Real column operates with **actual stages** = $N_{theoretical}/\text{stage efficiency}$, typically 0.5–0.7 for sieve trays.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying: Equilibrium Moisture and Free Moisture, Critical Moisture Content, Bound and Unbound Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying: Equilibrium Moisture and Free Moisture, Critical Moisture Content, Bound and Unbound Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Drying?

**Drying** is a unit operation in chemical engineering that involves the removal of a volatile liquid (usually water) from a solid material by the application of heat, under controlled conditions. It is fundamentally a combined **heat transfer** (to evaporate the liquid) and **mass transfer** (to move the vapor away from the surface) process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> *Drying is defined as the final removal of small quantities of moisture (or other volatile substances) from a material by thermal means, employing air as the drying medium to carry away the vaporized moisture.*

The **primary purpose** of drying in the process industry is to:
1. **Reduce transportation cost** (water adds dead weight).
2. **Prevent microbial spoilage** (e.g., food grains, pharmaceuticals).
3. **Enable downstream handling** (granulation, packaging, storage).
4. **Achieve a specified product specification** (final moisture content).

---

## 1.2 Key Moisture Terminologies — The "Big Four"

The single biggest confusion students face in Module 4 is the difference between the **four types of moisture**. Let us decode each one with crystal-clear definitions.

### A. Moisture Content (Basis of Expression)

The amount of water present in a wet solid can be expressed on two bases:

$$X = \frac{\text{Mass of moisture (water)}}{\text{Mass of dry solid (bone-dry solid)}}$$

This is called the **dry basis moisture content** and is the **preferred engineering basis** in KTU problems. It is dimensionless but often written as **kg water / kg dry solid**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet Basis ($X_w$):** Mass of moisture divided by total mass of wet solid. Used commercially (e.g., "10% moisture" on a fertilizer bag).
> **Dry Basis ($X$):** Mass of moisture divided by mass of completely dry solid. Used in all engineering calculations.

The conversion formula is:

$$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w} \quad \text{and} \quad X_w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

---

### B. Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Equilibrium Moisture Content is the moisture content of a solid which is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air (at a given temperature and relative humidity). At this state, no net transfer of moisture occurs between the solid and the air.*

**Key insight:** A material can NEVER be dried below its equilibrium moisture content using air of a given humidity. You can dry forever and the solid will still retain $X^*$ of water.

### C. Free Moisture Content ($X - X^*$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Free Moisture Content is the moisture content in excess of the equilibrium moisture content. This is the portion of water that can actually be removed by drying under the prevailing air conditions.*

$$\boxed{X_{\text{free}} = X - X^*}$$

### D. Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Critical Moisture Content is the moisture content of the solid at which the drying rate begins to drop. Below this value, the entire exposed surface is no longer saturated with liquid, and the drying mechanism transitions from surface evaporation to internal diffusion.*

### E. Bound and Unbound Water

- **Unbound Water:** Water that exerts a vapor pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature. It behaves as if it were pure liquid water sitting on the surface.
- **Bound Water:** Water that is held within the solid structure such that its vapor pressure is **less than** that of pure water at the same temperature. It requires extra energy to remove.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge Story"

Imagine a **wet kitchen sponge** placed on a table in a humid kitchen.

| Concept | Sponge Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Moisture** | Total water absorbed in the sponge | Mass of water in the wet solid |
| **Equilibrium Moisture** | Water that the sponge holds even after hours in that humid room (it will never dry below this) | The moisture that air of given T & RH cannot pull out |
| **Free Moisture** | The water that evaporates in the first few hours when you keep the sponge on the table | The water that is removable by drying |
| **Critical Moisture** | The moisture at which the sponge stops "shining" wet on its surface (becomes only damp internally) | Transition point from constant to falling rate |
| **Bound Water** | Water trapped inside the polymer network of the sponge (chemically/physically bound) | Water with vapor pressure < pure water |
| **Unbound Water** | Free-flowing water in the sponge pores that behaves like bulk water | Water with vapor pressure = pure water |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this:** The dividing line between Bound and Unbound water is **vapor pressure equality with pure water**. The dividing line between Free and Equilibrium is **removability by the drying air**.

---

## 1.4 Summary Block Diagram

```
TOTAL MOISTURE (X)
       │
       ├──> EQUILIBRIUM MOISTURE (X*)  ──── Cannot be removed by drying
       │        (Bound + a portion of free that air cannot take)
       │
       └──> FREE MOISTURE (X - X*)    ──── CAN be removed by drying
                │
                ├──> UNBOUND WATER   ──── Behaves like pure water (vp = vp°)
                │
                └──> BOUND WATER     ──── vp < vp° (trapped in solid)
                       │
                       └──> Critical Moisture Content (Xc) marks the
                            transition between surface-controlled and
                            internal diffusion-controlled drying.
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Equilibrium Moisture Content — Thermodynamic Origin

The equilibrium moisture content is **NOT a property of the solid alone**. It depends on:
1. **Relative Humidity (RH)** of the surrounding air.
2. **Temperature (T)** of the air.

A plot of $X^*$ vs RH at constant T is called the **Sorption Isotherm**. The most common form (BET Isotherm for multilayer adsorption) is:

$$\frac{X^*}{X_m} = \frac{C \cdot RH}{(1 - RH)(1 + (C - 1) RH)}$$

where $X_m$ is the monolayer moisture content and $C$ is a constant related to heat of adsorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When RH = 0 (perfectly dry air), $X^* = 0$. When RH = 1 (saturated air), $X^*$ is maximum. So equilibrium moisture **increases monotonically** with relative humidity.

---

### 2.1.2 Free Moisture Content — The "Removable" Portion

Free moisture is the **driving force** for drying. The drying rate is fundamentally proportional to the free moisture (in the constant-rate region).

$$R \propto (X - X^*) \quad \text{(approximately, in falling rate region)}$$

- If $X > X^*$ → Drying is possible (free moisture exists).
- If $X = X^*$ → Drying stops (no free moisture).
- $X^*$ depends on air conditions, so the same solid may be "wet" in summer (low RH) and "dry" in monsoon (high RH) — interesting!

---

### 2.1.3 Critical Moisture Content — The Transition Point

The critical moisture content is the moisture at which the **constant rate drying period ends** and the **falling rate period begins**.

**Physical interpretation:**
- **Above $X_c$:** The surface is fully wetted. Drying rate is governed by **external mass/heat transfer** (i.e., the air-side conditions).
- **Below $X_c$:** The surface starts becoming "patchy dry". Liquid water cannot reach the surface fast enough. Drying rate is governed by **internal diffusion** of moisture to the surface.

**Factors affecting $X_c$:**
| Factor | Effect on $X_c$ |
|---|---|
| Increasing air velocity | $X_c$ **increases** (faster external drying → surface dries earlier) |
| Increasing air temperature | $X_c$ **increases** |
| Increasing air humidity | $X_c$ **decreases** |
| Thicker slabs / larger particles | $X_c$ **increases** (longer path for internal diffusion) |
| More porous materials | $X_c$ **decreases** (easier internal transport) |

---

### 2.1.4 Bound vs Unbound Water — Molecular View

**Unbound water:**
- Held in capillaries and on surfaces.
- Vapor pressure = Vapor pressure of pure water ($p_w = p_w^0$ at that T).
- Removed easily during constant-rate period.

**Bound water:**
- Held by physical adsorption (van der Waals) or chemical bonds.
- Vapor pressure **<** pure water vapor pressure ($p_w < p_w^0$).
- Released only with difficulty during falling-rate period.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trap Question:** "Is all unbound water free moisture?"
> **Answer: No.** Unbound water can exist *below* $X^*$ as well? Actually, by definition: water with $p_w = p_w^0$ is unbound, but if the air humidity is such that $X^* > 0$, even some unbound water may not be removed. In standard KTU textbooks (Treybal, McCabe), **unbound water is identified with free moisture that can be evaporated at $p_w = p_w^0$**, while bound water has $p_w < p_w^0$.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} = \dfrac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}$ | Dry basis moisture content | dimensionless |
| 2 | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$ | Conversion wet → dry basis | dimensionless |
| 3 | $X_{\text{free}} = X - X^*$ | Free moisture content | kg/kg dry |
| 4 | $X_{\text{bound}} = X^*$ (when $X > X^*$) | Bound water ≈ equilibrium moisture | kg/kg dry |
| 5 | $X_{\text{unbound}} = X - X^*$ | Unbound water at given air RH | kg/kg dry |
| 6 | $R_c = h_g (T_{\text{air}} - T_s) / \lambda_s$ | Constant rate drying rate (heat transfer limited) | kg/m²·s |
| 7 | $R_c = k_y (H_s - H_{\text{air}})$ | Constant rate (mass transfer basis) | kg/m²·s |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -\dfrac{R_c}{L \rho_s}$ | Drying rate during constant rate period | 1/s |
| 9 | $t_c = \dfrac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c}$ | Time to reach critical moisture | s |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -\dfrac{R_c (X - X^*)}{X_c - X^*}$ | Falling rate (linear model) | 1/s |
| 11 | $t_f = \dfrac{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}{R_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Falling rate time (linear model) | s |
| 12 | $t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$ | Total drying time | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **L** = half-thickness of slab (m), **$\rho_s$** = bulk density of dry solid (kg/m³), **$\lambda_s$** = latent heat at surface T, **$k_y$** = mass transfer coefficient, **$H$** = humidity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use of These Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Food Processing** | Drying of grains, milk powder, spices — knowing $X_c$ helps design batch driers |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Lyophilization (freeze-drying) of vaccines — bound water removal is critical |
| **Ceramics & Pulp/Paper** | Drying stress cracks depend on falling-rate kinetics |
| **Textile Industry** | $X^*$ of cotton at 65% RH ≈ 7-8%; this affects "bone-dry" claims |
| **Agriculture Storage** | Storage moisture (must be $<X^*$ at storage RH to prevent mold) |
| **Polymer Processing** | PET pellets must be dried below 50 ppm to prevent hydrolysis in extrusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **equilibrium moisture content is the lower limit** for any drying process. Air-drying can never achieve absolute dryness. To achieve bone-dry solids, one must use **desiccant drying** or **vacuum drying**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Critical Moisture and Drying Conditions

### Step 1 — Mass Balance on a Slab Being Dried

Consider a slab of wet solid of thickness $2L$ (half-thickness = $L$). The mass of dry solid per unit area is $L \rho_s$.
