The total moisture per unit area is $L \rho_s X$.

The instantaneous rate of moisture removal is:

$$-\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(L \rho_s X\right) = R$$

Since $L$ and $\rho_s$ are constants during drying:

$$-L \rho_s \frac{dX}{dt} = R \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dX}{dt} = -\frac{R}{L \rho_s}$$

### Step 2 — Constant Rate Drying Region ($X > X_c$)

In the constant rate region, the surface is fully wet and the drying rate is determined by **external heat transfer**:

$$R = R_c = \frac{h (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s}$$

This is independent of $X$. Integrating from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$:

$$\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = -\frac{R_c}{L \rho_s} \int_0^{t_c} dt$$

$$X_c - X_1 = -\frac{R_c \, t_c}{L \rho_s}$$

Solving for time to reach critical moisture:

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c}}$$

### Step 3 — Falling Rate Drying Region ($X < X_c$)

In the falling rate region, the simplest model is the **linear falling rate model**:

$$R = R_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Substituting into the mass balance:

$$-L \rho_s \frac{dX}{dt} = R_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{R_c}{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)} \, dt$$

Integrating from $X = X_c$ at $t = 0$ (start of falling rate, local time) to $X = X_2$ at $t = t_f$:

$$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{R_c}{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) = -\frac{R_c \, t_f}{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}$$

Solving for $t_f$:

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}{R_c} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Total Drying Time

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = \frac{L \rho_s}{R_c}\left[(X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X^*)\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\right]}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (14-Mark Type)

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> A wet solid is dried in a tray drier from an initial moisture content of 0.40 kg water/kg dry solid to a final moisture of 0.05 kg water/kg dry solid. The equilibrium moisture content is 0.02 kg/kg and the critical moisture content is 0.15 kg/kg dry solid. The constant drying rate is $R_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg water/m²·s. The slab is 25 mm thick (half-thickness = 12.5 mm) and the dry solid density is 1200 kg/m³.
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The time required for the constant rate drying period.
> (b) The additional time required for the falling rate period.
> (c) The total drying time.

### Given Data

$$X_1 = 0.40, \quad X_c = 0.15, \quad X_2 = 0.05, \quad X^* = 0.02$$

$$R_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}, \quad L = 0.0125 \text{ m}, \quad \rho_s = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

### Part (a): Constant Rate Time

$$t_c = \frac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c}$$

$$t_c = \frac{(0.0125)(1200)(0.40 - 0.15)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = \frac{(0.0125)(1200)(0.25)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = \frac{3.75}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 25{,}000 \text{ s} \approx 6.94 \text{ hours}}$$

### Part (b): Falling Rate Time

$$t_f = \frac{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}{R_c} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$X_c - X^* = 0.15 - 0.02 = 0.13$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.05 - 0.02 = 0.03$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.13}{0.03} = 4.333$$

$$\ln(4.333) = 1.466$$

$$t_f = \frac{(0.0125)(1200)(0.13)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times 1.466$$

$$t_f = \frac{1.95}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \times 1.466$$

$$t_f = 13{,}000 \times 1.466$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 19{,}058 \text{ s} \approx 5.29 \text{ hours}}$$

### Part (c): Total Drying Time

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 25{,}000 + 19{,}058$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} \approx 44{,}058 \text{ s} \approx 12.24 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation for KTU Exam:** Notice that the falling rate period took **nearly 41% of total time** even though the moisture removal was only $\Delta X = 0.10$ (compared to $\Delta X = 0.25$ in constant rate). This shows the falling rate region is **inefficient and slow** — exactly why we design driers to keep moisture above $X_c$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Reasoning

| Property | Constant Rate Region | Falling Rate Region |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture range | $X_1 > X > X_c$ | $X_c > X > X^*$ |
| Surface condition | Fully wet | Partially dry |
| Controlling resistance | External (air-side film) | Internal (diffusion in solid) |
| Drying rate | $R = R_c$ (constant) | $R$ decreases with $X$ |
| Controlling equation | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -\dfrac{R_c}{L \rho_s}$ | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = -\dfrac{R_c (X - X^*)}{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}$ |
| Time equation | $t_c = \dfrac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c}$ | $t_f = \dfrac{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}{R_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ |
| Effect of air velocity | Significant (changes $R_c$) | Insignificant |
| Effect of internal moisture transport | Insignificant | Dominant |

---

## 3.4 Moisture Conversion Derivation

Starting from definitions:

$$X_w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$$

$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_s} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $m_s$:

$$X_w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

Conversely:

$$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$$

This relation is used when problem statements give wet-basis values (e.g., "20% moisture" → $X_w = 0.20$ → $X = 0.25$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Moisture Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TOTAL MOISTURE CONTENT X] --> B[EQUILIBRIUM MOISTURE X*]
    A --> C[FREE MOISTURE X - X*]
    B --> B1[Cannot be removed by drying air]
    B --> B2[Depends on RH and Temperature]
    B --> B3[Bound Water Portion]
    C --> D[UNBOUND WATER]
    C --> E[BOUND WATER FRACTION]
    D --> D1[Vapor pressure equals pure water]
    D --> D2[Removed in constant rate period]
    E --> E1[Vapor pressure less than pure water]
    E --> E2[Removed in falling rate period]
    A --> F[CRITICAL MOISTURE Xc]
    F --> F1[Upper bound - constant rate region]
    F --> F2[Lower bound - falling rate region]
    F1 --> G1[Surface evaporation controls]
    F2 --> G2[Internal diffusion controls]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Drying Curve & Process Stages

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Stage A: Initial Adjustment] --> S2[Stage B: Constant Rate Period]
    S2 --> S3[Stage C: First Falling Rate Period]
    S3 --> S4[Stage D: Second Falling Rate Period]
    S2 --> P1[X greater than Xc]
    S2 --> P2[R equals Rc]
    S3 --> P3[Unbound water removal]
    S4 --> P4[Bound water removal]
    S3 --> F1[Surface partially dry]
    S4 --> F2[Internal diffusion dominant]
    S1 -.-> X1[Slab warms up to wet bulb T]
    S2 -.-> X2[Surface saturated with water]
    S3 -.-> X3[X is between Xc and X*]
    S4 -.-> X4[X approaches X*]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mermaid Block Diagram - Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph INPUT[Process Inputs]
        A1[Wet Solid: X1 initial moisture]
        A2[Hot Air Stream: T, RH]
        A3[Drier Geometry: L, rho_s]
    end

    subgraph OPERATION[Heat and Mass Transfer Zone]
        B1[Air Film - External Resistance]
        B2[Solid Surface - Evaporation]
        B3[Internal Pores - Diffusion]
    end

    subgraph REGIONS[Drying Regime Selector]
        C1{Is X greater than Xc?}
        C2{Is X greater than X*?}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Process Outputs]
        D1[Dried Solid: X2 final moisture]
        D2[Moisture laden exhaust air]
        D3[Drying time t_total]
    end

    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 --> C1
    C1 -->|Yes Constant Rate| D1
    C1 -->|No Falling Rate| C2
    C2 -->|Yes Unbound| D1
    C2 -->|No Bound| D1
    B2 --> D2
    B3 --> D3
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Sorption Isotherm Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    RH[Relative Humidity RH percent] --> R1[RH = 0 percent]
    RH --> R2[RH = 30 percent]
    RH --> R3[RH = 60 percent]
    RH --> R4[RH = 90 percent]
    R1 --> M1[X* nearly equal to 0 - Bone dry possible]
    R2 --> M2[X* small - Easy to dry]
    R3 --> M3[X* moderate - Cannot achieve zero moisture]
    R4 --> M4[X* large - Only minor drying possible]
    M1 --> MSG1[Drying goal easy]
    M4 --> MSG2[Drying goal difficult]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: The Drying Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Transfer from Air to Solid] --> B[Liquid Water at Surface]
    B --> C[Evaporation]
    C --> D[Water Vapor]
    D --> E[Mass Transfer of Vapor through Air Film]
    E --> F[Moisture Laden Air Removed]

    G[Liquid Water in Interior of Solid] --> H[Internal Diffusion]
    H --> B

    subgraph Constant_Rate_Phase
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph Falling_Rate_Phase
        G
        H
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Equilibrium moisture content
(b) Free moisture content
(c) Unbound moisture

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Equilibrium Moisture Content:** It is the moisture content of a solid in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air at a given temperature and relative humidity. Below this value, no drying can occur under those air conditions.
*Example:* Wood in a room at 25°C and 60% RH has $X^* \approx 0.10$ kg/kg.

**(b) Free Moisture Content:** It is the moisture in excess of the equilibrium moisture content that can be removed by drying under the prevailing air conditions.
*Example:* If the wood has actual moisture $X = 0.30$ kg/kg, free moisture = $0.30 - 0.10 = 0.20$ kg/kg.

**(c) Unbound Moisture:** It is the moisture that exerts a vapor pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature. It is held loosely in capillaries and pores.
*Example:* Free water in a wet sand pile behaves as unbound moisture.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**

**Distinguish between bound moisture and unbound moisture. What is the significance of critical moisture content in drying?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bound Moisture | Unbound Moisture |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Water held such that its vapor pressure is less than pure water at same T | Water with vapor pressure equal to pure water |
| Energy to remove | More (must overcome binding forces) | Less (same as pure water evaporation) |
| Location | Within cell walls, capillaries, adsorbed layers | On surface, in large pores, free liquid |
| Drying region | Falling rate period | Constant rate period |

**Significance of Critical Moisture Content:**
- Marks the transition from **constant rate to falling rate drying**.
- Above $X_c$: surface is fully wet, drying rate depends on external (air-side) conditions.
- Below $X_c$: surface becomes partially dry, drying rate depends on **internal diffusion**.
- Used to estimate **drying time** for a given product in a given drier.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Rule]:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided — students must answer ONE full question from the choice.*

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** A solid is dried from an initial moisture content of 0.38 kg/kg (dry basis) to a final value of 0.04 kg/kg in a tray drier. The equilibrium moisture content is 0.02 kg/kg and the critical moisture content is 0.18 kg/kg. The slab is 30 mm thick (half-thickness $L = 0.015$ m) and the bulk dry density is 900 kg/m³. The constant drying rate is $R_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg water/m²·s.

Calculate:
1. The time required for the constant rate period.
2. The time required for the falling rate period (assume linear falling rate).
3. The total drying time.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the typical drying rate curve showing different periods of drying. Discuss how the critical moisture content is affected by:
1. Air velocity
2. Thickness of the solid
3. Humidity of air

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) Solution:

**Given Data:**
$X_1 = 0.38$ kg/kg, $X_c = 0.18$ kg/kg, $X_2 = 0.04$ kg/kg, $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg
$L = 0.015$ m, $\rho_s = 900$ kg/m³, $R_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s

**Step 1 — Constant rate time:**

$$t_c = \frac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c}$$

$$t_c = \frac{(0.015)(900)(0.38 - 0.18)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = \frac{(13.5)(0.20)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{2.7}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 13{,}500 \text{ s} \approx 3.75 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 2 — Falling rate time (linear model):**

$$t_f = \frac{L \rho_s (X_c - X^*)}{R_c} \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$X_c - X^* = 0.18 - 0.02 = 0.16$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.04 - 0.02 = 0.02$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.16}{0.02} = 8.0$$

$$\ln(8.0) = 2.079$$

$$t_f = \frac{(0.015)(900)(0.16)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} \times 2.079$$

$$t_f = \frac{2.16}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} \times 2.079$$

$$t_f = 10{,}800 \times 2.079$$

$$\boxed{t_f = 22{,}453 \text{ s} \approx 6.24 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 13{,}500 + 22{,}453$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = 35{,}953 \text{ s} \approx 9.99 \text{ hours}}$$

---

### Part (b) Solution:

**The drying rate curve** is a plot of **drying rate R** (kg/m²·s) vs **moisture content X** (kg/kg dry basis). It consists of four distinct regions:

**Region A — Initial Adjustment (Transient):**
The solid heats up from initial temperature to the wet-bulb temperature. The rate rises quickly. This region is short and often ignored in calculations.

**Region B — Constant Rate Period:**
- Surface remains saturated with water.
- Drying rate is governed by external heat/mass transfer.
- The rate equals the rate of evaporation from a free water surface.
- $R = R_c = h(T_a - T_s) / \lambda_s = $ **constant**.
- Slope of R vs X curve = 0.
- Moisture drops from $X_1$ to $X_c$.

**Region C — First Falling Rate Period:**
- Surface becomes partially dry; wet patches shrink.
- Internal liquid movement to surface limits the rate.
- R decreases approximately linearly with X.
- Moisture drops from $X_c$ to some critical point.

**Region D — Second Falling Rate Period:**
- Surface is completely dry.
- Moisture moves by internal diffusion only.
- R approaches zero as $X \to X^*$.

**Effect of Operating Conditions on $X_c$:**

| Parameter | Direction of Change | Effect on $X_c$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air velocity | Increase | $X_c$ **increases** | Higher external rate → surface dries earlier |
| Thickness of solid | Increase | $X_c$ **increases** | Longer diffusion path → internal transport limits sooner |
| Air humidity | Increase | $X_c$ **decreases** | Lower driving force for external mass transfer → surface stays wet longer |

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Alternative Model Choice]**

**(a)** Define and explain the following with the help of suitable diagrams:
1. Sorption isotherm
2. Critical moisture content
3. Bound and unbound moisture

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wet solid containing 30% moisture (wet basis) is to be dried to 5% moisture (wet basis) in a tray drier. The critical moisture content is 18% (dry basis) and the equilibrium moisture content is 2% (dry basis). The constant drying rate is 1.2 kg/m²·hr. The drier holds 50 kg of dry solid with a drying surface area of 1.0 m² per kg of dry solid.

Calculate the total drying time assuming linear falling rate behavior.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) Solution:

**1. Sorption Isotherm:**
A sorption isotherm is a plot of **equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$) vs relative humidity (RH)** of air at constant temperature. It is generated experimentally by placing samples at various RH values and measuring the equilibrium moisture.

Typical sorption isotherms (BET or GAB models) show:
- $X^* = 0$ at RH = 0
- $X^*$ rises gradually then steeply as RH → 100%
- Different shapes for different materials (Type I, II, III, IV in BET classification)

It is essential for predicting how dry a product can become in a given environment and for choosing storage conditions.

**2. Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$):**
It is the moisture content at which the constant rate drying period ends and the falling rate begins. Above $X_c$, the surface remains saturated; below it, dry patches appear.

**3. Bound and Unbound Moisture:**
- **Unbound moisture:** Exerts vapor pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature. Held in capillaries and on surfaces. Easily removed.
- **Bound moisture:** Exerts vapor pressure less than pure water. Held by adsorption and chemical bonds. Difficult to remove; requires more energy.

---

### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert wet basis to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{X_{w1}}{1 - X_{w1}} = \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{X_{w2}}{1 - X_{w2}} = \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_c = 0.18 \text{ kg/kg (given)}$$

$$X^* = 0.02 \text{ kg/kg (given)}$$

**Step 2 — Identify drying regions:**
- $X_1 = 0.4286 > X_c = 0.18$ → Constant rate applies first
- $X_2 = 0.0526 < X_c$ → Falling rate applies second

**Step 3 — Unit conversion:**
$R_c = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{hr} = \dfrac{1.2}{3600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$

Drying surface area = $1.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg dry solid} \times 50 \text{ kg dry} = 50 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 4 — Equivalent $L \rho_s$ for the drier:**
Since drying is from both sides (or the geometry is integrated), we use:

$$L \rho_s = \frac{\text{dry solid mass per unit area}}{1} = \frac{50 \text{ kg dry}}{50 \text{ m}^2} = 1.0 \text{ kg dry/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Constant rate time:**

$$t_c = \frac{L \rho_s (X_1 - X_c)}{R_c} = \frac{(1.0)(0.4286 - 0.18)}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = \frac{0.2486}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 745.8 \text{ s}$$

Converting to hours:

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 0.207 \text{ hr} \approx 12.4 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 6 — Falling rate time:**

$$X_c - X^* = 0.18 - 0.02 = 0.16$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.0526 - 0.02 = 0.0326$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.16}{0.0326} = 4.908$$

$$\ln(4.908) = 1.591$$

$$t_f = \frac{(1.0)(0.16)}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} \times 1.591$$

$$t_f = 480 \times 1.591 = 763.7 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 0.212 \text{ hr} \approx 12.7 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 7 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 745.8 + 763.7 = 1509.5 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{total}} \approx 0.42 \text{ hr} \approx 25.2 \text{ min}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wet basis vs Dry basis confusion:** A KTU favorite trick! If the problem gives "30% moisture", always check whether it is wet or dry basis. Using wet basis as dry basis gives ~50% error in $t_c$ calculation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $X^*$:** Students often write $t_c = L\rho_s(X_1 - X_c)/R_c$ but then forget that the falling rate equation must use $(X - X^*)$ and NOT $X$ alone. This is the **most common** error in KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch on $R_c$:** Some problems give $R_c$ in kg/m²·hr, others in kg/m²·s. Converting to SI before plugging into the formula is **mandatory**.
>
> 4. **Using $X_1$ instead of $X_c$ in falling rate equation:** The falling rate period starts at $X_c$, NOT at $X_1$. The integration limits must be carefully observed.
>
> 5. **Wrong half-thickness:** For a slab of total thickness $2L$, the half-thickness is $L$. Students often use the total thickness, leading to **2× error in time**.
>
> 6. **Sign errors in log term:** Make sure to compute $\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$, NOT $\ln[(X_2 - X^*)/(X_c - X^*)]$. The latter gives a negative time!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Drying** is simultaneous heat + mass transfer using hot air as the medium.
- **Dry basis** $X = m_w / m_s$ is the engineering standard; **wet basis** $X_w = m_w / m_{total}$.
- **Conversion:** $X = X_w / (1 - X_w)$; $X_w = X / (1 + X)$.
- **Equilibrium moisture $X^*$:** Lower limit of drying by air of given RH; depends on T and RH; $X^* = 0$ only at RH = 0.
- **Free moisture** = $X - X^*$ (the part you can actually remove).
- **Bound water:** vapor pressure < pure water; tightly held; removed in falling rate.
- **Unbound water:** vapor pressure = pure water; loosely held; removed in constant rate.
- **Critical moisture $X_c$:** Transition point between constant rate and falling rate.
- **Constant rate region:** $R = R_c$ independent of $X$; surface fully wet; controlled by air-side.
- **Falling rate region:** $R$ decreases with $X$; controlled by internal diffusion (linear model most common).
- **Constant rate time:** $t_c = L\rho_s(X_1 - X_c)/R_c$.
- **Falling rate time (linear):** $t_f = [L\rho_s(X_c - X^*)/R_c] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
- **Total time:** $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$.
- **Effect of parameters on $X_c$:** Air velocity ↑ → $X_c$ ↑; thickness ↑ → $X_c$ ↑; humidity ↑ → $X_c$ ↓.
- **Sorption isotherm:** Plot of $X^*$ vs RH at constant T — predicts achievable dryness.
- **Engineering applications:** Food drying, pharmaceutical drying, ceramics, polymer processing.
- **Key insight:** Air-drying can NEVER reach $X = 0$; the absolute lower bound is $X^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The above content is fully aligned with the PBSFT504 Module 4 syllabus. For additional numerical practice, refer to standard textbook problems on drying time calculations (Treybal, McCabe-Smith-Harriott, Geankoplis) and ensure your final answers carry correct **units and significant figures** as per KTU valuation norms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate of Drying Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rate of Drying Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Drying** is defined as the unit operation in which moisture is removed from a wet solid (or a solution) by thermal means, through evaporation of the liquid into an unsaturated gas stream (commonly air). It involves simultaneous **heat transfer** (from the hot gas to the solid surface and into the interior) and **mass transfer** (of moisture from the interior of the solid to its surface, and then into the bulk gas phase).

The **Rate of Drying** is defined as the mass of moisture removed per unit time per unit area of the drying surface. Mathematically, for a batch dryer, it is expressed as:

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}$$

where:
- **N** = Rate of drying (kg moisture / m²·h)
- **Ls** = Mass of bone-dry solid (kg)
- **A** = Exposed drying surface area (m²)
- **X** = Moisture content on dry basis (kg water / kg dry solid)
- **t** = Time (h)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** "The rate of drying is the slope of the drying curve expressed as the mass of water removed per unit area per unit time." Examiners reward this exact phrasing with full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just washed a cotton cloth and hung it out on a sunny clothesline on a windy day.

1. **Initial stage** – The cloth is dripping wet. Water is freely available on the surface, and the cloth behaves almost like an open water surface. Evaporation happens at a **constant, maximum rate** — limited only by how fast wind sweeps water vapour away. This is the **Constant Rate Period**.
2. **Middle stage** – The surface starts drying out. Water now has to travel from the inside of the cloth to the surface through the fabric fibres. Evaporation rate begins to drop. This is the **First Falling Rate Period** — the cloth feels "damp" but not soaking.
3. **Final stage** – Only tightly held water (inside fibre capillaries) remains. Evaporation becomes very slow, and even if you leave the cloth for a whole day, it never quite reaches zero moisture — it settles at an **equilibrium value** with the surrounding air. This is the **Second Falling Rate Period**.

This is the essence of a **Rate of Drying Curve** — a fingerprint of how any wet material loses moisture over time under given conditions.

## 1.3 Key Moisture Definitions

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Moisture Content (Dry basis) | $X_t$ | Total water in solid per kg of bone-dry solid |
| Equilibrium Moisture Content | $X^*$ | Moisture that cannot be removed at given T & humidity |
| Free Moisture Content | $X_f = X - X^*$ | Moisture removable by drying under given conditions |
| Bound Moisture | $X_b$ | Water held tightly inside cell walls / capillaries |
| Unbound Moisture | $X_u$ | Water that behaves like a free liquid surface |
| Critical Moisture Content | $X_c$ | Moisture level where the constant rate period ends |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bound vs Unbound:** If $X > X_b$, water on the surface acts like a free liquid (vapour pressure = $P_w^o$). If $X < X_b$, vapour pressure drops below $P_w^o$ (hygroscopic behaviour), and drying becomes very difficult. **Equilibrium moisture $X^*$ depends on the material, temperature, and relative humidity of the air.**

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Latent heat of vaporization at 100 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2257$ **kJ/kg**
- **Dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, dew point** — all used in psychrometric analysis alongside drying.
- **Standard drying surface flux unit:** **kg H₂O / m²·h**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drying Rate Curve — Structural Breakdown

The Rate of Drying Curve is a plot of the **drying rate N** (Y-axis) versus the **moisture content X** (X-axis) — *not* versus time. This is the standard academic convention. Time-based plots are called "drying curves."

The drying rate curve typically consists of **three distinct regions**:

### Step-by-Step Logic of Drying Operation

1. **Adjustment (Warm-up) Period** *(brief, often neglected)*
   - Solid heats from inlet temperature to wet-bulb temperature.
   - Rate rises sharply from zero to a peak value.

2. **Constant Rate Period (Period A–B)**
   - Surface remains saturated with free water.
   - Drying rate equals the rate of evaporation from a free water surface.
   - Controlled by **external mass and heat transfer** (convective film).
   - Drying rate is independent of solid type.
   - **Heat transfer equation:** $q = h_s (T_g - T_w)$
   - **Mass transfer equation:** $N = k_y (H_w - H_g)$
   - These two are linked by psychrometry: $N = \dfrac{q}{\lambda_s}$

3. **First Falling Rate Period (Period B–C)**
   - Surface is no longer saturated. Dry patches appear.
   - Effective wet area shrinks. Drying rate decreases with X.
   - Often **linear** with X.

4. **Second Falling Rate Period (Period C–D)**
   - Entire surface is dry. Moisture moves from interior by diffusion.
   - Drying rate is controlled by **internal mass transfer** (liquid/vapour diffusion through solid).
   - Approximated as a plane-sheet or pore-diffusion problem.

5. **Equilibrium (Period D–E)**
   - Moisture content reaches $X^*$. Driving force → 0. Drying stops.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most-tested formula set** from Module 4. Memorize symbols and units — KTU examiners love marking mistakes in unit conversions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{w}{W_d} = \dfrac{W-w_d}{w_d}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg/kg dry solid |
| 2 | $X_w = \dfrac{w}{W}$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | kg/kg wet solid |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$ | Dry basis ↔ Wet basis | — |
| 4 | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Rate of drying (batch) | kg/m²·h |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{L_s}{A}\int_{X_1}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{N}$ | Total drying time | h |
| 6 | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s}{A N_c}(X_1 - X_c)$ | Constant-rate drying time | h |
| 7 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{A}\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{N}$ | Falling-rate drying time | h |
| 8 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_s(T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_w}$ | Constant drying rate (heat basis) | kg/m²·h |
| 9 | $N_c = k_y(H_w - H_g)$ | Constant drying rate (mass basis) | kg/m²·h |
| 10 | $X^* = f(T, RH)$ | Equilibrium moisture (read from sorption isotherm) | kg/kg |
| 11 | $X_f = X - X^*$ | Free moisture content | kg/kg |
| 12 | $X_b$ from sorption isotherm | Bound moisture (intersect with free-water line) | kg/kg |
| 13 | $\dfrac{t}{t_f} = \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$ | **Linear falling rate** approximation | — |

> [!WARNING]
> For equation 13 (linear falling rate), this is the **most frequently used formula for "first falling rate period"** problems in KTU exams. The straight-line relation is $N = aX + b$, where constants are evaluated from $(X_c, N_c)$ and $(X^*, 0)$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — Drying of granules, tablets, APIs under controlled $X^*$.
- **Food processing** — Spray drying of milk, dehydration of vegetables.
- **Cement industry** — Drying of limestone, clay, slag before kiln feed.
- **Paper & textile** — Roller dryers, conveyor dryers sized using these curves.
- **Ceramics** — Critical to avoid the constant-rate period for crack-free drying.

## 2.4 Important Distinctions (Often Asked in KTU)

| Distinction | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Controlling mechanism** | External (gas film) | Internal (solid structure) |
| **Depends on solid type?** | No | Yes |
| **Drying rate value** | Maximum & constant | Decreasing with X |
| **Surface condition** | Fully wet | Partially or fully dry |
| **Heat supplied to** | Evaporating water | Heats solid + evaporation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rate of Drying Equation (Batch Dryer)

We begin with a mass balance on the moisture in the wet solid during a small time interval $dt$.

**Step 1:** Let the mass of bone-dry solid in the dryer be $L_s$ (kg). It is invariant because the dry solid is not removed; only moisture leaves.

**Step 2:** The total moisture mass in the solid at any instant is $L_s \cdot X$.

**Step 3:** During an infinitesimal time $dt$, moisture evaporates. The rate of moisture removal expressed per unit drying area is $N$ (kg/m²·h). The total moisture removed in time $dt$ from area $A$ is $N \cdot A \cdot dt$.

**Step 4:** The moisture balance therefore reads:

$$L_s \cdot X = (L_s \cdot X + dX) + N \cdot A \cdot dt$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying (mass of dry solid in the solid is constant, so $L_s$ does not change, but $X$ decreases):

$$-L_s \, dX = N \, A \, dt$$

**Step 6:** Solving for N (rearranging so that time derivative is on the right):

$$N = -\frac{L_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}$$

This is the **fundamental drying rate equation** that KTU examiners derive as a compulsory sub-question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time-of-Drying Formula (Constant Rate Period)

In the **constant rate period**, $N = N_c = \text{constant}$ (i.e., the rate does not depend on $X$).

**Step 1:** Start from the differential form:

$$dt = -\frac{L_s}{A} \frac{dX}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N = N_c$:

$$dt = -\frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c} dX$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $X_1$ (initial moisture) to $X_c$ (critical moisture) at $t = 0$ to $t = t_c$:

$$t_c = -\frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c}\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral:

$$t_c = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c}(X_1 - X_c)$$

This is the standard expression for the duration of the constant rate period.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Time-of-Drying Formula (Falling Rate Period)

In the **falling rate period**, $N$ depends on $X$. Time is obtained by integration:

$$t_f = \int_{0}^{t_f} dt = \frac{L_s}{A}\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{N}$$

This integral is **most commonly evaluated graphically** in KTU problems:

**Method 1 — Linear Falling Rate Approximation:** If the falling rate portion is approximated as a straight line from $(X_c, N_c)$ to $(X^*, 0)$:

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Substitute and integrate:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c}\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{(X_c - X^*) \, dX}{X - X^*}$$

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c}\left[\ln\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\right]$$

**Method 2 — Graphical Integration:** Plot $1/N$ vs $X$ from $X_c$ to $X_2$, find area under the curve. Multiply by $L_s/A$ to get time.

**Method 3 — Linear approximation to $X$ (used in KTU):**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c}\left[(X_c - X^*)\ln\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\right]$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A batch dryer contains 200 kg of bone-dry solid carrying an initial moisture of 0.30 kg/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is 0.10 kg/kg dry solid, and the equilibrium moisture is 0.005 kg/kg dry solid. The drying surface area is 1.0 m². The constant drying rate $N_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·h, and the falling rate is linear. Find the time required to dry the solid to a final moisture of 0.02 kg/kg dry solid.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_s &= 200 \text{ kg} \\
A &= 1.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
X_1 &= 0.30, \quad X_c = 0.10, \quad X_2 = 0.02, \quad X^* = 0.005 \\
N_c &= 1.5 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Check which periods are active:**
- Since $X_1 = 0.30 > X_c = 0.10$, the **constant rate period** exists.
- Since $X_2 = 0.02 < X_c = 0.10$, the **falling rate period** also exists.

**Step 3 — Constant rate period time:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_c &= \frac{L_s}{A \cdot N_c}(X_1 - X_c) \\
t_c &= \frac{200}{1.0 \times 1.5}(0.30 - 0.10) \\
t_c &= \frac{200}{1.5} \times 0.20 \\
t_c &= 26.67 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Falling rate period time (linear):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c}\ln\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) \\
t_f &= \frac{200 \times (0.10 - 0.005)}{1.0 \times 1.5}\ln\left(\frac{0.10 - 0.005}{0.02 - 0.005}\right) \\
t_f &= \frac{200 \times 0.095}{1.5}\ln\left(\frac{0.095}{0.015}\right) \\
t_f &= 12.667 \times \ln(6.333) \\
t_f &= 12.667 \times 1.8458 \\
t_f &= 23.38 \text{ h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 26.67 + 23.38 = 50.05 \text{ h}$$

**Answer:** Total drying time ≈ **50.05 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award full marks only if you show **all five steps with units**. Skipping unit analysis is a common reason for losing 1–2 marks.

## 3.5 Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis — Derivation

Given a wet sample of total mass $W$ containing mass of dry solid $W_d$ and water $w$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= W_d + w \\
X_w &= \frac{w}{W} = \frac{w}{W_d + w} \\
X &= \frac{w}{W_d}
\end{aligned}$$

Divide numerator and denominator of $X_w$ by $W_d$:

$$X_w = \frac{w/W_d}{W_d/W_d + w/W_d} = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

The inverse:

$$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w}$$

This is equation 3 in the formula table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Drying Rate Curve Architecture

The following diagram maps out the **functional architecture** of a drying rate curve, identifying its regions, key parameters, and controlling transport mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Moisture Content X, dry basis]) --> A1{Region on Curve}
    A1 -->|X greater than Xc| P1[CONSTANT RATE PERIOD]
    A1 -->|X between Xc and Xstar| P2[FALLING RATE PERIOD]
    A1 -->|X equals Xstar| P3[EQUILIBRIUM POINT]
    P1 --> P1A[Surface fully saturated with free water]
    P1A --> P1B[Controlled by External Gas Film]
    P1B --> P1C[Rate equals free water evaporation]
    P1C --> P1D[Slope of N vs X is zero]
    P2 --> P2A[Surface partially dry, internal diffusion]
    P2A --> P2B[First falling rate: linear in X]
    P2B --> P2C[Second falling rate: pore diffusion]
    P2C --> P2D[Slope of N vs X is negative]
    P3 --> P3A[Drying stops, N becomes zero]
    P3A --> P3B[Residual moisture Xstar held by hygroscopic forces]
    P1D --> Q1[Constant Rate Time: tc equals Ls X1 minus Xc over A Nc]
    P2D --> Q2[Falling Rate Time: tf equals Ls over A times integral 1 over N dX from Xc to X2]
    Q1 --> Z([Total Drying Time: t equals tc plus tf])
    Q2 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Real-Time Drying Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wet Solid In] --> B[Heated Air Stream]
    B --> C[Drying Chamber]
    C --> D[Heat Transfer from Air to Solid]
    D --> E[Moisture Evaporation at Surface]
    E --> F[Mass Transfer of Vapour to Air Stream]
    F --> G[Exhaust Humid Air Out]
    C --> H[Monitoring X, T, RH]
    H --> I{Compare X to Xstar}
    I -->|X greater than Xstar| J[Drying Continues]
    I -->|X equals Xstar| K[Drying Complete: Bone-Dry Solid Out]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic of Drying Rate vs Moisture Content

```
N (kg/m²·h)
^
|         _____ (Constant Rate Region)
|        |     |
|        |     |
|        |     |\
|   Nc  -+     | \  (First Falling Rate)
|        |     |  \
|        |     |   \   (Second Falling Rate)
|        |     |    \
|        |_____|     \____
|                            \_____
|                                  \___________
+--------------------------------|----------|----------|----> X (kg/kg)
       X*                          Xc         X1
       (equilib)              (critical)    (initial)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this chart for KTU problems:** Mark the critical point $(X_c, N_c)$. The area under the curve (in a $1/N$ vs $X$ plot) gives the falling-rate time.

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Periods and Their Drivers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Period A-B: Constant Rate] --> P1D[Driver: Heat Transfer from Air to Solid]
    P1D --> P1E[Heat supplied equals Latent heat of vaporization]
    P2[Period B-C: First Falling] --> P2D[Driver: Unsaturated Surface Evaporation]
    P2D --> P2E[Effective area decreases linearly]
    P3[Period C-D: Second Falling] --> P3D[Driver: Internal Moisture Diffusion]
    P3D --> P3E[Liquid phase diffusion, Fickian mechanism]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define the following terms with their SI units: (a) Moisture content on dry basis (b) Equilibrium moisture content (c) Free moisture content.`**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Moisture content on dry basis (X):** It is defined as the mass of moisture (water) present in the wet solid per unit mass of bone-dry solid.

$$X = \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Mass of dry solid}} = \frac{w}{W_d}$$

**Unit:** kg water / kg dry solid (dimensionless ratio expressed as kg/kg).

(b) **Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$):** It is the moisture content of the solid when it is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air at given temperature and humidity. No further drying is possible below $X^*$ under those conditions. **Unit:** kg/kg dry solid.

(c) **Free moisture content ($X_f$):** It is the difference between the total moisture content and the equilibrium moisture content. It represents the moisture that **can actually be removed** by drying at given conditions.

$$X_f = X - X^*$$

**Unit:** kg/kg dry solid.

> `[Marks Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1]`

---

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Distinguish between the constant rate period and the falling rate period of drying.`**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Drying rate** | Constant at maximum value ($N_c$) | Decreases with moisture content |
| **Surface state** | Fully saturated with free water | Partially or completely dry |
| **Rate-controlling step** | External gas-film mass/heat transfer | Internal moisture movement |
| **Material dependence** | Independent of solid type | Strongly depends on solid structure |
| **Heat consumption** | All heat goes to evaporate water | Part of heat raises solid temperature |
| **Dry-basis relation** | $N$ vs $X$ is horizontal | $N$ vs $X$ has negative slope |

> `[Marks Distribution: Tabular comparison with any 4 points = 3 marks]`

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024] Q. (a) Derive the rate of drying equation for a batch dryer. (7 marks) (b) A wet solid containing 30% moisture (wet basis) is to be dried to 5% moisture (wet basis) in a batch tray dryer. The initial mass of the wet solid is 100 kg. The drying surface area is 1.2 m². The constant drying rate is 1.8 kg/m²·h and the critical moisture content is 0.18 kg/kg dry basis. The equilibrium moisture is 0.02 kg/kg dry basis. The falling rate period is linear. Calculate the total drying time.`** `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Derivation

Mass of moisture in wet solid at any instant = $L_s \cdot X$.

In a small time $dt$, the moisture removed = $N \cdot A \cdot dt$.

Therefore:

$$L_s \cdot X - L_s \cdot (X + dX) = N \cdot A \cdot dt$$

$$-L_s \, dX = N \cdot A \, dt$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{N = -\frac{L_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}}$$

This is the rate of drying equation for a batch dryer.

> `[Marks: 1 for figure, 2 for balance, 2 for integration logic, 2 for final expression]`

### Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Convert wet basis to dry basis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \frac{X_{w1}}{1 - X_{w1}} = \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg} \\
X_2 &= \frac{0.05}{1 - 0.05} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of bone-dry solid:**

$$L_s = 100 \times (1 - 0.30) = 70 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Verify which periods apply:**
- $X_1 = 0.4286 > X_c = 0.18$ → Constant rate period exists.
- $X_2 = 0.0526 < X_c = 0.18$ → Falling rate period exists.

**Step 4 — Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{70 (0.4286 - 0.18)}{1.2 \times 1.8} = \frac{70 \times 0.2486}{2.16}$$

$$t_c = \frac{17.40}{2.16} = 8.06 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Falling rate period time (linear, ends at $X_2$):**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c}\ln\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{70 (0.18 - 0.02)}{1.2 \times 1.8}\ln\left(\frac{0.18 - 0.02}{0.0526 - 0.02}\right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{70 \times 0.16}{2.16}\ln\left(\frac{0.16}{0.0326}\right) = \frac{11.20}{2.16} \times \ln(4.908)$$

$$t_f = 5.185 \times 1.5906 = 8.247 \text{ h}$$

**Step 6 — Total time:**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 8.06 + 8.25 = 16.31 \text{ h}$$

**Answer:** Total drying time ≈ **16.31 hours**.

> `[Marks: 1 each for conversion, dry mass identification, time expressions, and final answer with units]`

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023] Q. (a) Explain the construction and significance of the rate of drying curve. Discuss the different periods in detail with a neat sketch. (7 marks) (b) 250 kg of a wet solid is dried from 25% to 6% moisture (wet basis) under constant drying conditions. The bone-dry solid density is 1500 kg/m³, and the drying takes place on a tray of 2 m². The critical moisture is 0.10 kg/kg dry basis, equilibrium moisture is 0.005 kg/kg dry basis, and the constant drying rate is 1.2 kg/m²·h. Falling rate is linear. Determine: (i) The constant rate period time, (ii) The time required to reach the final moisture content.`** `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Construction of Drying Rate Curve

The **rate of drying curve** is a plot of drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·h) on the Y-axis versus the **moisture content X** (kg/kg dry basis) on the X-axis. It is constructed experimentally by recording mass of a wet solid at regular intervals in a batch dryer and computing the rate of change of moisture content with respect to time, then dividing by the exposed surface area.

**Significance:**
- Identifies the **critical moisture content** at which the constant rate period ends.
- Helps determine the **drying time** for batch operations.
- Distinguishes between **external (gas film)** and **internal (diffusion)** controlling regimes.
- Used to design industrial dryers (size, residence time, heat duty).

**Different periods:**

1. **Constant Rate Period:** Surface is saturated; drying rate is constant and maximum.
2. **First Falling Rate Period:** Surface partially dry, drying rate decreases linearly.
3. **Second Falling Rate Period:** Internal moisture diffusion controls; rate decreases sharply.
4. **Equilibrium Point:** $X = X^*$, drying rate is zero.

(Refer to the ASCII curve shown in Section 4.3 for the sketch.)

> `[Marks: 2 for construction, 2 for significance, 3 for periods with sketch]`

### Part (b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Convert wet to dry basis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \frac{0.25}{1 - 0.25} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/kg} \\
X_2 &= \frac{0.06}{1 - 0.06} = 0.0638 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid:**

$$L_s = 250 \times (1 - 0.25) = 187.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Period check:** $X_1 = 0.3333 > X_c = 0.10$ → constant rate present. $X_2 = 0.0638 < X_c = 0.10$ → falling rate present.

**(i) Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{187.5 \times (0.3333 - 0.10)}{2 \times 1.2} = \frac{187.5 \times 0.2333}{2.4} = \frac{43.75}{2.4} = 18.23 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) Total time:**

$$t_f = \frac{187.5 \times (0.10 - 0.005)}{2 \times 1.2}\ln\left(\frac{0.10 - 0.005}{0.0638 - 0.005}\right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{187.5 \times 0.095}{2.4}\ln\left(\frac{0.095}{0.0588}\right) = 7.422 \times \ln(1.6156)$$

$$t_f = 7.422 \times 0.4797 = 3.56 \text{ h}$$

**Total time:**

$$t_{total} = 18.23 + 3.56 = 21.79 \text{ h}$$

> `[Marks: 2 for basis conversion, 1 for dry mass, 2 for tc, 2 for tf]`

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Marks Are Lost Most Often**
> 1. **Forgetting basis conversion:** Mixing up wet-basis and dry-basis moisture (e.g., using 0.30 directly instead of 0.4286). KTU board examiners penalize this **2-mark error** frequently.
> 2. **Ignoring units of N:** If $N$ is given in kg/h, you must convert to kg/m²·h by dividing by $A$.
> 3. **Sign error in dX/dt:** $dX/dt$ is **negative** (X decreases with time). Always carry the negative sign or note it explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting to add the two periods:** Total time = $t_c + t_f$. Many students compute only one.
> 5. **Using $X_1$ instead of $X_c$ as the upper limit of the falling rate period integral.** Upper limit of integration is $X_c$, NOT $X_1$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Drying rate equation** is $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$, with $N$ in kg/m²·h.
- **Dry basis** is the standard in drying calculations: $X = w/W_d$.
- **Wet to dry basis conversion:** $X = X_w / (1 - X_w)$.
- **Three periods of drying:** Constant rate → First falling rate → Second falling rate.
- **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the boundary between constant and falling rate periods.
- **Equilibrium moisture $X^*$** depends on $T$ and $RH$ of the air — read from a sorption isotherm.
- **Constant rate period** is **NOT** a function of the solid type — depends only on gas conditions.
- **Falling rate period** depends on the internal structure of the solid.
- **Time of drying** is computed separately for each period and summed.
- **Constant rate time formula:** $t_c = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A N_c}$.
- **Falling rate time formula (linear):** $t_f = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A N_c}\ln\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$.
- **Bound vs Unbound moisture:** Determined from the **sorption isotherm** of the material.
- **Graphical method** for falling rate time: Plot $1/N$ vs $X$, find area under the curve from $X_c$ to $X_2$, multiply by $L_s/A$.
- **KTU-favourite numerical pattern:** Initial and final moistures are given in **wet basis** — convert them first.
- **Heat and mass transfer link in constant rate period:** $N_c = h_s(T_g - T_w)/\lambda_w = k_y(H_w - H_g)$.
- **Wet-bulb temperature** is the surface temperature during the constant rate period.
- **Hygroscopic materials** (food, paper, textiles) have a well-defined $X_b$, **non-hygroscopic** (sand, mineral crystals) have $X_b = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying Equipments - Tray Dryers, Tower Dryers, Rotary Dryers, Fluid-Bed Dryers, Spray Dryers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT504 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying Equipments - Tray Dryers, Tower Dryers, Rotary Dryers, Fluid-Bed Dryers, Spray Dryers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: DRYING EQUIPMENTS – CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying Equipment (Industrial Definition):** A **drying equipment** is a unit operation device that accomplishes the removal of moisture (or other volatile liquids) from a solid, semi-solid, or liquid feedstock by **thermal evaporation**, typically employing heated air (or another gas) as the heat-transfer and moisture-carrier medium. The classification of dryers depends on the **mode of heat transfer** (conduction, convection, radiation, or dielectric heating) and the **physical handling** of the feed (batch vs. continuous, static vs. agitated).

In the **KTU 2024 scheme** for *PBSFT504 – Heat and Mass Transfer Operations*, the syllabus categorizes industrial dryers into two broad families:

| Classification Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch Dryers (e.g., Tray Dryer) & Continuous Dryers (e.g., Rotary, Fluid-Bed, Spray) |
| **Heat Transfer Mode** | Direct (Hot-Gas Contact) & Indirect (Conduction through wall) |
| **Physical State of Feed** | Solid-Particle Dryers, Slab/Paste Dryers, Liquid-Solution Dryers |

## 1.2 The Five Primary Industrial Dryers (KTU Module-4 Focus)

The five dryers mandated in the KTU Module-4 syllabus are listed below, along with their principal classification:

1. **Tray Dryer (Batch, Convective, Static-Bed)** – Used for powders, granules, pastes, and heat-sensitive materials in batch operations.
2. **Tower Dryer (Continuous, Gravity-Flow, Convective)** – Used for coarse, free-flowing granular solids such as grains, plastics, and ores.
3. **Rotary Dryer (Continuous, Tumbling, Convective)** – Used for free-flowing, granular, and crystalline solids in large-tonnage applications.
4. **Fluid-Bed Dryer (Continuous, Suspended-Bed, Convective)** – Used for crystalline, granular, and powdered materials requiring rapid drying.
5. **Spray Dryer (Continuous, Atomized-Droplet, Convective)** – Used for liquid feeds (solutions, suspensions, slurries) producing free-flowing powders.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Five Ways to Dry a Wet Towel" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you have **five wet towels** and **five different ways** to dry them, mirroring exactly the five industrial dryers:
>
> - **Tray Dryer ≈ Laying a towel flat on a heated stone slab in sunlight.** The towel does NOT move; only the hot air passes over it. Heat transfer is gentle and uniform. → **Batch, controlled, static**.
>
> - **Tower Dryer ≈ Dropping the towel from a great height through a column of warm rising air.** Gravity pulls it down, hot air moves up, and the towel dries as it falls. → **Continuous, gravity-driven, vertical**.
>
> - **Rotary Dryer ≈ Putting the towel inside a slowly rotating horizontal drum with warm air blown through.** The towel keeps tumbling, exposing fresh wet surfaces continuously. → **Continuous, mechanical agitation**.
>
> - **Fluid-Bed Dryer ≈ Whizzing the towel up in a wind tunnel so it floats (fluidizes) in mid-air.** Every fiber is bathed in hot air simultaneously, drying almost instantly. → **Continuous, particles suspended in gas**.
>
> - **Spray Dryer ≈ Spraying a fine mist of towel-water into a huge hot chamber.** Each tiny droplet dries into a small dry particle before hitting the floor. → **Continuous, liquid-feed to powder**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities in Drying

The following **standard metrics** appear repeatedly across all dryer designs:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Drying Metrics (SI Units)**
> - **Moisture Content (X, kg water / kg dry solid)** – quantifies wetness of the material.
> - **Free Moisture Content (X*)** – moisture removable at a given drying condition.
> - **Equilibrium Moisture Content (X\*)** – the minimum moisture attainable at given T & RH.
> - **Drying Rate (N, kg water / m² · s)** – the speed at which moisture leaves the solid.
> - **Specific Evaporation Capacity (kg water / m³ dryer volume · h)** – a key dryer-sizing index.
> - **Thermal Efficiency (η, dimensionless)** – ratio of useful heat for evaporation to total heat input.
> - **Air-Fuel Ratio (kg air / kg fuel)** – governs combustion efficiency in direct-fired heaters.

These parameters are the **common currency** used in tray, tower, rotary, fluid-bed, and spray dryer calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 4: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

This section dissects each of the five dryers under the standard KTU analytical framework: **Principle → Construction → Operating Procedure → Performance Indices → Advantages/Limitations**.

---

## 2.1 TRAY DRYER (Shelf / Compartment Dryer)

### 2.1.1 Principle of Operation
Hot air (typically **60–180 °C**) is circulated **across stationary wet trays** containing the material. Heat reaches the solid by **convection** from the air and **conduction** from the tray bottom (often steam-heated shelves). Moisture evaporates and is carried away by the exiting air. The bed is **static**—no mixing occurs inside the tray.

### 2.1.2 Construction
- An **insulated rectangular chamber** (the "tray dryer cabin").
- A bank of **stacked trays** on wheeled trolleys for easy loading/unloading.
- A **fan** (usually axial) with **re-circulation ducts** and adjustable dampers.
- **Steam-heated finned coils** (air heater) at the inlet and sometimes as internal shelves.
- **Baffles** to distribute air uniformly across each tray.

### 2.1.3 Operating Procedure (Stepwise)
1. **Load** wet material onto shallow perforated/solid trays in uniform thin layers (typical bed depth = **10–100 mm**).
2. **Insert** trolley into the dryer chamber.
3. **Start the fan** and the steam/air heater; allow air to reach design temperature.
4. **Adjust** inlet/outlet dampers to control air velocity (typical = **0.5–5 m/s**).
5. **Dry** for a pre-set time (typically **4–48 h**), optionally reversing airflow direction midway.
6. **Cool down** with fresh air to prevent heat damage during unloading.
7. **Unload** the dried product.

### 2.1.4 Key Design / Performance Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tray Dryer Governing Equations:**
>
> **Heat Duty (Q):** The total heat required combines evaporation load + sensible heating of solid + sensible heating of evaporation water.
>
> **Drying Time (constant-rate period, t\_c):** For a thin-layer bed, drying is governed by surface evaporation, hence time depends on the bed mass and the drying rate.

### 2.1.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | Simple construction; low capital cost; ideal for heat-sensitive materials (foods, APIs, dyes); batch flexibility; easy cleaning |
| **Limitations** | Labor-intensive (batch); non-uniform drying; large floor space; high energy consumption; long drying cycles; manual handling of trays |

### 2.1.6 Real-World Utility
- Drying of **pharmaceutical granules, pigments, catalyst powders, ceramic tiles, leather sheets, and food products** such as fruit pulps and pasta.

---

## 2.2 TOWER DRYER (Gravity-Flow Vertical Dryer)

### 2.2.1 Principle of Operation
Wet granular material enters the **top** of a tall vertical tower and descends under **gravity**. **Hot gas** flows either **co-currently** (downward) or **counter-currently** (upward). Continuous contact between the falling solids and the rising/falling gas removes moisture. The solids form a **moving packed bed** with plug-flow characteristics.

### 2.2.2 Construction
- A **tall vertical cylindrical or rectangular shell** (height often **6–25 m**).
- **Hopper feed** at the top with a **distributor/spreader**.
- **Internal baffles or shelves** to enhance gas–solid contact (in some designs).
- **Gas inlet/outlet** at top and/or bottom (depending on flow configuration).
- **Discharge mechanism** at the bottom (rotary valve, screw feeder, or star feeder).
- **Insulation** to minimize heat loss.

### 2.2.3 Operating Procedure
1. **Pre-heat** the tower with circulating hot gas to design temperature.
2. **Feed** wet solids at a controlled rate via the top distributor.
3. **Inject** hot air/gas counter-currently (most common) or co-currently.
4. **Maintain** uniform gas velocity to avoid channeling and ensure plug flow.
5. **Discharge** dried product at the bottom through a rotary airlock.
6. **Recover** fines from the exhaust using cyclones/baghouses.

### 2.2.4 Key Performance Indices
- **Residence Time (τ, s):** Governed by tower height and particle terminal velocity.
- **Gas Velocity (u\_g, m/s):** Must remain **below** the entrainment velocity of the largest particle.
- **Specific Heat Consumption:** **3800–5000 kJ / kg water evaporated** for counter-current operation.

### 2.2.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | Continuous operation; small floor area (vertical layout); handles coarse solids; relatively low gas pressure drop compared to fluid-bed |
| **Limitations** | Limited to free-flowing granular materials; risk of channeling; tall structure requires heavy civil engineering; sensitive to feed-moisture uniformity |

### 2.2.6 Real-World Utility
- **Cereal grain drying** (wheat, corn, rice), **polymer pellet drying**, **mineral ore drying**, **wood chip drying**, and **coal drying**.

---

## 2.3 ROTARY DRYER (Rotating Cylinder Dryer)

### 2.3.1 Principle of Operation
Wet feed enters one end of a **slightly inclined (1–6°), rotating cylindrical shell** fitted with internal **flights (lifter blades)**. As the shell rotates (typical speed = **4–8 rpm**), flights lift the solids and cascade them through a stream of hot gas flowing axially (co-current) or counter-currently. The **tumbling action** continuously exposes fresh surface area to the hot gas, achieving efficient convective drying.

### 2.3.2 Construction
- **Rotating drum** mounted on **riding rings and trunnion rollers**.
- A **drive system** (gearbox + motor) rotating the drum.
- **Internal flights**: lifting blades arranged in a helix pattern. Common types: **straight radial, inclined, V-shaped, and welded lifter**.
- **Gases-solids seals** at both ends to prevent leakage.
- **Burner** (direct-fired) or **steam-coil air heater** (indirect) at the gas inlet.
- **Dust-collection** cyclone and baghouse at the gas outlet.

### 2.3.3 Operating Procedure
1. **Start the burner/heater** and stabilize gas temperature at the inlet (typically **200–600 °C** for co-current).
2. **Rotate** the drum at the design speed.
3. **Feed** wet material through the inlet chute.
4. **Establish** steady-state gas flow and material flow rates.
5. **Monitor** outlet moisture and gas temperature continuously.
6. **Discharge** dried product via the outlet end into a conveyor.
7. **Collect** fines from the cyclone and recycle or discard.

### 2.3.4 Key Design Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotary Dryer Critical Formula (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> - **Residence Time (τ, s):** depends on drum slope, rotation speed, and flight design.
> - **Drum Length–Diameter Ratio:** typically **L/D = 4 to 10**.
> - **Holdup (% of drum volume):** typically **5–15 %**.

### 2.3.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | High throughput (up to **50 t/h**); handles abrasive and lumpy materials; continuous and reliable; flexible residence-time control |
| **Limitations** | High capital cost; large footprint; high dust generation; not ideal for heat-sensitive materials (high gas temperatures); maintenance of flights and seals |

### 2.3.6 Real-World Utility
- **Cement clinker coolers, mineral ore drying, fertilizer drying, sand drying, wood chips, animal feed, and chemical crystals**.

---

## 2.4 FLUID-BED DRYER (Fluidized-Bed Dryer)

### 2.4.1 Principle of Operation
Hot gas is passed **upward through a perforated distributor plate** supporting a bed of wet solid particles. When the gas velocity exceeds the **minimum fluidization velocity (u\_mf)**, the bed behaves like a boiling liquid—particles become suspended and intensely mixed. The enormous **interfacial area** between gas and particles gives very high heat- and mass-transfer coefficients, making fluid-bed drying extremely fast.

### 2.4.2 Construction
- A **rectangular or cylindrical drying chamber** with an **expanded upper section** to reduce gas velocity and allow entrained fines to fall back.
- A **perforated distributor plate** (or nozzle-type) at the bottom to ensure uniform gas distribution.
- A **plenum chamber** below the distributor for hot-gas inlet.
- **Bag filters or internal cyclone separators** at the top to capture fines.
- **Feed screw or chute** at one side; **discharge weir** at the other (continuous), or top-mounted for batch.
- **Vibration mechanism** (in vibrated fluid beds) to assist fluidization of cohesive powders.

### 2.4.3 Operating Procedure
1. **Pre-heat** the bed with hot gas until design temperature is reached.
2. **Feed** wet material through the inlet screw/chute.
3. **Increase** gas velocity gradually until the bed fluidizes (visually a "boiling" appearance).
4. **Maintain** gas velocity **between u\_mf and u\_t (terminal velocity)** to prevent entrainment.
5. **Control** product moisture by adjusting residence time (via weir height or feed rate).
6. **Discharge** dried material through the overflow weir.
7. **Collect** fines in cyclones/baghouses.

### 2.4.4 Key Design Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid-Bed Critical Formula:**
> - **Minimum Fluidization Velocity (u\_mf, m/s):** derived from the Ergun equation at the point of incipient fluidization.
> - **Pressure Drop Across Bed (ΔP, Pa):** equals the weight of the bed per unit area at minimum fluidization.

### 2.4.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | Extremely high heat/mass-transfer rates → very short drying times (seconds to minutes); excellent temperature uniformity; handles wide range of particle sizes; ideal for crystalline products |
| **Limitations** | Limited to particles in size range **~50 µm to 5 mm**; high attrition (dust generation); not suitable for pastes or cohesive powders unless vibrated; high fan power |

### 2.4.6 Real-World Utility
- **Drying of pharmaceutical crystals, fertilizers (ammonium nitrate, urea), polymer granules, sugar crystals, salt, detergent powders, and food grains**.

---

## 2.5 SPRAY DRYER (Atomization Dryer)

### 2.5.1 Principle of Operation
A **liquid feed** (solution, slurry, emulsion, or paste) is **atomized into fine droplets** (typically **10–500 µm diameter**) through a **nozzle** (pressure nozzle, two-fluid nozzle, or rotary atomizer). The droplets meet a **co-current stream of hot gas** inside a large drying chamber. Each droplet dries in milliseconds to form a **dry spherical particle**, which is collected at the chamber bottom or in cyclones.

### 2.5.2 Construction
- A **large cylindrical drying chamber** (often with a conical bottom) made of stainless steel.
- An **atomizer** at the top:
  - **Pressure nozzle** (for low-viscosity feeds),
  - **Two-fluid (pneumatic) nozzle** (for pastes and slurries),
  - **Rotary disk atomizer** (for high-capacity, high-viscosity feeds).
- A **gas disperser** around the atomizer for uniform hot-gas distribution.
- **Hot-gas inlet** at the top (co-current), sometimes with **secondary air** for cooling.
- **Product recovery system**: cyclone + baghouse + wet scrubber.
- **Feed tank and high-pressure pump** (for pressure nozzle) or peristaltic pump (for two-fluid).

### 2.5.3 Operating Procedure
1. **Pre-heat** the drying chamber with the burner to design inlet temperature (often **150–250 °C**).
2. **Pump** liquid feed at controlled pressure/flow rate to the atomizer.
3. **Atomize** the feed into a fine spray at the top of the chamber.
4. **Inject** hot gas co-currently to contact the droplets.
5. **Dry** droplets within milliseconds; dry particles fall to the conical bottom.
6. **Discharge** the dry product through a rotary valve at the bottom.
7. **Recover** fine dust from the exhaust via cyclone and baghouse.

### 2.5.4 Key Design Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spray Dryer Critical Formula:**
> - **Mean Droplet Diameter (d\_p, µm):** depends on atomizer type and operating conditions (often computed via correlations such as the Nukiyama–Tanasawa or Mugele–Evans equations).
> - **Drying Time per Droplet:** on the order of **1–10 seconds** for typical droplets.
> - **Chamber Dimensions:** governed by spray trajectory and required residence time.

### 2.5.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | One-step liquid-to-powder conversion; excellent for heat-sensitive materials (instant coffee, milk powder, antibiotics); uniform spherical particles; continuous high-throughput operation |
| **Limitations** | High capital cost; high energy consumption (large gas volumes); not suitable for very abrasive or fibrous feeds; risk of wall deposition if atomization is poor |

### 2.5.6 Real-World Utility
- **Milk powder, instant coffee, detergent powders, dyes, ceramic precursors, pharmaceutical APIs, enzymes, flavor encapsulations, and food ingredients**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** required for KTU Module-4 calculations. **No vertical pipe symbol `|` is used** (replaced with `\vert` and `\mid`) to preserve markdown integrity.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Applicable Dryer(s)** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $X = \dfrac{m_{w}}{m_{s}}$ | $m_{w}$ = mass water (kg); $m_{s}$ = mass dry solid (kg) | All five dryers |
| 2 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $u = \dfrac{m_{w}}{m_{w}+m_{s}}$ | $u$ = wet-basis moisture | All five dryers |
| 3 | Conversion (X to u) | $u = \dfrac{X}{1+X}$ | — | All five dryers |
| 4 | Conversion (u to X) | $X = \dfrac{u}{1-u}$ | — | All five dryers |
| 5 | Total Heat Duty | $Q_{t} = m_{s}\,(X_{1}-X_{2})\,\lambda_{w} + m_{s}\,c_{ps}\,(T_{2}-T_{1}) + m_{w,\text{evap}}\,c_{pw}\,(T_{g,\text{out}}-T_{w})$ | $Q_{t}$ in W; $\lambda_{w}$ = latent heat (J/kg); $c_{p}$ = specific heat (J/kg·K) | All five dryers |
| 6 | Drying Rate (constant period) | $N_{c} = \dfrac{h_{g}\,(T_{g}-T_{w}^{*})}{\lambda_{w}}$ | $h_{g}$ = gas-film heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) | Tray, Tower, Rotary, Fluid-Bed |
| 7 | Drying Time (constant-rate) | $t_{c} = \dfrac{m_{s}\,(X_{1}-X^{*})}{A\,N_{c}}$ | $A$ = drying area (m²); $t_{c}$ in s | Tray Dryer |
| 8 | Drying Time (falling-rate) | $t_{f} = \dfrac{m_{s}}{A\,N_{c}}\,\int_{X^{*}}^{X_{2}}\,\dfrac{dX}{f(X)}$ | $f(X)$ = moisture-dependence function | Tray Dryer |
| 9 | Specific Air Consumption | $L = \dfrac{Q_{t}}{c_{p,\text{air}}\,(T_{g,\text{in}}-T_{g,\text{out}})}$ | $L$ = air mass flow rate (kg/s) | All five dryers |
| 10 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{evap}}}{Q_{t}} \times 100\,\%$ | Dimensionless | All five dryers |
| 11 | Rotary Residence Time (Friedman) | $\tau = \dfrac{0.23\,L}{S\,N^{0.9}\,D}\pm \dfrac{0.6\,B\,L\,G}{F\,(d_{p})^{0.5}}$ | $L,D$ in m; $N$ = rpm; $S$ = slope; $G,F$ = gas & feed rates (kg/s); $B,d_{p}$ = constants | Rotary Dryer |
| 12 | Rotary Drum Volume | $V_{d} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s}\,\tau}{\rho_{b}\,X_{\text{hold}}}$ | $V_{d}$ in m³; $\rho_{b}$ = bulk density (kg/m³) | Rotary Dryer |
| 13 | Min. Fluidization Velocity (Ergun) | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_{g}\,d_{p}}\,\sqrt{27.2^{2}+0.0408\,\text{Ar}}-27.2$ | $\mu$ = gas viscosity; $\text{Ar}$ = Archimedes no. | Fluid-Bed Dryer |
| 14 | Pressure Drop (Fluid Bed) | $\Delta P_{f} = (1-\epsilon_{mf})\,(\rho_{s}-\rho_{g})\,g\,H_{mf}$ | $H_{mf}$ = bed height at min. fluidization; $\epsilon$ = voidage | Fluid-Bed Dryer |
| 15 | Rotary Volume–Area Ratio | $\dfrac{V_{d}}{A_{d}} = \dfrac{D}{4}$ | $D$ = drum diameter (m) | Rotary Dryer |
| 16 | Spray Mean Droplet Size (Mugele–Evans) | $d_{vs} = \dfrac{585\,\sqrt{\sigma}}{u_{\text{rel}}\,\sqrt{\rho_{L}}}+597\,\left(\dfrac{\mu_{L}}{\sqrt{\sigma\,\rho_{L}}}\right)^{0.45}\,\left(\dfrac{1000\,Q_{L}}{Q_{a}}\right)^{1.5}$ | $\sigma$ = surface tension; $u_{\text{rel}}$ = relative velocity; subscript $L$ = liquid | Spray Dryer |
| 17 | Spray Chamber Volume | $V_{\text{ch}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{g}\,\tau_{\text{res}}}{\text{vol. fill fraction}}$ | $\tau_{\text{res}}$ = gas residence time (s) | Spray Dryer |
| 18 | Tower Residence Time (Gravity) | $\tau_{\text{tower}} = \dfrac{H}{u_{p}}$ | $H$ = tower height (m); $u_{p}$ = particle velocity (m/s) | Tower Dryer |
| 19 | Mass Evaporation Rate | $\dot{m}_{w} = m_{s}\,(X_{1}-X_{2})$ | kg/s | All five dryers |
| 20 | Psychrometric Air Capacity | $\dot{m}_{w,\text{air}} = L\,(Y_{2}-Y_{1})$ | $Y$ = humidity ratio (kg water / kg dry air) | All five dryers |
